signals.modelWording: The registrar's fear that calling would show inability to cope indicates a supervision culture failure.

signals.name: Unsafe consultant cover across three hospitals
signals.clueInStem: consultant was covering three hospitals
signals.whyItMatters: The consultant workload may have created unsafe barriers to direct supervision.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires roster review and escalation to service leadership.
signals.modelWording: Consultant cover across three hospitals is a system contributor to inadequate after-hours supervision.

signals.name: Email discouraged escalation
signals.clueInStem: Use your judgement overnight unless absolutely necessary
signals.whyItMatters: This wording may discourage junior trainees from calling in high-risk situations.
signals.relatedSystem: Supervision policy should define mandatory escalation triggers.
signals.modelWording: The email 'unless absolutely necessary' created an unsafe ambiguity for a Stage 1 registrar.

modelAnswer:
This incident should be reviewed as both a clinical safety event and a supervision governance failure. Talia's ligature self-harm in a police cell was high risk; discharge without psychosocial formulation, mother contact, police collateral or safety plan was unsafe. The label "emotionally unstable personality traits; no admission benefit" suggests diagnostic shortcutting rather than acute risk formulation.

The registrar's distress and belief that calling at 4 am would show they could not cope is a central safety signal. I would support the registrar while addressing accountability, ensure Talia's current safety, report the incident, and review after-hours supervision. The consultant covering three hospitals and the email advising registrars to use judgement unless "absolutely necessary" require roster and escalation-policy review, including mandatory consultant contact triggers for high-risk discharge.

title: Repeated bipolar admissions and carer burnout
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, community continuing care team
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist in a regional community continuing care team.

Maria is a 48-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder and seven admissions in the past four years, mostly following medication cessation during hypomanic periods. Her husband reports she becomes "brilliant for a few weeks" before stopping lithium because she believes she no longer needs treatment. During recent episodes she has accumulated large debts and driven long distances without sleep.

Maria was discharged from the inpatient unit three weeks ago after a manic relapse. At today's review she says, "Lithium takes away who I am." She admits reducing the dose herself because she wants "more energy to start a business." Her husband becomes visibly distressed and says, "I can't keep doing this every year." He reports hiding car keys and monitoring Maria's medications because he fears another relapse.

The case manager believes Maria requires intensive monitoring, while Maria says the team is "treating me like a child." The registrar asks whether ongoing assertive follow-up is justified when Maria is not currently psychotic and technically attends appointments voluntarily.

signals.name: Identity-based ambivalence about lithium
signals.clueInStem: Lithium takes away who I am.
signals.whyItMatters: Identity-related ambivalence is a major driver of relapse and requires nuanced formulation beyond simple non-compliance.
signals.modelWording: Explore ambivalence regarding mood stabilisation including perceived loss of identity, creativity and autonomy — this is not simple non-compliance, it is a formulation target.

signals.name: High historical risk justifies assertive follow-up
signals.clueInStem: driven long distances without sleep.
signals.whyItMatters: Repeated severe behavioural consequences — dangerous driving, financial harm, repeated admissions — justify proactive relapse prevention even when currently euthymic.
signals.modelWording: Recognise that seven admissions with dangerous driving and financial harm justify assertive follow-up regardless of current stability — this is not paternalism, it is proportionate risk management.

signals.name: Carer over-functioning and burnout
signals.clueInStem: hiding car keys and monitoring Maria's medications
signals.whyItMatters: A husband hiding car keys and monitoring medications daily is over-functioning unsustainably — this will break down and must be addressed as a clinical issue.
signals.modelWording: Assess caregiver burden and reduce reliance on the husband as the sole relapse monitoring system — this is unsustainable and relationship-damaging.

signals.name: Autonomy versus assertive follow-up tension
signals.clueInStem: treating me like a child.
signals.whyItMatters: Maria's experience of care as paternalistic is clinically significant and must be addressed through collaborative, autonomy-respecting care planning.
signals.modelWording: Balance assertive relapse prevention with collaborative care — explain rationale for close follow-up based on specific historical consequences, not general concern.

signals.name: Predictable relapse signature — opportunity for advance planning
signals.clueInStem: becomes brilliant for a few weeks
signals.whyItMatters: A consistent early warning sign — feeling brilliant, stopping lithium — creates a specific opportunity for an advance directive and agreed early intervention plan.
signals.modelWording: Identify Maria's relapse signature and develop an advance directive with agreed interventions when she reports feeling brilliant — this converts a predictable pattern into a prevention opportunity.

signals.name: Longitudinal care model needed
signals.clueInStem: seven admissions in the past four years
signals.whyItMatters: Seven admissions in four years suggests episodic crisis care rather than structured longitudinal management.
signals.modelWording: Review whether current service structure addresses Maria's recurrent pattern — a structured longitudinal care model with named case manager and rapid review pathway is indicated.

modelAnswer:
Review Maria using a longitudinal bipolar formulation. Seven admissions in four years is not a coincidence — it is a pattern driven by identity-based ambivalence about lithium that requires direct formulation, not just psychoeducation.

Assess current mood, insight, adherence, sleep, and psychosocial stressors. Explore the specific meaning of lithium for Maria — fear of emotional blunting, loss of creativity, loss of the "brilliant" self. This is the treatment target.

Recognise high historical risk despite current stability: dangerous driving, financial harm, repeated admissions justify assertive follow-up. Explain this rationale to Maria directly — not as paternalism but as proportionate response to specific consequences.

Develop an advance directive with Maria: what are her early warning signs, what has she agreed can happen when she reports feeling brilliant, who contacts the team.

Address carer burden: the husband hiding car keys daily is unsustainable. Reduce his sole responsibility through a structured team relapse response plan.

Review medication tolerability — explore whether side effect concerns are driving dose reduction, and whether adjuncts or alternatives address her identity concerns.

Supervise registrar: assertive follow-up for a patient with seven admissions and dangerous historical behaviour is clinically justified regardless of current voluntary attendance.

title: Dementia, driving and enduring power of attorney conflict
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, old age psychiatry consultation service
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist providing outreach to a residential aged care facility.

George is an 82-year-old retired accountant with vascular dementia who moved into residential care six months ago after several episodes of getting lost while driving. Staff report increasing irritability, accusations that belongings are being stolen, and repeated attempts to leave the facility to "go check the office." George insists he is capable of driving and says, "My son just wants control of my money."

George's son holds enduring power of attorney for finances and health matters. He asks you to formally document that George lacks capacity so the family can sell his car and former home. George's daughter disagrees and says, "Dad still knows exactly what he wants — you're all trying to strip away his independence." Facility staff report escalating conflict between siblings during visits.

Last week George attempted to enter another resident's vehicle in the car park. The GP has already notified the licensing authority regarding driving concerns. The registrar asks whether your role is primarily to assess cognition, mediate the family dispute, or support the facility's risk management concerns.

signals.name: Decision-specific capacity — not global incapacity
signals.clueInStem: Dad still knows exactly what he wants
signals.whyItMatters: Dementia does not automatically mean global incapacity — capacity must be assessed for each specific decision including driving, finances, and accommodation separately.
signals.modelWording: Conduct decision-specific capacity assessments for driving, finances, accommodation and health decisions separately — dementia diagnosis alone does not equal global incapacity.

signals.name: Public safety — driving risk is immediate
signals.clueInStem: attempted to enter another resident's vehicle
signals.whyItMatters: Attempting to enter another person's vehicle demonstrates active driving-related risk that is immediate and public — this cannot wait for family resolution.
signals.modelWording: George's attempt to enter another resident's vehicle is an immediate public safety issue requiring urgent action independent of the family dispute — I will support the GP's licensing notification and recommend means restriction.

signals.name: EPOA conflict of interest — assessment must remain neutral
signals.clueInStem: sell his car and former home.
signals.whyItMatters: A son holding EPOA and asking for a capacity documentation to enable asset sale creates a potential conflict of interest — the assessment must be clinically neutral and not serve family financial interests.
signals.modelWording: I will conduct a neutral capacity assessment based on clinical evidence only — I will not allow the son's request to sell assets to frame my findings, and I will document my independence explicitly.

signals.name: Family conflict worsening BPSD
signals.clueInStem: escalating conflict between siblings during visits
signals.whyItMatters: Escalating sibling conflict during visits is a direct trigger for George's behavioural disturbance — addressing the family system is part of treating the BPSD.
signals.modelWording: Address sibling conflict as a clinical issue — it is directly contributing to George's distress and behavioural symptoms, and requires structured family communication with consistent information from the team.

signals.name: BPSD — paranoia and exit-seeking require assessment and management
signals.clueInStem: accusations that belongings are being stolen
signals.whyItMatters: Paranoid ideation and exit-seeking in vascular dementia are BPSD symptoms requiring non-pharmacological management first — they are not simply character traits.
signals.modelWording: Assess BPSD including paranoid ideation, exit-seeking and irritability — implement non-pharmacological strategies first and reserve antipsychotics for severe distress after risk-benefit discussion.

signals.name: Integrated consultant role — not just cognitive testing
signals.clueInStem: whether your role is primarily to assess cognition
signals.whyItMatters: The consultant old age psychiatrist integrates cognition, risk, systems, family, and legal frameworks — not just administers cognitive tests.
signals.modelWording: My role is integrated assessment of cognition, risk, functional capacity, BPSD, family systems and legal/EPOA issues — not cognitive testing in isolation.

modelAnswer:
Immediate priority: George's attempt to enter another resident's vehicle is a public safety issue requiring action now — I will support the GP's licensing notification and recommend means restriction independent of the family dispute.

Capacity assessment: I will conduct decision-specific assessments for driving, finances, accommodation and health decisions separately. Dementia does not mean global incapacity. I will assess each domain with clinical evidence and document my findings independently of the son's financial request.

EPOA neutrality: The son's request to document incapacity to enable asset sales creates a potential conflict of interest. My assessment is clinical, not financial. I will document this explicitly.

BPSD: Paranoid ideation about stolen belongings and exit-seeking are BPSD symptoms driving distress and conflict — I will recommend non-pharmacological management first and involve RACF staff in consistent behavioural strategies.

Family conflict: Escalating sibling conflict is a clinical issue — it is worsening George's distress. I will hold a structured family meeting with consistent information and clear role boundaries.

My role is integrated: cognition, risk, BPSD, family, legal and systems — not cognitive testing in isolation.

title: Stage 1 Registrar Wants to Discharge a High-Risk ED Patient
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist supervising a junior registrar after hours
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on call. A Stage 1 registrar phones at 2 am asking for permission to discharge Liam, a 23-year-old man brought to ED by police after standing on a motorway overpass. Liam now denies suicidal ideation and says he was "just trying to clear my head."

The registrar says Liam has "borderline traits" and that admission will "reinforce behaviour." The ED is overcrowded and the registrar sounds irritated. Police report Liam had sent his ex-partner a message saying, "You will all be sorry tomorrow." His mother is in the waiting room and says Liam has not slept for two nights, recently lost his apprenticeship, and has been drinking heavily. The registrar has not spoken to the mother because "he refused consent." The registrar asks, "Can I just give him a crisis number and let him go?"

signals.name: High-risk behaviour despite denial of SI
signals.clueInStem: standing on a motorway overpass
signals.whyItMatters: The behaviour is high lethality and cannot be negated by later denial of suicidal ideation.
signals.relatedRisk: Risk of imminent self-harm remains elevated despite verbal denial.
signals.modelWording: Standing on a motorway overpass is a high-lethality suicidal behaviour signal; denial of SI does not make discharge safe.

signals.name: Threatening farewell-style message
signals.clueInStem: You will all be sorry tomorrow
signals.whyItMatters: This message suggests possible suicidal communication, revenge fantasy, or escalating risk to others.
signals.relatedRisk: It raises both self-harm and possible interpersonal violence concerns.
signals.modelWording: The message 'You will all be sorry tomorrow' should trigger concern for imminent suicide or retaliatory risk.

signals.name: Stigmatising diagnostic shortcut
signals.clueInStem: borderline traits
signals.whyItMatters: Using personality language to minimise acute risk is poor formulation and creates unsafe therapeutic nihilism.
signals.relatedSystem: This requires corrective supervision on formulation and stigma.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's phrase 'borderline traits' is not a risk formulation and risks minimising a high-lethality presentation.

signals.name: Collateral not obtained despite critical risk information
signals.clueInStem: The registrar has not spoken to the mother because "he refused consent"
signals.whyItMatters: Collateral can usually be received even when information cannot be disclosed; refusal does not prevent listening to family risk information.
signals.relatedLegal: Confidentiality must be balanced with safety and duty of care.
signals.modelWording: The registrar should receive collateral from the mother even if Liam refuses consent to disclosure.

signals.name: Acute destabilising factors
signals.clueInStem: not slept for two nights, recently lost his apprenticeship, and has been drinking heavily
signals.whyItMatters: Insomnia, job loss and alcohol use acutely increase impulsivity and suicide risk.
signals.relatedRisk: Alcohol and sleep deprivation increase disinhibition.
signals.modelWording: Two nights without sleep, job loss and heavy alcohol use are acute dynamic risk factors.

signals.name: Consultant must actively supervise, not rubber-stamp discharge
signals.clueInStem: asks for permission to discharge
signals.whyItMatters: A Stage 1 registrar seeking discharge approval for a high-risk presentation requires direct consultant supervision and clear documented decision-making.
signals.relatedSystem: Consultant accountability cannot be delegated to a junior registrar under ED pressure.
signals.modelWording: I would not authorise discharge by phone; I would require further assessment, collateral and likely attend or directly supervise the plan.

modelAnswer:
This is not a safe telephone discharge. Standing on a motorway overpass is high-lethality behaviour, and the message "You will all be sorry tomorrow" raises imminent self-harm and possible interpersonal risk. Liam's denial of suicidal ideation is not sufficient.

I would instruct the registrar to pause discharge, obtain collateral from the mother, review police information, reassess intoxication/sleep deprivation and consider involuntary assessment if Liam lacks capacity or remains at imminent risk. I would correct the registrar's formulation: "borderline traits" is not a reason to minimise acute risk. As consultant, I must actively supervise the decision, not rubber-stamp discharge under ED pressure.

title: Registrar Misses Capacity Issue in CL Psychiatry
stem:
You are the consultation-liaison consultant. Your registrar asks you to countersign a note for Mr Chen, a 69-year-old Mandarin-speaking man with bowel obstruction who is refusing surgery. The registrar has written: "Patient understands risks. Capacity intact. Refuses surgery due to cultural beliefs."

When you review the chart, you see nursing notes describing fluctuating alertness overnight, pulling out his IV line, and saying his dead brother was standing by the bed. The registrar assessed him using his daughter as interpreter because "formal interpreter would take too long." The surgical team is angry and says psychiatry is delaying life-saving treatment. The daughter says her father keeps asking why he is in hospital, but she does not want to contradict the registrar.

signals.name: Possible delirium invalidating simple capacity conclusion
signals.clueInStem: fluctuating alertness overnight, pulling out his IV line, and saying his dead brother was standing by the bed
signals.whyItMatters: Fluctuation, perceptual disturbance and line-pulling suggest delirium, requiring reassessment of capacity.
signals.relatedRisk: Missed delirium may lead to unsafe refusal of life-saving treatment.
signals.modelWording: The nursing notes suggest delirium; capacity cannot be accepted as intact without assessing this.

signals.name: Capacity assessment through family interpreter is unsafe
signals.clueInStem: using his daughter as interpreter because "formal interpreter would take too long"
signals.whyItMatters: Capacity assessment requires accurate communication; family interpretation is unreliable and creates conflict of interest.
signals.relatedLegal: Procedurally unsafe assessment may not support refusal or treatment decisions.
signals.relatedCultural: Language access is part of culturally safe care.
signals.modelWording: A professional Mandarin interpreter is required before concluding capacity about surgery.

signals.name: Registrar used vague cultural explanation
signals.clueInStem: Refuses surgery due to cultural beliefs
signals.whyItMatters: This phrase is unsupported and risks stereotyping rather than assessing actual values, understanding and reasoning.
signals.relatedCultural: Cultural formulation must be specific and elicited, not assumed.
signals.modelWording: The phrase 'cultural beliefs' is too vague; the registrar must document the actual belief and how it affects decision-making.

signals.name: Life-saving surgery creates time-critical legal issue
signals.clueInStem: refusing surgery
signals.whyItMatters: Refusal of potentially life-saving surgery requires decision-specific, time-specific capacity assessment and legal pathway if capacity lacking.
signals.relatedLegal: May require emergency treatment or substitute decision-making depending urgency and jurisdiction.
signals.modelWording: The refusal of surgery requires urgent decision-specific capacity assessment and, if lacking, a lawful treatment pathway.

signals.name: Daughter discomfort signals unreliable assessment context
signals.clueInStem: she does not want to contradict the registrar
signals.whyItMatters: The daughter may have relevant information but feels inhibited, which affects collateral accuracy and cultural safety.
signals.modelWording: The daughter's reluctance to contradict the registrar suggests the assessment context is unsafe and needs careful re-engagement.

signals.name: Supervision requires correction before countersignature
signals.clueInStem: asks you to countersign a note
signals.whyItMatters: Countersigning an unsafe assessment would transfer consultant accountability to a flawed conclusion.
signals.relatedSystem: Consultant must supervise, correct and document revised reasoning.
signals.modelWording: I should not countersign this note until capacity, delirium and interpreter issues are properly reassessed.

modelAnswer:
I would not countersign the registrar's note. The nursing notes describe fluctuating alertness, line-pulling and seeing a dead brother, which strongly suggests delirium. The capacity assessment was also procedurally unsafe because it was conducted through the daughter rather than a professional Mandarin interpreter.

I would supervise the registrar to redo the assessment: define the exact decision, use an interpreter, assess delirium/cognition, document understanding, retention, weighing and communication, and identify the lawful pathway if capacity is lacking. I would also communicate with surgery about urgency and explain that psychiatry is not delaying treatment; it is ensuring the decision is legally and clinically valid.

title: Registrar Writes Poor Mental Health Act Documentation
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist supervising involuntary treatment documentation
stem:
You are the supervising consultant on an acute inpatient unit. A registrar asks you to review Mental Health Act paperwork for Sofia, a 31-year-old woman detained overnight after she tried to leave the ward while saying staff were poisoning her baby through breast milk.

The registrar's form states: "Psychotic. Needs admission. Risk to baby. Detain." There is no description of the actual behaviour, no record of Sofia's views, no capacity reasoning, no explanation of less restrictive alternatives, and no reference to the baby being temporarily cared for by Sofia's partner on the maternity ward. Sofia is Aboriginal and repeatedly asked for her aunt to be called; this is not mentioned in the form.

The nurse in charge says the paperwork is "probably fine" because everyone knows Sofia is unwell. The registrar says, "I wrote the key points — the tribunal won't read all the details anyway."

signals.name: Bare diagnostic label is inadequate legal documentation
signals.clueInStem: Psychotic. Needs admission. Risk to baby. Detain.
signals.whyItMatters: MHA documentation must justify criteria with facts, behaviour, risk, capacity and least restrictive reasoning.
signals.relatedLegal: Poor documentation may make detention unlawful or indefensible.
signals.modelWording: The form is inadequate because it uses labels without evidencing the legal criteria.

signals.name: Missing behaviour and risk evidence
signals.clueInStem: saying staff were poisoning her baby through breast milk
signals.whyItMatters: This belief creates specific infant safety and treatment-refusal risks that must be documented, not summarised generically.
signals.relatedRisk: Risk relates to maternal psychosis, infant care and refusal of support.
signals.modelWording: The belief that staff were poisoning the baby through breast milk should be documented as the risk-relevant behaviour and belief.

signals.name: Capacity reasoning absent
signals.clueInStem: no capacity reasoning
signals.whyItMatters: Compulsory treatment decisions require clear explanation of decision-specific capacity where relevant.
signals.relatedLegal: Capacity reasoning supports least restrictive and rights-based decision-making.
signals.modelWording: The registrar must document how psychosis affected Sofia's ability to understand, weigh or use information about admission and care.

signals.name: Least restrictive alternatives not documented
signals.clueInStem: no explanation of less restrictive alternatives
signals.whyItMatters: The legal and clinical threshold requires showing why voluntary care, family support or maternity liaison were insufficient.
signals.relatedLegal: Least restrictive care is a core compulsory treatment principle.
signals.modelWording: The form must state what less restrictive options were considered and why they were inadequate.

signals.name: Cultural support request omitted
signals.clueInStem: Sofia is Aboriginal and repeatedly asked for her aunt to be called
signals.whyItMatters: Her request for kin support is clinically relevant to engagement, distress and least restrictive care.
signals.relatedCultural: Cultural safety requires recording and acting on nominated support requests.
signals.modelWording: Sofia's request for her aunt should be documented and acted upon as part of culturally safe, least restrictive care.

signals.name: Registrar dismisses tribunal/legal scrutiny
signals.clueInStem: the tribunal won't read all the details anyway
signals.whyItMatters: This reflects unsafe professional attitude toward rights-based documentation and needs corrective supervision.
signals.relatedSystem: Consultant must reinforce documentation standards and legal accountability.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's comment about the tribunal is a supervision issue requiring correction and education.

modelAnswer:
The paperwork is not adequate. "Psychotic. Needs admission. Risk to baby. Detain" does not evidence the legal criteria. The registrar must document Sofia's actual belief about poisoning through breast milk, the behaviour leading to detention, risk to the baby, Sofia's views, capacity reasoning, and why less restrictive options were insufficient.

I would supervise the registrar to rewrite the form before relying on it. Sofia's Aboriginal identity and repeated request for her aunt are clinically relevant and should be documented and acted upon. The registrar's statement that the tribunal will not read details requires immediate corrective feedback: MHA documentation is a rights-based clinical document, not an administrative formality.

title: Registrar Distressed After Patient Suicide
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist for a community mental health team. A registrar you supervise saw Ethan, a 34-year-old man, on Friday and agreed to discharge him from the service after he said he was "doing much better." Ethan died by suicide on Sunday.

The registrar is crying in your office and says, "It is my fault. I should never be a psychiatrist." You review the note: there was no collateral from Ethan's partner, no updated safety plan, and no documentation of means restriction despite a previous overdose with prescribed medication. The registrar had discussed the case briefly with you in the corridor on Friday, saying "he seems fine now," but you did not review the file.

The service manager wants a written statement by 5 pm and asks whether the registrar should be removed from clinical duties immediately.

signals.name: Immediate support and containment for distressed registrar
signals.clueInStem: crying in your office and says, "It is my fault. I should never be a psychiatrist"
signals.whyItMatters: The registrar is acutely distressed and requires support, containment and assessment of fitness for work.
signals.relatedSystem: Staff wellbeing is part of safe governance after adverse events.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's distress requires immediate support, not blame or abandonment.

signals.name: Clinical documentation gaps in discharge decision
signals.clueInStem: no collateral from Ethan's partner, no updated safety plan, and no documentation of means restriction
signals.whyItMatters: These omissions are central to the clinical review and cannot be ignored because the registrar is distressed.
signals.relatedRisk: Previous overdose with medication makes means restriction particularly important.
signals.modelWording: The discharge note lacked partner collateral, updated safety plan and medication means-restriction documentation.

signals.name: Consultant supervision responsibility
signals.clueInStem: discussed the case briefly with you in the corridor
signals.whyItMatters: Corridor supervision for discharge from service may be inadequate; consultant accountability and supervision process must be reviewed.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires examination of supervision structure, not only trainee performance.
signals.modelWording: The corridor discussion raises consultant supervision and governance issues; I must include my own role in the review.

signals.name: Avoid premature blame before review
signals.clueInStem: It is my fault
signals.whyItMatters: The supervisor must support accountability while avoiding premature conclusions before fact-finding.
signals.modelWording: I would acknowledge the registrar's distress but avoid agreeing with self-blame before a structured review.

signals.name: Need for formal incident process
signals.clueInStem: service manager wants a written statement by 5 pm
signals.whyItMatters: Statements should be factual, timely and aligned with incident review processes, not rushed defensively.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires incident reporting, open disclosure and coronial awareness where applicable.
signals.modelWording: The written statement should be factual and part of the formal incident process, not a defensive rush.

signals.name: Fitness for work should be assessed proportionately
signals.clueInStem: asks whether the registrar should be removed from clinical duties immediately
signals.whyItMatters: The decision should balance patient safety, registrar wellbeing, supervision and stigma; removal is not automatic.
signals.modelWording: I would assess the registrar's immediate fitness for work and arrange supported duties or leave if needed, rather than automatic punitive removal.

modelAnswer:
I would first support and contain the registrar: they are acutely distressed and may not be fit for immediate clinical work. I would not reinforce self-blame, but I would not minimise the seriousness of the event.

The clinical review must identify documentation and process issues: no partner collateral, no updated safety plan, no means-restriction documentation despite previous overdose, and only corridor supervision. I must include my own supervision role, not place all responsibility on the registrar. I would ensure formal incident reporting, factual documentation, staff support, open disclosure processes and a proportionate decision about the registrar's duties based on fitness and safety.

title: Registrar Uses Culturally Unsafe Language in Family Meeting
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist observing and debriefing a registrar
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist observing a family meeting led by your registrar. The patient, Wiremu, is a 40-year-old Māori man admitted with mania. His whānau attend and ask whether kaumātua support can be included in care planning.

The registrar says, "We need to focus on evidence-based treatment, not cultural extras," and then tells the family that Wiremu's non-adherence is "probably because your family system is too chaotic." Wiremu becomes silent and leaves the room. His sister later tells you, "That doctor talked down to us and made it sound like being Māori is the problem."

The registrar says afterwards, "I was just being direct. They needed psychoeducation." The nurse facilitator is angry but says she did not want to challenge the registrar in front of the family.

signals.name: Culturally unsafe dismissal of kaumātua support
signals.clueInStem: not cultural extras
signals.whyItMatters: Dismissing cultural support as an extra undermines culturally safe care and engagement.
signals.relatedCultural: For Māori patients, whānau and kaumātua involvement may be central to care planning.
signals.modelWording: The phrase 'cultural extras' is culturally unsafe and requires immediate supervisory correction.

signals.name: Blaming family system in a stigmatising way
signals.clueInStem: your family system is too chaotic
signals.whyItMatters: This alienates family supports and frames them as the problem rather than partners in care.
signals.relatedCultural: It risks stereotyping kinship structures and worsening distrust.
signals.modelWording: Calling the family system 'too chaotic' is not psychoeducation; it is blaming and clinically harmful.

signals.name: Patient disengagement during meeting
signals.clueInStem: Wiremu becomes silent and leaves the room
signals.whyItMatters: The meeting caused disengagement, which may worsen adherence, trust and risk management.
signals.modelWording: Wiremu leaving the room is evidence the communication harmed engagement and needs repair.

signals.name: Registrar lacks insight into harmful communication
signals.clueInStem: I was just being direct. They needed psychoeducation
signals.whyItMatters: The registrar reframes harmful comments as psychoeducation, requiring corrective feedback and supervision.
signals.relatedSystem: Supervisor must address communication, cultural safety and reflective capacity.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's defence shows poor insight and requires structured feedback, not reassurance.

signals.name: Need to repair therapeutic rupture with whānau
signals.clueInStem: talked down to us
signals.whyItMatters: The consultant must repair harm with patient and whānau to preserve engagement and safety.
signals.relatedCultural: Repair should include apology, listening and culturally appropriate support.
signals.modelWording: I would meet Wiremu and whānau to acknowledge harm, apologise and invite culturally safe care planning.

signals.name: Team member did not feel safe to challenge
signals.clueInStem: did not want to challenge the registrar in front of the family
signals.whyItMatters: The nurse's silence indicates hierarchy and psychological safety issues within the MDT.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires team culture allowing real-time safety challenge.
signals.modelWording: The nurse's inability to challenge the registrar shows an MDT psychological safety issue.

modelAnswer:
The registrar's comments were culturally unsafe and clinically harmful. Calling kaumātua support "cultural extras" and describing the whānau as "too chaotic" dismissed cultural identity and family partnership. Wiremu leaving the room shows immediate disengagement.

I would repair the rupture with Wiremu and whānau: acknowledge harm, apologise, invite kaumātua/whānau involvement and reframe care collaboratively. With the registrar, I would provide clear corrective feedback: this was not direct psychoeducation; it was culturally unsafe communication. I would also address the MDT issue that the nurse did not feel able to challenge the registrar in the meeting.

title: Registrar Overconfident About Seclusion Decision
stem:
You are the consultant on call for a high-dependency mental health unit. The night registrar phones after authorising seclusion for Aisha, a 26-year-old woman with complex trauma and psychosis. Aisha had been pacing and shouting that male security guards were "the men from before." She threw a cup of water at a nurse but did not assault anyone.

The registrar says, "She is psychotic and dangerous; seclusion is clearly indicated." The chart shows no documented attempt at sensory modulation, female staff engagement, oral PRN, or contacting her nominated support person. The registrar says the unit was short-staffed and "security were already there, so it was efficient." Aisha is now banging the seclusion room door and screaming, "Don't lock me in again."

signals.name: Seclusion may not be least restrictive
signals.clueInStem: no documented attempt at sensory modulation, female staff engagement, oral PRN, or contacting her nominated support person
signals.whyItMatters: Restrictive practice requires evidence that less restrictive options were tried or considered.
signals.relatedLegal: Seclusion must be necessary, proportionate and least restrictive.
signals.modelWording: The absence of documented alternatives means seclusion has not been justified as least restrictive.

signals.name: Trauma re-enactment triggered by male security
signals.clueInStem: male security guards were "the men from before"
signals.whyItMatters: The presentation signals re-traumatisation and may worsen with male security and confinement.
signals.relatedRisk: Trauma activation may escalate agitation and injury risk.
signals.modelWording: Aisha's reference to male guards as 'the men from before' should trigger trauma-informed alternatives.

signals.name: Current seclusion distress is a safety signal
signals.clueInStem: banging the seclusion room door and screaming, "Don't lock me in again"
signals.whyItMatters: Active distress in seclusion requires urgent review, observation and consideration of termination or modification.
signals.relatedRisk: Risk of injury, worsening trauma and escalation.
signals.modelWording: Her current door-banging and screaming require immediate review of seclusion safety and necessity.

signals.name: Overconfident registrar formulation
signals.clueInStem: seclusion is clearly indicated
signals.whyItMatters: The registrar shows premature certainty without weighing proportionality, alternatives or trauma context.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires supervision on restrictive practice reasoning.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's certainty is unsafe because the least-restrictive and trauma-informed analysis is missing.

signals.name: Efficiency is not a valid seclusion rationale
signals.clueInStem: security were already there, so it was efficient
signals.whyItMatters: Operational convenience cannot justify coercive restrictive practice.
signals.relatedLegal: Seclusion must be clinically necessary, not merely efficient.
signals.modelWording: The fact security were already present is not a lawful or ethical justification for seclusion.

signals.name: Short staffing is a system contributor
signals.clueInStem: the unit was short-staffed
signals.whyItMatters: Short staffing may contribute to restrictive practice use and should be escalated and reviewed.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires governance review of staffing, acuity and restrictive practice rates.
signals.modelWording: Short staffing is a systems contributor, not a justification for seclusion.

modelAnswer:
I would urgently review Aisha in seclusion and determine whether seclusion remains necessary, proportionate and least restrictive. The stem gives several missed alternatives: sensory modulation, female staff engagement, oral PRN and contacting her nominated support person. Her statements about male security being "the men from before" and screaming "Don't lock me in again" make trauma-informed care central.

I would supervise the registrar to move beyond "psychotic and dangerous." The issue is not whether she is unwell, but whether coercion is justified and whether it is worsening risk. Short staffing and convenience are systems factors, not valid clinical justifications for seclusion.

title: Registrar Boundary Problem in Psychotherapy Case
stem:
You are supervising a registrar providing weekly psychotherapy to Nadia, a 30-year-old woman with complex trauma. The registrar tells you casually that Nadia has started sending long text messages between sessions and that he has replied late at night because "she has no one else." He also agreed to extend the last three sessions by 25 minutes because Nadia became distressed at the end.

In supervision, the registrar says he feels "protective" and worries that setting limits will "repeat abandonment." He has not documented the text messages in the clinical record. Nadia recently told reception she only wants appointments when this registrar is available and refused crisis team contact when he was on leave.

The registrar asks whether he should give Nadia his personal email so she can "feel secure."

signals.name: Boundary crossing through late-night texting
signals.clueInStem: replied late at night because "she has no one else"
signals.whyItMatters: Late-night personal responsiveness blurs therapeutic boundaries and may increase dependency.
signals.relatedRisk: Creates risk to patient, registrar and therapeutic frame.
signals.modelWording: Late-night replies are a boundary crossing and should be addressed in supervision and treatment planning.

signals.name: Repeated session extensions destabilise therapy frame
signals.clueInStem: extend the last three sessions by 25 minutes
signals.whyItMatters: Repeated unplanned extensions reinforce boundary instability and may worsen attachment anxiety.
signals.modelWording: Repeated 25-minute extensions indicate loss of therapeutic frame and need structured ending work.

signals.name: Registrar countertransference
signals.clueInStem: feels "protective"
signals.whyItMatters: Protective feelings may reflect countertransference influencing boundary decisions.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's protective feelings should be explored as countertransference affecting clinical judgement.

signals.name: Failure to document clinically relevant contact
signals.clueInStem: has not documented the text messages in the clinical record
signals.whyItMatters: Out-of-session therapeutic contact is clinically relevant and must be documented.
signals.relatedSystem: Creates medico-legal and continuity risk.
signals.modelWording: The text messages should be documented as clinically relevant out-of-session contact.

signals.name: Dependency and service-splitting risk
signals.clueInStem: only wants appointments when this registrar is available and refused crisis team contact
signals.whyItMatters: Nadia is becoming dependent on one clinician and rejecting broader crisis supports.
signals.relatedRisk: Risk escalates during registrar absence or leave.
signals.modelWording: Her refusal of crisis team contact and exclusive reliance on the registrar indicates dependency and safety risk.

signals.name: Personal email would worsen boundary breach
signals.clueInStem: give Nadia his personal email
signals.whyItMatters: Personal email would further blur professional boundaries and bypass service safety systems.
signals.modelWording: He should not provide personal email; communication must occur through agreed clinical channels.

modelAnswer:
This is a psychotherapy supervision and boundary-risk case. The registrar has blurred the therapeutic frame through late-night texting, repeated session extensions and proposed personal email contact. His feeling "protective" suggests countertransference that is shaping decisions.

I would advise him not to provide personal email, to stop ad hoc late-night contact, and to re-establish a clear communication and crisis plan through service channels. The text contact must be documented. Nadia's refusal of crisis team contact and insistence on this registrar only indicates dependency and risk during absences. Supervision should focus on boundaries, endings, countertransference, documentation and patient safety.

title: Bullying Allegation Against Senior Registrar
stem:
You are the term supervisor for an inpatient unit. A Stage 1 registrar, Priyanka, tells you privately that the senior registrar repeatedly calls her "too slow" in front of nurses and told her, "Maybe psychiatry is not for you." Priyanka says she now avoids asking questions and copied forward an old risk assessment because she was afraid of being criticised for staying late.

A nurse separately tells you Priyanka looked tearful after ward round and that the copied risk assessment missed a new threat by a patient to stab his neighbour. The senior registrar says, "I am just maintaining standards; she needs to toughen up." The unit is short of consultants and the senior registrar is relied on heavily to run the ward.

signals.name: Bullying undermines trainee safety and patient safety
signals.clueInStem: repeatedly calls her "too slow" in front of nurses
signals.whyItMatters: Public humiliation creates psychological unsafety and impairs learning, escalation and clinical care.
signals.relatedSystem: Training culture is a governance and patient safety issue.
signals.modelWording: The repeated public criticism is a bullying concern and a patient-safety issue, not just interpersonal style.

signals.name: Trainee avoids asking questions
signals.clueInStem: now avoids asking questions
signals.whyItMatters: Avoiding questions prevents escalation and increases risk for junior clinicians.
signals.relatedSystem: Supervision environment is unsafe.
signals.modelWording: Priyanka avoiding questions is a direct supervision safety signal.

signals.name: Copied-forward risk assessment missed new threat
signals.clueInStem: missed a new threat by a patient to stab his neighbour
signals.whyItMatters: The bullying context has already translated into a serious clinical documentation and risk failure.
signals.relatedRisk: Threat to stab neighbour requires immediate risk review and safety action.
signals.modelWording: The missed threat to stab a neighbour requires immediate patient risk review and correction of the copied assessment.

signals.name: Senior registrar rationalises harmful behaviour
signals.clueInStem: she needs to toughen up
signals.whyItMatters: This shows poor insight and normalisation of unsafe training culture.
signals.modelWording: The 'toughen up' response suggests lack of insight into bullying and requires formal feedback.

signals.name: Service dependence on senior registrar creates conflict
signals.clueInStem: relied on heavily to run the ward
signals.whyItMatters: Operational dependence may inhibit appropriate response to bullying and unsafe supervision.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires consultant oversight despite staffing pressures.
signals.modelWording: Reliance on the senior registrar cannot prevent addressing unsafe behaviour.

signals.name: Need to protect complainant from retaliation
signals.clueInStem: tells you privately
signals.whyItMatters: Private disclosure requires confidentiality, support and protection from retaliation while facts are assessed.
signals.modelWording: Priyanka needs support, confidentiality and a safe pathway to report concerns without retaliation.

modelAnswer:
This is not merely a personality clash. The alleged bullying has already affected patient safety: Priyanka avoids questions and copied forward a risk assessment that missed a new threat to stab a neighbour. I would first ensure the patient's risk is urgently reviewed and the record corrected.

I would support Priyanka, maintain confidentiality as far as possible, assess immediate safety and follow the training/workplace process. The senior registrar requires clear feedback and investigation; "toughen up" is not acceptable. The fact that the service relies on the senior registrar does not justify unsafe supervision culture. Consultant oversight and a safe reporting pathway are required.

title: Remote Supervision in Rural ED
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on call for a rural mental health service. A junior registrar is physically located 300 km away and is supervising a mental health nurse by video link. The nurse is assessing Grace, a 67-year-old woman brought in by her neighbour after she was found wandering on a highway at night.

The registrar tells you Grace is "probably dementia with behavioural issues" and plans discharge to the neighbour because there are no local beds and the ambulance transfer will take six hours. The nurse reports Grace is febrile, has new urinary incontinence, and keeps falling asleep during the interview. Grace says the neighbour "steals my pension," but the neighbour is pushing to take her home. There is no onsite doctor after midnight.

The registrar asks, "Given the distance, can I document that I supervised remotely and let the nurse manage it?"

signals.name: Possible delirium, not simple dementia
signals.clueInStem: febrile, has new urinary incontinence, and keeps falling asleep during the interview
signals.whyItMatters: Fever, incontinence and reduced alertness suggest delirium or acute medical illness requiring urgent medical assessment.
signals.relatedRisk: Discharge could miss sepsis or other acute illness.
signals.modelWording: The fever, new incontinence and drowsiness should trigger delirium/medical deterioration, not dementia discharge.

signals.name: Unsafe discharge to potentially exploitative neighbour
signals.clueInStem: neighbour "steals my pension," but the neighbour is pushing to take her home
signals.whyItMatters: Grace may be vulnerable to financial abuse or coercion; the proposed carer may be unsafe.
signals.relatedRisk: Risk of elder abuse, neglect and exploitation.
signals.modelWording: The allegation that the neighbour steals her pension makes discharge to that neighbour unsafe without safeguarding assessment.

signals.name: Rural bed pressure cannot override safety
signals.clueInStem: there are no local beds and the ambulance transfer will take six hours
signals.whyItMatters: Distance and lack of beds are system constraints but do not make unsafe discharge acceptable.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires escalation to retrieval, medical, aged care or regional duty systems.
signals.modelWording: No local beds and a six-hour transfer are system pressures requiring escalation, not discharge to an unsafe carer.

signals.name: Remote supervision does not remove consultant responsibility
signals.clueInStem: document that I supervised remotely and let the nurse manage it
signals.whyItMatters: Remote supervision must be active and clinically meaningful, especially for high-risk vulnerable patients.
signals.relatedSystem: Consultant must set clear plan, escalation and documentation.
signals.modelWording: Remote supervision requires active consultant decision-making, not simply documenting that the nurse will manage.

signals.name: No onsite doctor after midnight
signals.clueInStem: There is no onsite doctor after midnight
signals.whyItMatters: Medical assessment capacity is limited, increasing risk if delirium or sepsis is suspected.
signals.relatedSystem: Requires urgent escalation before medical cover disappears or retrieval advice.
signals.modelWording: No onsite doctor after midnight increases urgency for medical escalation now.

signals.name: Premature diagnostic closure by registrar
signals.clueInStem: probably dementia with behavioural issues
signals.whyItMatters: The registrar is closing diagnostically without addressing acute delirium and safeguarding evidence.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's 'dementia with behavioural issues' formulation is premature and unsafe.

modelAnswer:
I would not support discharge. Grace has fever, new urinary incontinence and drowsiness, which suggest delirium or acute medical illness. She is also a vulnerable older person found wandering on a highway, and the proposed neighbour-carer is implicated by Grace's statement that he "steals my pension."

Remote supervision must be active. I would direct the registrar to escalate medical assessment urgently, consider transfer/retrieval advice, safeguard Grace from discharge to the neighbour, and document a clear consultant-led plan. No local beds and a six-hour transfer are rural system pressures, not reasons for unsafe discharge.

title: Registrar Provides Poor Feedback to Intern
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist supervising clinical teaching culture
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on a consultation-liaison term. An intern approaches you after ward round and says your registrar told her, in front of the medical team, "Your presentation was useless; you clearly don't understand psychiatry." The intern is upset and says she is now reluctant to refer patients to psychiatry because "I don't want to be humiliated again."

You observed that the intern's presentation was disorganised and did omit key points about capacity and delirium. However, the registrar did not give specific feedback or invite questions. Later, the medical consultant says psychiatry often seems "elitist and unhelpful." The registrar says, "I was under pressure; they need to learn standards."

signals.name: Humiliating feedback harms learning culture
signals.clueInStem: Your presentation was useless; you clearly don't understand psychiatry
signals.whyItMatters: Public humiliation is not effective feedback and damages psychological safety.
signals.relatedSystem: Poor teaching culture affects referrals and patient care.
signals.modelWording: The registrar's comment was humiliating and not educational; it requires corrective supervision.

signals.name: Patient safety impact through reduced referrals
signals.clueInStem: reluctant to refer patients to psychiatry
signals.whyItMatters: Poor interprofessional behaviour may reduce appropriate psychiatry referrals and harm patient care.
signals.relatedSystem: CL psychiatry depends on trust and accessibility.
signals.modelWording: The intern becoming reluctant to refer is a patient-safety and systems issue, not just hurt feelings.

signals.name: Need to separate content standard from feedback method
signals.clueInStem: presentation was disorganised and did omit key points about capacity and delirium
signals.whyItMatters: The intern did need teaching, but feedback must be specific, respectful and behaviour-based.
signals.modelWording: The correct supervision point is to teach capacity and delirium gaps using specific behaviour-based feedback.

signals.name: Registrar fails to give actionable feedback
signals.clueInStem: did not give specific feedback or invite questions
signals.whyItMatters: Feedback without specificity does not improve performance and may impair learning.
signals.modelWording: The registrar should have named the missing capacity and delirium elements and invited clarification.

signals.name: Psychiatry's reputation with medical team damaged
signals.clueInStem: psychiatry often seems "elitist and unhelpful"
signals.whyItMatters: CL psychiatry requires collaborative credibility with medical teams.
signals.relatedSystem: Poor cross-specialty relationships affect access to consultation.
signals.modelWording: The medical consultant's comment shows a broader MDT relationship problem requiring repair.

signals.name: Pressure does not justify poor supervision behaviour
signals.clueInStem: I was under pressure
signals.whyItMatters: Workload pressure is relevant but does not justify humiliating communication.
signals.modelWording: Pressure may explain but does not excuse humiliating feedback.

modelAnswer:
The registrar's feedback was unacceptable. "Your presentation was useless" is humiliating and not educational. It has already created a patient-safety issue because the intern is now reluctant to refer to psychiatry.

I would support the intern, acknowledge the harm, and repair the relationship with the medical team. With the registrar, I would separate standards from method: the intern did miss capacity and delirium points, but feedback must be specific, respectful and behaviour-based. The registrar should learn to say, for example, "Next time, structure the referral around diagnosis, delirium screen, capacity question and immediate risk." Pressure explains context but does not justify humiliation.

title: Inadequate Discharge Summary — First Episode Psychosis
setting: Acute inpatient psychiatric unit, metropolitan hospital
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on an acute inpatient unit. You receive a call from Dr Sarah Adeyemi, a GP, who is frustrated. Her patient Reza, a 34-year-old Iranian man, was discharged from your unit five days ago after a four-week admission for first episode schizophrenia. He has just presented to her clinic looking unwell.

Dr Adeyemi tells you: "The discharge summary doesn't tell me his diagnosis — it says 'psychotic disorder under investigation.' No medications, no doses, no follow-up plan, no case manager. I have no idea who to call if he deteriorates tonight."

You check the summary. Three paragraphs, written by the after-hours registrar at 11pm on the day of discharge. No consultant co-signature. Reza's wife called this morning — he has stopped his tablets and has been "acting strangely" since yesterday.

Outline the issues you would identify and the actions you would take. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: Reza is deteriorating — immediate clinical review required
signals.clueInStem: Reza has just presented to her clinic looking unwell
signals.whyItMatters: A patient five days post-discharge from first episode psychosis who is already deteriorating and non-adherent requires urgent review today — not a phone discussion. The post-discharge period carries the highest relapse risk.
signals.modelWording: Reza must be reviewed urgently today. I will arrange immediate community mental health contact or direct review — this is a potential relapse in the highest-risk post-discharge period for first episode psychosis.

signals.name: Discharge summary fails minimum clinical standards
signals.clueInStem: doesn't tell me his diagnosis — it says psychotic disorder under investigation. No medications, no doses, no follow-up plan, no case manager
signals.whyItMatters: A discharge summary for first episode psychosis must contain working diagnosis, medications with doses, relapse indicators, safety plan, follow-up appointments, case manager contact, and emergency escalation. This summary fails every minimum standard and has directly contributed to Reza's deterioration.
signals.modelWording: The discharge summary fails minimum clinical standards — no working diagnosis, no medications, no follow-up plan, no case manager. I take responsibility. I will send a comprehensive addendum today.

signals.name: No consultant co-signature — supervision failure
signals.clueInStem: written by the after-hours registrar at 11pm on the day of discharge. No consultant co-signature
signals.whyItMatters: Discharge summaries for complex psychiatric cases must be reviewed and co-signed by a consultant. An after-hours registrar writing an unsupervised discharge letter for first episode schizophrenia is a supervision failure.
signals.modelWording: A discharge summary for first episode psychosis written by an after-hours registrar without consultant co-signature is a supervision failure. Complex psychiatric discharges require consultant oversight regardless of time.

signals.name: Medication non-adherence — urgent clinical priority
signals.clueInStem: stopped his tablets and has been acting strangely since yesterday
signals.whyItMatters: Non-adherence in the first weeks after discharge from first episode psychosis is the primary driver of relapse. The discharge plan clearly failed to address adherence strategies.
signals.modelWording: Non-adherence five days post-discharge from first episode psychosis is a medical emergency — relapse risk is highest in the first weeks. Today's review must address adherence urgently, including consideration of depot antipsychotic.

signals.name: GP relationship — acknowledge failure, send addendum today
signals.clueInStem: Dr Adeyemi is frustrated and concerned
signals.whyItMatters: A GP without adequate discharge information is a patient safety risk. This relationship must be urgently repaired — the GP is Reza's primary community contact.
signals.modelWording: I will call Dr Adeyemi back, acknowledge the failure, and send a comprehensive addendum letter today with all missing clinical information.

modelAnswer:
Immediate: Reza must be reviewed today — five days post-discharge, stopped medication, deteriorating. I will arrange urgent community mental health contact now.

Discharge summary failure: The letter fails every minimum standard. No diagnosis, no medications, no follow-up, no case manager. I take responsibility and will send a comprehensive addendum today.

GP: I will call Dr Adeyemi immediately, acknowledge the failure, and provide all missing information — working diagnosis, medications and doses, relapse indicators, case manager contact, outpatient appointment, and emergency escalation.

Supervision failure: After-hours registrar discharge letter for first episode psychosis without consultant co-signature is a supervision failure. This will be raised at the next governance meeting.

Adherence: Today's urgent review must address non-adherence — depot antipsychotic should be considered.

title: Capacity Assessment Documentation — Life-Sustaining Treatment Refusal
stem:
You are the consultation-liaison consultant. During your ward round you review the previous day's notes. You find an entry from your registrar, Dr Claire Wu, following a bedside consultation for Mrs Irene Papageorgiou, a 78-year-old Greek-born woman who has been in Australia for 50 years and is fluent in English. Mrs Papageorgiou has metastatic bowel cancer and has refused her third cycle of chemotherapy.

Dr Wu's capacity assessment note reads in full: "Seen and assessed. Patient appears to understand her diagnosis. She said she doesn't want more chemo as she's tired of being sick. She seems to have capacity. Discussed with oncology — treatment will not proceed. Impression: capacity intact. Plan: discharge to palliative care."

No formal four-domain assessment is documented. The note does not state what information was provided to the patient, whether she retained and understood it, what the consequences of refusal were explained to be, or whether her decision is consistent with her values. There is no mention of the patient's cultural or spiritual background. The oncology team has proceeded with discharge planning on the basis of this note.

Outline the issues with this documentation and what you would do. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: Four-domain capacity assessment absent — note is not legally defensible
signals.clueInStem: appears to understand her diagnosis. She seems to have capacity
signals.whyItMatters: A legally defensible capacity assessment must explicitly document all four domains: understanding information about the proposed treatment, retaining it, weighing it against personal values, and communicating a decision. Words like 'appears' and 'seems' are not documentation — they are impressions. This note would not withstand medico-legal scrutiny.
signals.modelWording: Dr Wu's note does not constitute a legally defensible capacity assessment. All four domains must be documented with specific evidence from the assessment: what information was given, what the patient demonstrated she understood and retained, how she weighed the decision against her values, and how she communicated her choice.

signals.name: Information provided not documented — consent validity unknown
signals.clueInStem: The note does not state what information was provided to the patient
signals.whyItMatters: Capacity to refuse treatment depends on the patient having received accurate information about her diagnosis, prognosis, the proposed treatment, and the likely consequences of refusal. Without documenting what was explained, it is impossible to know whether her refusal was informed.
signals.modelWording: There is no documentation of what information Mrs Papageorgiou was given about her prognosis, the trajectory of untreated metastatic bowel cancer, or the alternatives to chemotherapy. Without this, her capacity to make an informed decision cannot be assessed.

signals.name: Discharge proceeding on invalid assessment — must be halted
signals.clueInStem: The oncology team has proceeded with discharge planning on the basis of this note
signals.whyItMatters: Discharge to palliative care based on an inadequate capacity assessment exposes the hospital and clinical team to significant medico-legal risk. The discharge must be paused until a proper capacity assessment is completed and documented.
signals.modelWording: Discharge to palliative care must be paused. I will conduct a full capacity assessment today and document it properly before discharge proceeds. I will inform the oncology team that the current documentation does not support a valid capacity determination.

signals.name: Cultural context not explored — Greek background, values-based decision
signals.clueInStem: 78-year-old Greek-born woman who has been in Australia for 50 years
signals.whyItMatters: A capacity assessment for a life-limiting decision must explore whether the decision is consistent with the patient's values, which may be shaped by cultural or spiritual background. Greek cultural traditions may influence views on dying, family involvement in medical decisions, and attitudes toward palliative care.
signals.modelWording: Mrs Papageorgiou's Greek cultural and spiritual background must be explored — not to challenge her decision, but to ensure it is genuinely her own and consistent with her values. This is particularly important for a life-limiting treatment refusal.

signals.name: Teaching opportunity — registrar needs specific feedback on capacity documentation
signals.clueInStem: entry from your registrar, Dr Claire Wu
signals.whyItMatters: Dr Wu needs direct, specific teaching on what capacity documentation requires — particularly for life-sustaining treatment refusals where the medico-legal stakes are highest. This is a supervision responsibility.
signals.modelWording: I will meet with Dr Wu to review the standard for capacity documentation. For life-sustaining treatment refusals, the four-domain structure, information provided, values exploration, and cultural context must all be explicitly documented.

modelAnswer:
Immediate action: Discharge to palliative care must be paused. I will conduct a proper capacity assessment today and document it fully before discharge proceeds.

Documentation inadequacy: Dr Wu's note is not a legally defensible capacity assessment. It contains no four-domain assessment, no documentation of information provided or confirmed understood, and no exploration of cultural values. Words like 'appears' and 'seems' are impressions, not assessment findings.

What a proper assessment requires: I will document all four domains explicitly — what information was given about diagnosis, prognosis, and consequences of refusal; what Mrs Papageorgiou demonstrated she understood and retained; how she weighed the decision against her personal values; and how she communicated her choice clearly.

Cultural context: Mrs Papageorgiou's Greek background must be explored to ensure this decision is genuinely her own and values-congruent — not because her refusal should be challenged, but because a life-limiting decision requires this level of care.

If capacity is confirmed: Her refusal of chemotherapy is legally and ethically binding. My note must document this clearly, with full four-domain evidence.

Teaching: I will meet with Dr Wu to provide specific feedback — capacity documentation for life-sustaining treatment refusals requires a higher standard than routine assessments, and this case demonstrates exactly why.

title: Mental Health Act Documentation — Inadequate Legal Justification
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, acute inpatient unit supervisor
stem:
You are the supervising consultant on an acute inpatient unit. A registrar, Dr James Park, asks you to review Mental Health Act paperwork he has completed for Sofia, a 28-year-old Aboriginal woman detained overnight after she attempted to leave the ward saying staff were "poisoning my baby through the breast milk."

The MHA form reads in full: "Psychotic. Risk to infant. Needs admission. Detained under s.26."

There is no description of the specific behaviour observed, no record of Sofia's own account of her beliefs, no documentation of less restrictive alternatives considered, no capacity reasoning, and no reference to the cultural context. Sofia is breastfeeding her three-day-old infant. She has repeatedly asked for her aunt to be called. The nurses tell you the aunt has not been contacted.

You also note that the infant has not been seen by the paediatric team and no child protection notification has been made.

Outline the problems with this documentation and what must happen now. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: MHA documentation legally inadequate — bare label, no criteria evidenced
signals.clueInStem: Psychotic. Risk to infant. Needs admission. Detained under s.26
signals.whyItMatters: Mental Health Act documentation must justify criteria with specific observed facts, behaviour, risk reasoning, capacity assessment, and evidence that less restrictive options were considered and rejected. Four words do not constitute lawful involuntary detention documentation — this form is not legally defensible.
signals.modelWording: The MHA form is not legally defensible. It uses diagnostic labels without evidencing the statutory criteria. The form must document: the specific behaviour and beliefs observed, Sofia's own account, the risk to the infant with clinical reasoning, why voluntary treatment is inadequate, and what less restrictive options were considered.

signals.name: Infant safety — paediatric review and child protection not actioned
signals.clueInStem: the infant has not been seen by the paediatric team and no child protection notification has been made
signals.whyItMatters: An acutely psychotic breastfeeding mother with delusional beliefs about poisoning through breast milk presents a specific and immediate risk to the infant. Paediatric review and child protection notification are mandatory — their absence is a serious patient safety failure.
signals.modelWording: The infant must be reviewed by paediatrics immediately. A child protection notification must be made — an acutely psychotic mother with delusional beliefs specifically involving harm through breast milk meets the threshold for mandatory notification in every Australian jurisdiction.

signals.name: Breastfeeding and psychosis — specific infant risk not assessed
signals.clueInStem: poisoning my baby through the breast milk
signals.whyItMatters: The specific delusional content — that breast milk is poisoned — creates a direct and specific risk of the mother stopping breastfeeding abruptly, which can cause medical harm to a three-day-old neonate, or acting on the belief in other ways. This specific risk must be documented and managed.
signals.modelWording: Sofia's belief that she is poisoning her infant through breast milk is not generic psychosis — it is a specific, targeted delusion with direct implications for infant safety. The clinical plan must address breastfeeding safely, neonatal nutrition, and supervision of mother-infant contact.

signals.name: Cultural safety — Aboriginal woman, aunt not contacted, cultural support absent
signals.clueInStem: She has repeatedly asked for her aunt to be called. The nurses tell you the aunt has not been contacted
signals.whyItMatters: Sofia has specifically and repeatedly requested her aunt. Failing to contact a nominated support person for an Aboriginal woman in a psychiatric crisis is a cultural safety failure. For Aboriginal patients, family and community connection is both a clinical and cultural right. This must be urgently rectified.
signals.modelWording: Sofia has asked for her aunt to be called — this has not happened. For an Aboriginal woman in an acute psychiatric crisis, failure to involve her nominated family support is both a cultural safety failure and a clinical one. The aunt must be contacted immediately.

signals.name: Least restrictive alternatives not documented
signals.clueInStem: no documentation of less restrictive alternatives considered
signals.whyItMatters: Every Australian Mental Health Act requires that involuntary detention is the least restrictive option available. The MHA form must show that voluntary admission, supported decision-making with the aunt, or other options were considered and found insufficient.
signals.modelWording: The MHA form must document why voluntary treatment is inadequate and what less restrictive alternatives were considered. Detention without this reasoning does not meet the statutory threshold for involuntary treatment.

signals.name: Registrar supervision — form must be rewritten before it is relied upon
signals.clueInStem: A registrar, Dr James Park, asks you to review Mental Health Act paperwork
signals.whyItMatters: I must not approve this form as written. A legally inadequate MHA form that is relied upon for continued detention creates significant medico-legal risk for the clinician, the hospital, and most importantly undermines Sofia's legal rights.
signals.modelWording: I will not approve this MHA documentation as written. Dr Park must rewrite the form with my supervision before it is relied upon — this is a teaching opportunity about MHA documentation standards and Sofia's legal rights.

modelAnswer:
Immediate priorities run in parallel:

Infant safety: The infant must be reviewed by paediatrics now. A child protection notification must be made — an acutely psychotic mother with a specific delusion about poisoning through breast milk meets the mandatory notification threshold. This cannot wait.

Sofia's aunt: Sofia has asked for her aunt repeatedly. This has not happened. The aunt must be called immediately — this is both a cultural safety obligation and a clinical one for an Aboriginal woman in acute crisis.

MHA documentation: The form is not legally defensible and must not be relied upon as written. "Psychotic. Risk to infant. Detained." evidences nothing. The form must document: specific observed behaviour and beliefs, Sofia's own account, the specific risk to the infant with clinical reasoning, why voluntary treatment is inadequate, and what less restrictive alternatives were considered.

Breastfeeding: Sofia's delusion specifically targets breast milk. The clinical plan must address safe feeding of the three-day-old neonate, mother-infant contact under supervision, and breastfeeding guidance in the context of acute psychosis and antipsychotic medication.

Teaching: I will supervise Dr Park in rewriting the MHA documentation. This is a direct teaching session on legal standards and Aboriginal patients' rights — the form must be completed properly before continued detention is lawful.

title: Forensic Discharge Summary — Do Not Co-Sign
setting: Forensic inpatient unit — co-sign review queue
stem:
You are the consultant forensic psychiatrist. A discharge summary is in your co-sign queue from Dr Michael Tan, a first-year registrar, for Wayne, a 44-year-old man being discharged after a 12-week admission. Wayne has schizophrenia, cannabis and methamphetamine use, a history of violence towards his mother when unwell, and a prior not guilty by reason of mental illness (NGMI) finding for assault six years ago.

The four-paragraph summary documents: diagnosis (schizophrenia), medication (olanzapine 20mg), and one line: "risk: reduced during admission." It does not mention the NGMI finding, forensic history, substance use, or violence history. There is no risk formulation and no relapse prevention plan. Follow-up reads: "Wayne — please call to arrange follow-up." Wayne's mother has not been notified. No forensic community support or housing is mentioned.

Outline what you would do and why. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: Do not co-sign — summary is clinically and legally inadequate
signals.clueInStem: A discharge summary has been placed in your co-sign queue
signals.whyItMatters: Co-signing this summary transfers consultant accountability to a document that could directly contribute to harm. A co-signed letter is taken as consultant approval — this summary cannot be approved as written.
signals.modelWording: I will not co-sign this discharge summary. I will return it to Dr Tan with a specific list of required additions before it is released.

signals.name: Forensic history absent — NGMI essential inclusion
signals.clueInStem: It does not mention the NGMI finding, forensic history
signals.whyItMatters: Any clinician receiving a discharge letter for a forensic patient must know the forensic history to calibrate risk. The NGMI finding and forensic supervision history are essential — their absence means the receiving clinician manages Wayne without critical legal and clinical context.
signals.modelWording: The NGMI finding and conditional forensic discharge history are essential inclusions. Their absence means the receiving clinician cannot manage Wayne's risk appropriately.

signals.name: Risk reduced is not a risk formulation
signals.clueInStem: risk: reduced during admission
signals.whyItMatters: 'Risk reduced during admission' describes controlled inpatient behaviour — not community risk. A proper formulation addresses static factors, dynamic factors including substance use, and protective factors in the specific community context.
signals.modelWording: A risk formulation for a forensic patient must address static, dynamic, and protective factors in the community environment. 'Risk reduced during admission' is not a community risk formulation.

signals.name: Mother not notified — identified victim, public safety obligation
signals.clueInStem: history of violence towards his mother. Wayne's mother has not been notified
signals.whyItMatters: Wayne's mother is both the identified victim of his prior violence and potentially his primary carer. Failing to notify her of his discharge is both a clinical and public safety failure.
signals.modelWording: Wayne's mother is the identified victim of his prior violence. She must be notified of his discharge and offered involvement in the safety and relapse prevention plan.

signals.name: Patient-initiated follow-up is not a plan
signals.clueInStem: Wayne — please call to arrange follow-up
signals.whyItMatters: For a forensic patient with schizophrenia and a violence history, leaving follow-up to patient initiative is not a plan. Confirmed outpatient appointments, community forensic team contact, and assertive follow-up are mandatory.
signals.modelWording: Leaving follow-up contingent on Wayne calling is not acceptable for a forensic patient. Confirmed appointments, community forensic team contact, and assertive follow-up dates must be in place before discharge.

modelAnswer:
I will not co-sign this summary. I will return it to Dr Tan with required additions before release.
