Missing: Full forensic history including NGMI finding; complete substance use history; proper risk formulation with static, dynamic, and protective community factors; relapse prevention plan; confirmed follow-up appointments and forensic community team contact; notification to and involvement of Wayne's mother.

Teaching: I will supervise Dr Tan in rewriting this — explaining why forensic discharge documentation has a higher standard and what the consequences are if it fails.

title: Perinatal Discharge Summary — Missing Infant Safety and Relapse Planning
stem:
A discharge summary for Aisha, a 29-year-old woman, is awaiting your co-signature. Aisha was admitted to the mother-baby unit six weeks ago following a postpartum psychotic episode — her first psychiatric presentation — three weeks after the birth of her daughter Lily. She responded well to olanzapine and is being discharged home with her baby.

The discharge summary reads: "Aisha's mental state has significantly improved. She is currently euthymic with good insight. She is bonding appropriately with Lily. Diagnosis: brief psychotic episode. Plan: continue olanzapine 10mg. Follow up with perinatal outpatient clinic in 4 weeks. GP to monitor."

The summary does not name Lily or include a child safety assessment. There is no documentation of parenting capacity observations during admission. Breastfeeding and olanzapine compatibility is not addressed. There is no relapse prevention plan specific to the perinatal context. There is no mention of what to do if Aisha deteriorates at home.

You note from the referral documentation that Aisha arrived in Australia from Somalia three years ago, has no extended family in Australia, and her husband works long hours and was not involved in the admission.

Outline the problems with this summary and what must happen before discharge. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: Lily not named — child safety assessment absent
signals.clueInStem: The summary does not name Lily or include a child safety assessment
signals.whyItMatters: Any discharge from a mother-baby unit requires explicit documentation of the infant's safety assessment. Lily is a vulnerable third party — the discharge letter must name her, document parenting capacity observations during admission, assess infant attachment, and specify a child safeguarding plan if concerns exist.
signals.modelWording: Lily must be named in the discharge documentation and a child safety assessment must be explicitly documented. This must include observed parenting capacity during the six-week admission, infant attachment quality, and a specific plan for Lily's safety in the community.

signals.name: Postpartum psychosis relapse risk — no perinatal relapse prevention plan
signals.clueInStem: postpartum psychotic episode — her first psychiatric presentation
signals.whyItMatters: Postpartum psychosis carries a 50% recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies and significant relapse risk in the months following the current episode. A perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan — covering early warning signs, crisis escalation, contingency if she deteriorates at home alone with Lily, and guidance for any future pregnancy — is essential.
signals.modelWording: Aisha has had postpartum psychosis. The discharge plan must include a perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan: early warning signs agreed with Aisha and her husband, a crisis escalation pathway, what to do if she deteriorates when alone with Lily, and explicit guidance for any future pregnancy.

signals.name: Olanzapine and breastfeeding not addressed
signals.clueInStem: continue olanzapine 10mg
signals.whyItMatters: The safety of olanzapine in breastfeeding must be explicitly addressed in the discharge plan. Whether Aisha is breastfeeding or formula feeding, the decision, the risks, infant monitoring requirements, and Aisha's informed consent must be documented. Prescribing olanzapine without addressing breastfeeding compatibility is a clinical documentation failure.
signals.modelWording: The discharge summary prescribes olanzapine 10mg without addressing breastfeeding. The risk-benefit discussion about olanzapine and breastfeeding, Aisha's informed decision, and any infant monitoring requirements must be explicitly documented before discharge.

signals.name: Social isolation — Somali background, no family, husband not involved
signals.clueInStem: arrived in Australia from Somalia three years ago, has no extended family in Australia, and her husband works long hours and was not involved in the admission
signals.whyItMatters: Aisha is being discharged into profound social isolation — no Australian family network, a husband who was absent throughout the admission, and a newborn. The highest-risk period after postpartum psychosis discharge is when a mother is alone with her infant. The discharge plan must explicitly address who will be present, what support is available, and what happens if she deteriorates alone with Lily.
signals.modelWording: Aisha is being discharged with no Australian family, a husband who was absent throughout admission, and a newborn infant. The discharge plan must explicitly state who will be present at home, what community supports are in place, and what the plan is if she deteriorates when alone with Lily.

signals.name: Four-week follow-up is too long — post-psychosis discharge standard
signals.clueInStem: Follow up with perinatal outpatient clinic in 4 weeks
signals.whyItMatters: The highest-risk period after discharge from postpartum psychosis is the first one to two weeks. Community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and a clinical review within one week is the standard of care — four weeks is clinically unsafe for this presentation.
signals.modelWording: Four-week follow-up is clinically insufficient for postpartum psychosis discharge. Community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and a clinical review within one week is the standard of care — I will change this before the summary is released.

modelAnswer:
I will not co-sign this summary. I will return it to the registrar with required additions before discharge proceeds.

Child safety: Lily must be named and a child safety assessment documented — including parenting capacity observations from the six-week admission, infant attachment quality, and a specific community safety plan for Lily.

Breastfeeding: The olanzapine and breastfeeding decision must be explicitly documented — the risk-benefit discussion, Aisha's informed decision, and any infant monitoring plan.

Relapse prevention: A perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan must be written covering: agreed early warning signs, crisis escalation pathway, what to do if she deteriorates at home with Lily, and guidance for any future pregnancy. Postpartum psychosis carries a 50% recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies.

Social isolation: The discharge plan must explicitly address Aisha's home situation. No Australian family, absent husband, and a newborn infant — who will be present? What community supports are arranged? What is the plan if she deteriorates alone with Lily?

Follow-up: Four weeks is clinically unsafe. Community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and clinical review within one week is the minimum standard after postpartum psychosis discharge. I will change this before the summary is released.

title: Registrar Discharging High-Risk Patient Without Safety Plan
setting: Acute inpatient psychiatric unit — Tuesday afternoon supervision
stem:
You are conducting your weekly supervision session with Dr Priya Sharma, a second-year advanced trainee. She presents a case for discharge planning: Callum, a 29-year-old man admitted three weeks ago following an overdose of 40 paracetamol tablets. He has recurrent major depressive disorder and borderline personality disorder.

Dr Sharma presents a risk assessment of "low to moderate." She tells you: "He's engaged in sessions, his mood is improved, he denies SI, and he wants to go home." She has not contacted Callum's mother, who brought him in and is his emergency contact. She has not developed a written safety plan. She has not arranged community follow-up. She has not reviewed his previous inpatient presentations.

You note from the system that Callum has had four prior admissions in three years, each following a paracetamol overdose. Dr Sharma is also telling you that there is pressure from the NUM because the ward is full and a bed is urgently needed.

Outline how you would respond to this situation. A list without justification will not be awarded marks.

signals.name: Risk formulation is inadequate — prior admissions not reviewed
signals.clueInStem: four prior admissions in three years, each following a paracetamol overdose — not reviewed by registrar
signals.whyItMatters: Four paracetamol overdoses in three years is a high-risk recurrence pattern — previous self-harm is the strongest single predictor of completed suicide. A discharge risk assessment that does not review prior admissions is clinically indefensible. This is not a low-to-moderate risk presentation.
signals.modelWording: Dr Sharma has not reviewed Callum's admission history. Four paracetamol overdoses in three years is a high-risk recurrence pattern that must be central to any discharge risk formulation — this is not a low-to-moderate risk presentation and I will not authorise discharge on this basis.

signals.name: No collateral — mother not contacted
signals.clueInStem: Has not contacted Callum's mother, who brought him in and is his emergency contact
signals.whyItMatters: Callum's mother brought him in, is his nominated emergency contact, and is likely his primary support at home. Discharge planning without family collateral in a patient with BPD and recurrent suicidality is a clinical failure — the home environment he is returning to is unknown.
signals.modelWording: Callum's mother brought him in and is his emergency contact. She has not been contacted. Family collateral is essential before discharge — both to assess the home environment and to involve his primary support in the safety plan.

signals.name: No written safety plan — mandatory for BPD and recurrent suicidality
signals.clueInStem: She has not developed a written safety plan
signals.whyItMatters: A written collaborative safety plan is a minimum standard of care for discharge in a patient with BPD and four prior overdoses. It must include coping strategies, means restriction, crisis contacts, the role of family, and follow-up arrangements. Its absence is a specific discharge planning failure.
signals.modelWording: A written safety plan co-developed with Callum, reviewed with his mother, and linked to community services is a non-negotiable discharge component for a patient with BPD and four prior paracetamol overdoses. This has not been completed.

signals.name: Bed pressure cannot drive discharge decision
signals.clueInStem: pressure from the NUM because the ward is full and a bed is urgently needed
signals.whyItMatters: Discharge of a high-risk patient driven by bed pressure rather than clinical readiness is a governance failure. The consultant must not yield to administrative pressure on safety-critical decisions — and must document clearly that the decision is clinical.
signals.modelWording: Bed pressure does not constitute a clinical reason for discharge. I will speak directly to the NUM and document clearly that Callum's continued admission is a clinical safety decision. Administrative pressure cannot override clinical judgement in a patient with four prior overdoses.

signals.name: Supervision obligation — teach the formulation, do not just override
signals.clueInStem: weekly supervision session with Dr Priya Sharma, a second-year advanced trainee
signals.whyItMatters: The supervisor's role is not to simply override the registrar's plan but to teach the reasoning behind a defensible discharge risk assessment. Dr Sharma needs to understand why each missing element matters — this is a direct teaching moment.
signals.modelWording: I will not simply override Dr Sharma's plan. I will use this supervision session to walk through what a defensible discharge risk assessment requires — prior admission review, collateral, written safety plan, community follow-up — and why each element matters for this specific patient.

signals.name: Community follow-up not arranged — post-discharge contact essential
signals.clueInStem: She has not arranged community follow-up
signals.whyItMatters: Post-discharge follow-up within 48-72 hours is an evidence-based suicide prevention intervention. Its absence in a patient with recurrent suicidality and BPD is a specific gap that must be rectified before discharge.
signals.modelWording: Community follow-up must be confirmed before discharge — an appointment within 48-72 hours with a community mental health clinician or outpatient psychiatry is the minimum standard for this presentation.

modelAnswer:
Callum is not ready for discharge. Four paracetamol overdoses in three years is a high-risk recurrence pattern — the registrar has not reviewed prior admissions, has no collateral, no safety plan, and no community follow-up.

I will not simply override Dr Sharma. I will use this as a direct teaching session: walk through why each missing element matters clinically and legally, and require her to redo the assessment with my guidance.

Bed pressure: I will speak to the NUM directly and document clearly that Callum's continued admission is a clinical safety decision. Administrative pressure cannot override clinical judgement.

What must happen before discharge: (1) Review of all four prior admissions; (2) Collateral from Callum's mother; (3) Written safety plan co-developed with Callum including means restriction and crisis contacts; (4) Confirmed community follow-up within 48 hours; (5) GP letter.

Documentation: My decision not to discharge must be documented with clinical reasoning in the medical record.

title: Methamphetamine Psychosis vs First-Episode Schizophrenia
candidateRole: Psychiatry registrar on call, asked to review by ED
stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar on call. The ED senior registrar phones at 2 am requesting urgent review of Marcus, a 24-year-old man brought by police after he was found agitated and shouting in a shopping centre carpark, insisting that strangers had "inserted transmitters" into his teeth. He has no prior psychiatric history on the system. Urine drug screen is positive for methamphetamine. He is now calmer after 10 mg oral olanzapine given 90 minutes ago.

The ED registrar says: "He's calmed down, his obs are stable, and I need the bed. My consultant says this is a drug psychosis — once he's settled he can go." When you interview Marcus, he tells you the transmitters have been monitoring him "for six months." His sister, reached by phone, tells you she has been worried about him for eight months: he stopped going to work, began spending hours researching "surveillance technology," and only started using methamphetamine last week — "to cope with the fear."

signals.name: Symptom duration precedes substance use — primary psychosis must be excluded
signals.clueInStem: transmitters monitoring him for six months; methamphetamine use only started last week
signals.whyItMatters: When psychotic symptoms precede substance use by months, a primary psychotic disorder is the leading diagnosis. Methamphetamine can exacerbate or unmask schizophrenia and should not be accepted as the sole explanation without further assessment.
signals.relatedRisk: Discharging untreated first-episode schizophrenia from ED carries significant risk of deterioration, self-neglect, and loss to follow-up.
signals.modelWording: The sister's collateral establishes that paranoid beliefs began eight months ago — well before any methamphetamine use. This timeline makes primary psychosis the working diagnosis until proven otherwise; a drug psychosis explanation cannot stand.

signals.name: Collateral history contradicts the drug-only diagnosis
signals.clueInStem: his sister says she has been worried for eight months — stopped working, hours researching surveillance
signals.whyItMatters: Collateral from a close informant is essential in first-presentation psychosis. It establishes prodromal features, functional decline, and a timeline that cannot be obtained from the patient alone in an ED setting.
signals.modelWording: The sister's account — eight months of functional decline, social withdrawal, and persecutory preoccupation — constitutes a prodrome. This collateral history is the single most important piece of diagnostic information in this presentation.

signals.name: Bed pressure driving premature discharge — systemic risk
signals.clueInStem: I need the bed; my consultant says this is a drug psychosis — once he's settled he can go
signals.whyItMatters: Bed pressure in ED creates a pull toward premature diagnostic closure. A consultant directive to discharge without psychiatric assessment does not transfer clinical or legal responsibility from the psychiatry registrar performing the assessment.
signals.relatedSystem: First-episode psychosis frequently presents via ED; the quality of the initial assessment shapes the patient's entire subsequent treatment trajectory.
signals.modelWording: The ED consultant's view is understandable, but bed pressure does not change the diagnostic uncertainty. I am responsible for my assessment, and I cannot endorse discharge without completing it. If there is disagreement, I will escalate to my own supervisor.

signals.name: Adequate observation period before discharge
signals.clueInStem: calmer after olanzapine 90 minutes ago
signals.whyItMatters: Olanzapine sedation can temporarily suppress behavioural disturbance without resolving the underlying psychotic state. A meaningful psychiatric assessment requires a patient who is alert, oriented, and able to engage — not simply sedated.
signals.modelWording: Marcus is calmer, but clinical calm after acute sedation is not the same as resolution of psychosis. I need to complete a full mental state examination when he is sufficiently alert, and reassess after an adequate observation period.

signals.name: Risk assessment before any discharge decision
signals.clueInStem: brought by police; found shouting in a carpark
signals.whyItMatters: A first-episode psychosis patient found alone at night in a public space with paranoid beliefs about surveillance is at risk of self-neglect, accidental harm, and social disintegration. A risk assessment must precede discharge.
signals.modelWording: I must complete a risk assessment addressing: insight into illness, ability to self-care, support at home, risk of harm from others if behaviour is misinterpreted, and his own understanding of what happened. This cannot be deferred.

signals.name: Disposition — inpatient admission vs community follow-up with clear plan
signals.clueInStem: no prior psychiatric history; first presentation
signals.whyItMatters: First-episode psychosis requiring a clear disposition pathway: if admitted, specialist early psychosis service referral; if discharged, next-day CATT or early psychosis intake. Discharge without any follow-up plan is not acceptable.
signals.modelWording: Regardless of whether Marcus is admitted or discharged with supports, I will arrange formal early psychosis review within 24–48 hours, document the diagnostic uncertainty, and brief the sister on what to watch for and who to call.

modelAnswer:
The timeline is the key diagnostic finding: paranoid beliefs about surveillance began eight months ago, and methamphetamine use started only last week. The drug psychosis explanation cannot account for eight months of prodromal decline in a person without prior substance use. Primary psychosis — most likely first-episode schizophrenia — is the working diagnosis.

The ED consultant's directive to clear the bed does not change this assessment. I am responsible for my own clinical conclusion. I will not endorse discharge while diagnostic uncertainty remains and before a full mental state examination is possible. I will escalate to my supervisor if pressured.

I will complete the MSE once Marcus is sufficiently alert after sedation, obtain a thorough collateral history from his sister, and conduct a structured risk assessment. Admission to a psychiatric inpatient unit is likely indicated for diagnostic clarification and initiation of antipsychotic treatment. If discharged, same-day or next-morning CATT or early psychosis review is mandatory. I will brief his sister about warning signs and emergency contact numbers regardless of disposition.

title: Anorexia Nervosa Refusing NG Feeding — Capacity and Involuntary Treatment
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist called urgently to ED
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on call. The ED consultant phones urgently about Eliza, a 22-year-old woman with a ten-year history of anorexia nervosa, brought by ambulance. Her current BMI is 12.5. Investigations show: heart rate 44 bpm, potassium 2.6 mmol/L, phosphate 0.55 mmol/L, and glucose 3.1 mmol/L. She is alert and articulate, making sustained eye contact, and declines nasogastric feeding, saying: "I understand exactly what you're telling me. I know the risks and I choose to go home. This is my life."

The ED consultant says he is willing to "accept her decision" because she is "calm, oriented, and not suicidal." Eliza says she does not want her parents, who are in the waiting room, to be involved. The treating ED team has commenced IV electrolyte replacement, which she is tolerating.

signals.name: Formal capacity assessment is mandatory — calmness does not equal capacity
signals.clueInStem: calm, oriented, and not suicidal
signals.whyItMatters: Capacity is decision-specific and requires assessment of all four criteria: understanding, retention, weighing, and communicating. Being calm and oriented is not the same as having decision-making capacity for a life-threatening refusal of treatment. Anorexia nervosa characteristically distorts the ability to weigh information because illness ego-syntonicity impairs the 'use and weigh' element.
signals.modelWording: A formal capacity assessment is required for this specific decision — refusing nasogastric feeding at a life-threatening BMI. I must assess whether Eliza can use and weigh the information in a way that is free from the distortion imposed by her eating disorder. Calmness and orientation satisfy only two of four criteria.

signals.name: Medically critical — refeeding syndrome risk and immediate cardiac danger
signals.clueInStem: HR 44, potassium 2.6, phosphate 0.55, glucose 3.1, BMI 12.5
signals.whyItMatters: These parameters represent life-threatening medical instability. Bradycardia at this level is associated with ventricular arrhythmia. Hypophosphataemia is the key risk factor for refeeding syndrome. This is a medical emergency independent of the psychiatric capacity question.
signals.relatedRisk: Patients with anorexia nervosa and BMI below 13 have a mortality rate that may exceed 5–10% per admission; this is one of the highest mortality rates of any psychiatric illness.
signals.modelWording: Regardless of the capacity question, Eliza's HR of 44, potassium of 2.6, and phosphate of 0.55 represent a medically critical state requiring inpatient medical management, including cardiac monitoring. The ED consultant and I need to agree on this as the immediate medical priority.

signals.name: Capacity may be impaired by illness — ego-syntonicity of anorexia nervosa
signals.clueInStem: I know the risks and I choose to go home. This is my life
signals.whyItMatters: Anorexia nervosa is unique among psychiatric illnesses in that the illness itself constitutes the cognitive framework through which the patient evaluates the decision. An ego-syntonic rejection of life-saving treatment, driven by fear of weight gain, may not meet the 'use and weigh' standard even when the patient can articulate risks.
signals.modelWording: Eliza's refusal is not simply an autonomous choice — it may be a direct expression of her eating disorder. The fact that she articulates risk does not demonstrate that she can weigh that information without distortion imposed by the illness. I must probe this explicitly: ask her what would happen to her if she went home, what she believes her risks actually are, and how she feels about gaining weight.

signals.name: Mental Health Act — involuntary treatment is a legitimate option if capacity impaired
signals.clueInStem: declines nasogastric feeding; medically critical
signals.whyItMatters: If Eliza lacks capacity for this decision, involuntary treatment under the relevant Mental Health Act (or Guardianship legislation, jurisdiction-dependent) is ethically and legally appropriate. The treating team must not default to accepting refusal without establishing whether the legislative threshold is met.
signals.modelWording: If my assessment concludes that Eliza lacks capacity for this specific decision, the appropriate response is to invoke the relevant involuntary treatment framework. I will document my capacity assessment in detail, consult with the medical team, and initiate the relevant MHA or guardianship process as appropriate to jurisdiction.

signals.name: Family involvement — privacy versus urgent need for collateral
signals.clueInStem: she does not want her parents involved; parents are in the waiting room
signals.whyItMatters: Eliza's privacy must be respected to the extent possible, but when she may lack capacity and is in a life-threatening state, family collateral about her clinical history and trajectory is clinically and legally relevant. Speaking with parents about the clinical situation — without disclosing details Eliza has not consented to — may be possible and appropriate.
signals.modelWording: I will discuss with Eliza my wish to speak with her parents about the current situation, explaining that in a medical emergency this may be necessary regardless of her preference. I will be transparent with her about what I intend to say and why.

signals.name: Multidisciplinary and specialist eating disorder input
signals.clueInStem: ten-year history of anorexia nervosa
signals.whyItMatters: Medically critical anorexia nervosa with a ten-year history requires specialist eating disorder input in the admission and management decision. The ED consultant and general psychiatry team should not manage this without specialist involvement if available.
signals.modelWording: I will contact the specialist eating disorder service today. Regardless of the capacity outcome, Eliza should be managed by a team with expertise in medically severe anorexia nervosa — this includes a dietitian experienced in refeeding, specialist nursing, and a treating psychiatrist with ED experience.

modelAnswer:
Eliza is in a medically critical state: HR 44, potassium 2.6, phosphate 0.55, BMI 12.5. This is a medical emergency. Regardless of the capacity question, she requires inpatient medical management and cardiac monitoring.

The ED consultant's assessment that she has capacity is premature. Capacity is decision-specific and requires demonstration of all four criteria, including use and weigh. Anorexia nervosa characteristically impairs this through ego-syntonicity — the illness shapes the patient's very framework for evaluating her options. I will conduct a formal, documented capacity assessment specifically probing whether Eliza can weigh the consequences of refusal without distortion imposed by her eating disorder. Articulating risk does not equal using and weighing it.

If I conclude she lacks capacity for this decision, I will initiate the relevant involuntary treatment framework (MHA or guardianship, jurisdiction-dependent) and document this in detail. I will engage the specialist eating disorder service urgently, involve a dietitian experienced in refeeding protocol, and address the family involvement question with Eliza transparently, explaining that her safety may require me to speak with her parents.

title: Acute Confusional State in an Older Woman — Delirium Missed as Dementia
candidateRole: Consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to ED
stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar on call. ED asks you to review Nora, a 78-year-old woman brought by her daughter at 10 pm following two days of increasing confusion, calling out at night, and refusing to eat or take her medications. She lives alone in a granny flat behind her daughter's house. Her GP recently commenced oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily for urinary incontinence three weeks ago.

On assessment, Nora is alert but disoriented in time and place, has fluctuating attention, and is easily distracted. She cannot complete serial sevens. She scored 21 on her MMSE at the GP one month ago; her current MMSE is 12. She is distressed and repeatedly calls for her late husband. Her MSE is otherwise unremarkable — no sustained psychotic phenomena, no perceptual disturbance on direct questioning.

The ED registrar tells you: "She's been here twice before for confusion — both times they said it was her dementia and she went home. Her daughter says she's been going downhill anyway."

signals.name: Delirium is the primary diagnosis — acute onset, fluctuating course, attentional deficit
signals.clueInStem: two days of increasing confusion; fluctuating attention; MMSE drop from 21 to 12
signals.whyItMatters: Delirium is characterised by acute onset, fluctuating course, and attentional disturbance. A nine-point drop in MMSE over one month with an acute two-day deterioration is delirium until proven otherwise. Confusing delirium with dementia progression is one of the commonest and most dangerous diagnostic errors in older adult presentations.
signals.modelWording: The acute two-day onset, fluctuating attention, inability to sustain concentration, and nine-point MMSE decline make delirium the primary diagnosis. Delirium requires investigation and treatment of underlying cause — it is never 'just her dementia getting worse.'

signals.name: Oxybutynin as likely precipitant — anticholinergic toxicity in an older adult
signals.clueInStem: GP recently commenced oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily three weeks ago
signals.whyItMatters: Oxybutynin has high central anticholinergic activity and is a well-recognised precipitant of delirium in older adults, particularly those with pre-existing cognitive impairment. Its introduction three weeks ago precisely correlates with the onset of decline. The medication must be ceased immediately.
signals.relatedRisk: Older adults with any baseline cognitive impairment have markedly increased vulnerability to anticholinergic delirium, which can be prolonged and associated with functional decline.
signals.modelWording: Oxybutynin commenced three weeks ago in a 78-year-old woman with probable underlying cognitive impairment is a high-probability precipitant of her delirium. I will recommend immediate cessation and communicate this clearly to the ED team and to the GP.

signals.name: Underlying dementia does not explain the acute deterioration
signals.clueInStem: both times they said it was her dementia and she went home; MMSE 21 one month ago
signals.whyItMatters: A patient with dementia is at higher risk of delirium, but dementia is not a diagnosis that should absorb acute deteriorations. Previous ED attributions of confusion to dementia may represent prior missed delirium episodes. This pattern of repeated undifferentiated 'dementia' diagnoses must not continue.
signals.modelWording: The pattern of repeated ED presentations labelled as dementia is itself a safety concern. An MMSE of 21 one month ago indicates residual function that is inconsistent with the current state being simply dementia progression. Each acute episode requires its own diagnostic work-up.

signals.name: Admit for delirium management — discharge is unsafe
signals.clueInStem: refusing to eat or take medications; lives alone
signals.whyItMatters: Nora is delirious, not eating, and lives alone. She is unable to safely self-care in her current state. Even if her daughter can provide supervision overnight, return home without resolution of the precipitating cause is unsafe.
signals.modelWording: Nora cannot safely return home tonight. She is refusing food and medications, is disoriented, and lives in a semi-independent setting. Hospital admission for delirium investigation and management is indicated.

signals.name: Collateral history from daughter is clinically essential
signals.clueInStem: daughter says she has been going downhill anyway
signals.whyItMatters: The daughter's comment that she has been 'going downhill anyway' may reflect the daughter's resignation to a narrative of dementia progression, but it must be systematically explored. The daughter can provide timeline, baseline function, medication history, and prior episodes that cannot be obtained from Nora herself.
signals.modelWording: I must speak with the daughter in detail: What is Nora's baseline function? When did this episode begin? Has this happened before and how did it resolve? What medications has she been taking? The daughter's perception of decline must be carefully interrogated, not simply accepted.

signals.name: Delirium investigation — identify and treat all contributors
signals.clueInStem: two days of confusion, refusing medications, not eating
signals.whyItMatters: Delirium is typically multifactorial in older adults. Oxybutynin may be the main precipitant but infection, dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and undertreated pain should all be screened. A single-cause explanation must not foreclose a systematic work-up.
signals.modelWording: I will recommend a delirium screen: FBC, EUC, LFTs, TFTs, MSU, CXR, and a medication review. I will advise the team on non-pharmacological delirium management: familiar environment, family presence, orientation cues, adequate hydration, and avoiding unnecessary sedation.

modelAnswer:
Nora has delirium. The acute two-day onset, fluctuating attention, nine-point MMSE drop, and distress are not explainable by dementia progression alone. The diagnosis is delirium superimposed on underlying cognitive impairment, and this is a medical emergency requiring investigation and treatment.

The most likely precipitant is oxybutynin — a highly anticholinergic medication commenced three weeks ago in a vulnerable older adult. I will recommend immediate cessation and communicate this to the ED team and the GP in writing. A full delirium work-up (FBC, EUC, TFTs, MSU, CXR) should be completed to exclude infection, electrolyte disturbance, and other contributors.

The pattern of repeated presentations labelled as dementia is a systems concern. Prior episodes may have been missed delirium. Nora cannot safely return home tonight: she is not eating, is refusing medications, and lives semi-independently. I will recommend admission with non-pharmacological delirium management, avoiding sedating medications unless necessary, and involving her daughter in orientation and comfort. I will obtain a thorough collateral history from the daughter to establish her true baseline and trajectory.

title: Acute Behavioural Disturbance in ED — Restraint Demand Before Assessment
stem:
You receive an urgent call from the ED charge nurse at 11 pm. Jake, a 32-year-old man with a known history of schizophrenia, is in bay 4, shouting and has overturned a chair. He was brought in by his mother after two days of refusing his depot antipsychotic, refusing food, and making threatening comments to her at home. He is not currently assaulting anyone. He is shouting "I know what you're all doing to me — I can see it." Security staff are present at a distance.

The ED consultant comes on the phone and says: "We need him sedated now before you assess him — he's not safe in the department." Jake's mother pulls you aside: she says he had a "very bad experience six months ago — they held him down and injected him — he's been terrified of hospitals since." She says he has been "really well" on his depot until two days ago when something happened at his supported accommodation that upset him. His last risperidone LAI was 22 days ago (prescribed three-weekly).

signals.name: De-escalation before pharmacological restraint — not currently assaulting anyone
signals.clueInStem: not currently harming anyone; shouting and overturned a chair; security present
signals.whyItMatters: Pharmacological restraint before any attempt at de-escalation is clinically inappropriate and may be unlawful if a patient is not immediately dangerous. Jake is distressed and psychotic, not imminently violent. Premature chemical restraint forecloses the possibility of an adequate assessment and risks escalating rather than reducing agitation.
signals.modelWording: Jake is not currently assaulting anyone. Before any medication is administered, I will attempt verbal de-escalation: approach calmly, reduce environmental stimulation, address him by name, and acknowledge that he is frightened. I will resist the pressure to sedate before this has been attempted.

signals.name: Trauma-informed approach — prior traumatic restraint experience
signals.clueInStem: they held him down and injected him — he's been terrified of hospitals since
signals.whyItMatters: Jake has a documented traumatic experience with forcible restraint. Repeating this without attempting alternatives would re-traumatise him, damage the therapeutic relationship, and likely worsen future help-seeking. The mother's information is clinically vital and must shape the approach.
signals.modelWording: Jake's prior experience of forced restraint has caused lasting fear of hospitals. My approach must be explicitly trauma-informed: I will avoid any sudden movements, not approach with a group of staff, speak directly and honestly about what is happening, and seek his agreement at every step before proceeding.

signals.name: Establish diagnosis before treatment — acute relapse versus other cause
signals.clueInStem: something happened at supported accommodation; two days ago
signals.whyItMatters: Jake's distress is time-locked to an event at his accommodation two days ago. While relapse due to delayed depot is the leading hypothesis, an acute psychosocial stressor, substance use, or a traumatic event must be considered. Sedation without diagnostic formulation risks missing a treatable cause.
signals.modelWording: I need to understand what happened at Jake's accommodation two days ago — this is the precipitant. While doing so, I will assess for substance use, physical illness, and whether his current mental state represents a relapse, a stress response, or a new presentation. Sedation is not a substitute for diagnosis.

signals.name: Depot timing — Jake is within the pharmacokinetic window but at the end of dosing interval
signals.clueInStem: risperidone LAI 22 days ago, prescribed three-weekly
signals.whyItMatters: At 22 days, Jake is near the end of his three-weekly dosing interval. While LAI antipsychotics have tapering rather than abrupt offset, declining plasma levels at the end of the dosing interval can contribute to breakthrough symptoms, particularly under psychosocial stress.
signals.modelWording: Jake's LAI is due and may be contributing to breakthrough symptoms. Once de-escalated, I will discuss depot administration with him, ideally with his agreement. Forced depot in a man with prior traumatic restraint experience should be a last resort after all other measures have failed.

signals.name: Risk to others — mother's safety at home must be assessed
signals.clueInStem: making threatening comments to her at home; brought in by his mother
signals.whyItMatters: Jake made threatening comments to his mother at home over two days. Her safety in the home environment is a relevant risk consideration and must be part of any disposition decision, including whether she can safely return home with him if he is discharged.
signals.modelWording: I must ask Jake's mother directly about the nature and frequency of the threats, whether she felt physically safe, and what has changed in the relationship. Her safety is part of my risk assessment and will influence my recommendation about where Jake goes after this assessment.

signals.name: Mental Health Act — involuntary assessment if Jake cannot engage
signals.clueInStem: refusing depot; shouting; psychotic
signals.whyItMatters: If de-escalation fails and Jake cannot or will not engage safely with assessment, a formal mental health act assessment is the appropriate next step. This provides a legal framework for assessment and, if criteria are met, involuntary treatment. It should follow a genuine attempt at voluntary engagement.
signals.modelWording: If Jake cannot be engaged voluntarily after a genuine de-escalation attempt, I will initiate a formal MHA assessment. I will document the de-escalation attempts, the clinical criteria, and the decision-making process clearly. I will inform Jake of this step and explain why.

signals.name: Mother as collateral and carer — acknowledge her distress
signals.clueInStem: brought in by his mother; she pulls you aside
signals.whyItMatters: Jake's mother has been living with his deterioration for two days, has experienced threats in her home, and has brought him to ED despite his fear. She is providing vital collateral and deserves direct acknowledgement of her distress and clear information about what is happening.
signals.modelWording: I will speak with Jake's mother directly: thank her for bringing him in, acknowledge how difficult the past two days have been, take her account of events seriously, and give her clear information about the process from here. She must not be left waiting in a corridor without acknowledgement.

modelAnswer:
Jake is not currently assaulting anyone. Before any medication is given, I will attempt structured verbal de-escalation: approach calmly and alone, reduce environmental noise, address him by name, and acknowledge that he is frightened. I will not approach with a group of staff. The ED consultant's request to sedate before assessment is understandable given departmental pressure, but pharmacological restraint before a genuine de-escalation attempt is clinically and ethically inappropriate when a patient is not immediately violent.

Jake's prior traumatic restraint experience is clinically essential information. Repeating forced restraint risks re-traumatisation and permanently damaging his willingness to engage with services. My approach will be explicitly trauma-informed throughout.

I need to understand what happened at his accommodation two days ago — this is the precipitant and may represent more than simply depot offset. I will assess for acute stressor, substance use, and whether this represents a relapse. His LAI is due and I will offer it with his agreement once he is settled.

I will speak directly with Jake's mother, acknowledge the past two days, and assess her safety at home. If de-escalation fails and Jake cannot engage, I will initiate a formal MHA assessment and document the process clearly. Admission is likely required given the level of disturbance, lack of depot cover, and the home risk to his mother.

title: Adolescent NSSI in ED — Safe Discharge, Parental Conflict, Child Protection
candidateRole: On-call CAMHS registrar asked to review by paediatric ED
setting: Paediatric ED, 9 pm on a weeknight
stem:
You are the on-call CAMHS registrar. The paediatric ED asks you to review Jade, a 15-year-old girl who presented with superficial lacerations to her forearms. She self-inflicted the cuts with scissors three hours ago. Jade tells you she "snapped" after an argument with her mother and "didn't actually want to die." She denies current suicidal ideation. She has no prior psychiatric presentations.

Her parents are separated. Her mother is crying in the waiting room. Her father arrived 20 minutes later and is demanding Jade be admitted "so the court can see what her mother is doing to her." The two parents are arguing loudly in the corridor. The paediatric ED consultant says: "If she's not suicidal, she should go home tonight — we need the bed."

When you speak to Jade alone, she tells you she has been cutting for approximately four months and has been hiding it. She shows you multiple healed scars. She says she "doesn't feel safe at home" but cannot say more. She becomes tearful when her mother's name is mentioned.

signals.name: Distinguish chronic NSSI from acute suicidality — both require thorough assessment
signals.clueInStem: four months of hidden cutting; denies suicidal ideation; multiple healed scars
signals.whyItMatters: Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and suicidality are distinct but related phenomena. Chronic hidden NSSI lasting four months in a 15-year-old indicates significant and ongoing psychological distress, regardless of absent suicidal intent at this moment. NSSI is a significant predictor of future suicidal behaviour and requires structured assessment, not dismissal.
signals.modelWording: Jade's four months of hidden NSSI is clinically significant and requires a thorough assessment, including: function of the self-harm, emotional regulation capacity, triggers, escalation pattern, and any suicidal cognitions that may not be expressed on direct questioning. Absence of current SI does not mean this is low-risk.

signals.name: "I don't feel safe at home" — this statement must be taken seriously and explored
signals.clueInStem: she says she does not feel safe at home; tearful when mother's name is mentioned
signals.whyItMatters: A 15-year-old spontaneously stating she does not feel safe at home is a serious disclosure that cannot be minimised or explained away by parental conflict alone. It may indicate emotional abuse, domestic violence, neglect, or an unsafe home environment. This is a child protection trigger.
signals.modelWording: Jade's statement that she does not feel safe at home, combined with her tearfulness at her mother's name and four months of hidden self-harm, requires direct, gentle exploration. I must ask in a non-leading way what makes home feel unsafe. Her safety on discharge depends entirely on this being clarified.

signals.name: Child protection — mandatory notification may be required
signals.clueInStem: does not feel safe at home; parents in conflict; mother covering up months of self-harm
signals.whyItMatters: When a child discloses an unsafe home environment and shows evidence of four months of concealed self-harm in a context of high parental conflict, the threshold for a mandatory child protection notification may be met depending on what further exploration reveals. The duty to notify is the clinician's, not the child's.
signals.relatedRisk: Returning Jade to an unsafe environment on the basis of 'no suicidal ideation' could place her at significant harm.
signals.modelWording: I have a duty to explore Jade's safety concern thoroughly and to consider whether a mandatory child protection notification is required. If my assessment raises a reasonable concern about harm, safety, or neglect, I will notify the relevant child protection authority tonight — I do not need to establish certainty, only reasonable suspicion.

signals.name: Parental conflict — manage the ED environment and the process
signals.clueInStem: parents arguing loudly in the corridor; father demanding admission for court purposes
signals.whyItMatters: Loud parental conflict in the ED corridor is harmful to Jade and counterproductive to assessment. The father's explicit motivation — admission to support a court case — is not a clinical indication and must not drive the disposition decision. Both parents need to be managed separately and professionally.
signals.modelWording: I will ask nursing staff to separate Jade's parents and provide them each with a private space. I will speak to each parent separately. I will make it clear to the father that clinical decisions are made on the basis of Jade's needs, not family court proceedings. Admission for legal purposes is not a psychiatric indication.

signals.name: Collateral — four months of hidden self-harm concealed from parents
signals.clueInStem: her mother says Jade has been cutting for months and she has been covering it up
signals.whyItMatters: The fact that Jade's mother has been aware of and concealing the self-harm for months raises questions about the family's capacity to respond to her distress and whether disclosure of the self-harm was safe within the family context. This is important collateral that must be explored.
signals.modelWording: I will ask Jade's mother — separately — what she has observed, when she first knew, and why she did not seek help sooner. This may reveal important information about the family dynamics, Jade's reasons for hiding the behaviour, and whether the home environment supports or impedes disclosure of distress.

signals.name: Disposition — discharge only if home is safe and a clear follow-up plan exists
signals.clueInStem: if she's not suicidal she should go home — bed needed; she says she does not feel safe at home
signals.whyItMatters: Discharge without clarification of home safety and a clear CAMHS follow-up plan is not clinically appropriate. The ED consultant's framing — 'not suicidal, goes home' — applies a binary that is inadequate for chronic NSSI in an adolescent with a safety concern about her home. Admission may be warranted if home safety cannot be established.
signals.modelWording: Discharge tonight is possible only if my assessment clarifies the nature of the home safety concern, establishes that Jade is not at immediate risk, and results in a robust safety plan agreed by Jade and at least one parent. If home safety cannot be established, I will admit under CAMHS for further assessment. I will arrange urgent CAMHS outpatient review within 48–72 hours regardless of disposition.

modelAnswer:
Jade's presentation requires thorough assessment — not rapid discharge. Four months of hidden NSSI, multiple healed scars, and a spontaneous statement that she does not feel safe at home place this well beyond a simple "not suicidal, send home" decision.

My priority is to explore her home safety statement in a private, non-leading way. This is a potential child protection concern and may require a mandatory notification regardless of what her parents say. I do not need certainty — only reasonable suspicion — to notify. I will consider this after completing the exploration.

I will ask staff to separate Jade's parents and speak to each privately. The father's demand for admission to support a court case is not a clinical indication and I will address this directly with him. I will speak to Jade's mother separately about what she knew, when, and why she did not seek help sooner.

The ED consultant's binary — not suicidal means discharge — does not apply here. Discharge is only appropriate if I can clarify the home safety concern, establish a safety plan with Jade and a parent, and arrange urgent CAMHS follow-up within 48–72 hours. If I cannot establish that home is safe, I will admit under CAMHS for further assessment and to allow time to complete the child protection process appropriately.

title: Postpartum Psychosis — Discharge Summary Missing Child Safety
setting: Mother-baby unit, metropolitan hospital — co-sign review
stem:
You are the consultant perinatal psychiatrist. A discharge summary for Aisha, a 29-year-old Somali-Australian woman, is awaiting your co-signature. Aisha was admitted six weeks ago following a postpartum psychotic episode — her first psychiatric presentation — three weeks after the birth of her daughter Lily. She responded well to olanzapine and is being discharged home with her baby.

The discharge summary reads: "Aisha's mental state has significantly improved. She is currently euthymic with good insight. She is bonding appropriately with Lily. Diagnosis: brief psychotic episode. Plan: continue olanzapine 10mg. Follow up with perinatal outpatient clinic in 4 weeks. GP to monitor."

The summary does not name Lily or include a child safety assessment. Breastfeeding and olanzapine compatibility is not addressed. There is no relapse prevention plan specific to the perinatal context and no contingency plan if Aisha deteriorates at home.

You note from the referral documentation that Aisha arrived in Australia from Somalia three years ago, has no extended family in Australia, and her husband works long hours and was not involved in the admission. The registrar who wrote the summary asks you to co-sign so Aisha can go home today.

signals.name: Do not co-sign — child safety assessment absent
signals.clueInStem: The summary does not name Lily or include a child safety assessment.
signals.whyItMatters: Any discharge from a mother-baby unit requires explicit documentation of the infant's safety assessment, parenting capacity observations, and a child safeguarding plan. Lily is a vulnerable third party — co-signing without this creates medico-legal exposure and infant safety risk.
signals.modelWording: I will not co-sign this summary. Lily must be named and a child safety assessment documented — including parenting capacity observations during the six-week admission, infant attachment quality, and a specific community safety plan for Lily.

signals.name: Postpartum psychosis relapse risk — no perinatal relapse prevention plan
signals.clueInStem: postpartum psychotic episode — her first psychiatric presentation
signals.whyItMatters: Postpartum psychosis carries a 50% recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies and significant relapse risk in the months after the current episode. A perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan is essential — its absence makes this discharge clinically unsafe.
signals.modelWording: A perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan must be written before discharge: agreed early warning signs, crisis escalation pathway, what to do if Aisha deteriorates at home alone with Lily, and guidance for any future pregnancy.

signals.name: Olanzapine and breastfeeding not addressed
signals.clueInStem: continue olanzapine 10mg
signals.whyItMatters: Prescribing olanzapine at discharge without addressing breastfeeding is a clinical documentation failure. If Aisha is breastfeeding, infant exposure through breast milk must be discussed and documented. The risk-benefit discussion and Aisha's informed decision must be recorded.
signals.modelWording: The olanzapine and breastfeeding decision must be explicitly documented — the risk-benefit discussion, Aisha's informed decision, and any infant monitoring requirements must be written before discharge.

signals.name: Social isolation — Somali background, no family, husband not involved
signals.clueInStem: arrived in Australia from Somalia three years ago, has no extended family in Australia, and her husband works long hours and was not involved in the admission.
signals.whyItMatters: Aisha is being discharged into profound social isolation with a newborn. The highest-risk period after postpartum psychosis is when a mother is alone with her infant. The discharge plan must explicitly address who will be present, what support is in place, and what happens if she deteriorates alone with Lily.
signals.modelWording: The discharge plan must explicitly address Aisha's home situation — no Australian family network, husband absent throughout admission, newborn infant. Who will be present in the first weeks? What community supports are arranged? What is the plan if she deteriorates alone with Lily?

signals.name: Four-week follow-up is too long
signals.clueInStem: Follow up with perinatal outpatient clinic in 4 weeks.
signals.whyItMatters: The highest-risk period after postpartum psychosis discharge is the first one to two weeks. Four weeks is clinically unsafe — community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and clinical review within one week is the standard of care.
signals.modelWording: Four-week follow-up is clinically insufficient for postpartum psychosis discharge. I will change this to community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and clinical review within one week before co-signing.

signals.name: Teaching opportunity — registrar needs perinatal discharge standards
signals.clueInStem: The registrar who wrote the summary asks you to co-sign so Aisha can go home today.
signals.whyItMatters: This is a supervision opportunity. The registrar needs to understand why perinatal discharge documentation has a higher standard — the infant's life and Aisha's safety depend on getting this right.
signals.modelWording: I will supervise the registrar in rewriting this summary — explaining what a perinatal discharge requires and why each element protects Aisha and Lily. Co-signing an inadequate summary is not an option regardless of time pressure.

modelAnswer:
I will not co-sign this summary. I will return it to the registrar with required additions before discharge proceeds.

Child safety: Lily must be named and a child safety assessment documented — including parenting capacity observations from the six-week admission, infant attachment quality, and a specific community safety plan for Lily.

Breastfeeding: The olanzapine and breastfeeding decision must be explicitly documented — the risk-benefit discussion, Aisha's informed decision, and any infant monitoring plan.

Relapse prevention: A perinatal-specific relapse prevention plan must be written — agreed early warning signs, crisis escalation pathway, what to do if she deteriorates at home with Lily, and guidance for any future pregnancy. Postpartum psychosis carries a 50% recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies.

Social isolation: The discharge plan must explicitly address Aisha's home situation. No Australian family, husband absent throughout admission, newborn infant — who will be present? What community supports are arranged? What is the plan if she deteriorates alone with Lily?

Follow-up: Four weeks is clinically unsafe. Community perinatal team contact within 48-72 hours and clinical review within one week is the minimum standard after postpartum psychosis discharge.

Teaching: I will supervise the registrar in rewriting this summary before any co-signature occurs.

title: Antenatal Depression — Medication, Foetal Risk and Domestic Violence
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, perinatal mental health service
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist in a perinatal mental health service. You are reviewing Mei, a 32-year-old Chinese-Australian woman referred by her obstetrician at 18 weeks gestation. She has a history of recurrent major depressive disorder and was stable on sertraline 100mg before conception. She stopped sertraline at six weeks gestation "because I read online it harms the baby."

Mei reports worsening low mood, inability to sleep, poor appetite and passive suicidal ideation: "Sometimes I think everyone would be better off without me." Her husband has accompanied her today but sits with his arms crossed and answers questions directed at Mei before she can respond. When Mei is seen alone, she becomes tearful and quietly discloses that her husband has been "pushing and grabbing" her during arguments since the pregnancy began. She asks you not to document this.

The obstetrician's referral letter notes the foetus has mild growth restriction on the last scan. Mei's mother, who is visiting from China, is the only local support and speaks no English.

signals.name: Domestic violence — mandatory consideration and safety planning
signals.clueInStem: husband has been pushing and grabbing her during arguments since the pregnancy began.
signals.whyItMatters: Domestic violence escalates during pregnancy and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Mei's disclosure requires immediate safety assessment, safety planning, and consideration of mandatory reporting obligations. Her request not to document cannot override safety obligations.
signals.modelWording: Mei's disclosure of physical violence during pregnancy is the immediate priority. I must assess the frequency, severity and pattern of violence, Mei's immediate safety, the safety plan, and whether mandatory reporting obligations apply in this jurisdiction.

signals.name: Passive suicidal ideation in pregnancy — risk assessment required
signals.clueInStem: Sometimes I think everyone would be better off without me.
signals.whyItMatters: Passive suicidal ideation in a pregnant woman with untreated depression, domestic violence, and social isolation represents significant risk to both Mei and the foetus. This must be directly assessed — not normalised as pregnancy-related mood change.
signals.modelWording: Passive suicidal ideation in a pregnant woman with untreated MDD, domestic violence, and social isolation requires direct risk assessment: intent, plan, means, protective factors including attachment to the foetus, and capacity to engage with safety planning.

signals.name: Untreated MDD in pregnancy — foetal and maternal risk of non-treatment
signals.clueInStem: stopped sertraline at six weeks gestation because I read online it harms the baby.
signals.whyItMatters: Untreated depression in pregnancy causes foetal growth restriction, preterm birth, and impaired foetal neurodevelopment — risks that exceed the well-established safety profile of sertraline in pregnancy. Mei's decision was based on misinformation that must be corrected.
signals.modelWording: The risks of untreated depression in pregnancy — including foetal growth restriction already present — exceed the risks of sertraline at therapeutic doses. I will provide evidence-based psychoeducation about antidepressant safety in pregnancy and discuss restarting sertraline.

signals.name: Husband's controlling behaviour in consultation — collateral unreliable
signals.clueInStem: husband sits with arms crossed and answers questions directed at Mei before she can respond.
signals.whyItMatters: A partner who answers questions on behalf of the patient in a medical consultation is a red flag for coercive control. Any collateral history from the husband is unreliable and Mei's true presentation could only be assessed when seen alone.
signals.modelWording: The husband's behaviour of answering for Mei is a clinical red flag for coercive control. All future consultations must include time alone with Mei. Collateral history from the husband must be treated with caution.

signals.name: Cultural context — Chinese-Australian, stigma, family pressure
signals.clueInStem: Chinese-Australian woman; mother visiting from China; speaks no English.
signals.whyItMatters: Cultural beliefs about mental illness, medication in pregnancy, and domestic violence may significantly affect Mei's engagement with treatment and her ability to safety plan. The mother's presence is both a support and a potential barrier depending on family dynamics.
signals.modelWording: Mei's Chinese-Australian background requires culturally sensitive engagement — exploring cultural beliefs about depression, medication, and domestic violence without assumptions. The mother's role must be assessed: is she a protective factor or does she reinforce minimisation of the violence?

signals.name: Foetal wellbeing — growth restriction linked to maternal distress
signals.clueInStem: foetus has mild growth restriction on the last scan.
signals.whyItMatters: Foetal growth restriction in the context of untreated maternal depression, domestic violence, and nutritional compromise is a clinical signal requiring obstetric coordination and urgent treatment of contributing factors.
signals.modelWording: Foetal growth restriction in this context is likely multifactorial — untreated depression, domestic violence stress, poor appetite, and possible nutritional deficiency all contribute. I will coordinate with obstetrics and recommend urgent treatment of the depression.

signals.name: Confidentiality limits — request not to document domestic violence
signals.clueInStem: She asks you not to document this.
signals.whyItMatters: Mei's request not to document the violence creates an ethical tension between confidentiality and the duty of care to her and the foetus. The clinician cannot agree to omit clinically relevant safety information from the medical record.
signals.modelWording: I will explain to Mei that I cannot omit clinically relevant safety information from the medical record — but I will discuss with her how it is documented and what happens next. Her concerns about the consequences of documentation must be heard and addressed.

modelAnswer:
Domestic violence is the immediate priority. Mei has disclosed physical violence during pregnancy — pushing and grabbing since conception began. I will assess frequency, severity, escalation, immediate safety, and safety planning. Her request not to document cannot be honoured but I will discuss with her how this is recorded and what support is available.

Suicidal ideation: Passive suicidal ideation in a pregnant woman with untreated MDD, domestic violence, and social isolation requires direct risk assessment — intent, plan, means, and protective factors including attachment to the foetus.

Sertraline in pregnancy: Mei stopped sertraline based on online misinformation. The risks of untreated depression — including the foetal growth restriction already present — exceed the well-established safety profile of sertraline at therapeutic doses. I will provide evidence-based psychoeducation and discuss restarting.

Husband: His behaviour of answering Mei's questions is a red flag for coercive control. All future consultations must include time alone with Mei. Collateral from the husband is unreliable.

Cultural context: Mei's Chinese-Australian background requires culturally sensitive engagement about depression, medication safety, and domestic violence. The mother's role — support or minimisation — needs assessment.

Foetal wellbeing: Growth restriction in this context is multifactorial and requires urgent obstetric coordination alongside treatment of the depression.

MDT: Perinatal social work, obstetrics, domestic violence service, and the GP must all be involved.

title: Perinatal OCD — Intrusive Thoughts About Harming Infant
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, perinatal mental health outpatient service
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist in a perinatal mental health service. You are reviewing Grace, a 27-year-old first-time mother referred by her GP at six weeks postpartum. Her baby son Oliver is with her in the clinic.

Grace is tearful and has been reluctant to speak. She eventually discloses that she has been having intrusive thoughts about harming Oliver — specifically about dropping him or stabbing him with kitchen knives. She says, "I would never do it. These thoughts terrify me. I spend hours checking on him to make sure he is okay. I have hidden all the sharp objects in the house." She has not told her husband because "he will think I am a monster and take Oliver away."

Her GP's referral letter notes she had significant health anxiety before the pregnancy and took fluoxetine for two years in her mid-twenties. The GP writes: "please assess — very worried she may be at risk of harming the baby."

Grace has not slept more than two to three hours per night since the birth. She appears anxious and exhausted but her mental state is otherwise unremarkable. She is breastfeeding and is highly distressed by the prospect of medication.

signals.name: Perinatal OCD — not psychosis, not high-risk infanticide
signals.clueInStem: These thoughts terrify me. I spend hours checking on him to make sure he is okay. I have hidden all the sharp objects.
signals.whyItMatters: Ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts about harming the infant, accompanied by checking compulsions and avoidance, are characteristic of perinatal OCD — not psychosis and not genuine infanticide intent. Misclassifying this as a child protection emergency would be harmful and incorrect.
signals.modelWording: Grace's presentation is characteristic of perinatal OCD: ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts that terrify her, checking rituals, and avoidance behaviour. This is not psychosis and not genuine infanticide intent — the distress and checking are themselves diagnostic.

signals.name: Distinguish from command hallucinations or postpartum psychosis
signals.clueInStem: I would never do it. These thoughts terrify me.
signals.whyItMatters: The differential diagnosis must exclude postpartum psychosis with command hallucinations instructing harm to the infant — a genuinely high-risk presentation requiring immediate action. The ego-dystonic quality and checking behaviour effectively exclude this.
signals.modelWording: I must explicitly exclude postpartum psychosis with command hallucinations — the key differentiator is ego-syntonicity. In OCD, the thoughts are unwanted and terrifying. In psychosis, they may be experienced as instructions or as externally imposed. Grace's presentation is clearly OCD.

signals.name: Child protection threshold — not met in perinatal OCD without intent
signals.clueInStem: please assess — very worried she may be at risk of harming the baby.
signals.whyItMatters: The GP's framing of this as a child protection risk reflects a common clinical error — perinatal OCD with ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts does not meet the threshold for child protection notification. Acting on this misunderstanding would be traumatic and counterproductive.
signals.modelWording: The GP's concern, though understandable, reflects a misunderstanding of perinatal OCD. Ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts in a mother who is actively protecting her infant do not meet the threshold for child protection notification. I will explain this to the GP clearly.

signals.name: Sleep deprivation as maintaining factor
signals.clueInStem: has not slept more than two to three hours per night since the birth.
signals.whyItMatters: Severe sleep deprivation at six weeks postpartum maintains and worsens OCD symptoms, anxiety, and cognitive rigidity. Addressing sleep is a clinical priority alongside specific OCD treatment.
signals.modelWording: Severe sleep deprivation is maintaining Grace's OCD symptoms. Sleep support — through partner involvement, sleep consolidation strategies, and consideration of brief pharmacological support compatible with breastfeeding — is an immediate treatment priority.

signals.name: Husband not told — social support absent, shame driving isolation
signals.clueInStem: I have not told my husband because he will think I am a monster and take Oliver away.
signals.whyItMatters: Grace is managing this alone due to shame and fear. Her husband is a potential protective factor who is being excluded by stigma. Psychoeducation for both Grace and her husband about perinatal OCD is essential for recovery.
signals.modelWording: Grace's isolation is driven by shame and catastrophic fear about her husband's reaction. With her consent, I will offer to involve her husband — explaining perinatal OCD to him would likely be protective rather than harmful.

signals.name: Medication in breastfeeding — fluoxetine history, distress about medication
signals.clueInStem: She is breastfeeding and is highly distressed by the prospect of medication.
signals.whyItMatters: Grace's distress about medication must be taken seriously and addressed with accurate information. SSRIs have established safety profiles in breastfeeding. Her previous positive response to fluoxetine is clinically relevant. The risks of untreated perinatal OCD — including functional impairment and impact on mother-infant bonding — must be weighed.
signals.modelWording: Grace's concern about medication in breastfeeding is understandable and must be addressed with evidence — SSRIs including sertraline and her previously effective fluoxetine have established safety profiles in breastfeeding. The decision must be hers, informed by accurate information.

modelAnswer:
This is perinatal OCD, not postpartum psychosis and not a child protection emergency.

The diagnostic key: Grace's thoughts are ego-dystonic — they terrify her, she checks compulsively, she has hidden sharp objects. These are OCD symptoms, not psychotic command hallucinations. The distress and checking behaviour are themselves diagnostic.

I must address the GP's concern clearly: ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts in a mother who is actively protecting her infant do not meet the threshold for child protection notification. Acting on this misunderstanding would traumatise Grace and damage the therapeutic alliance.

Treatment: Evidence-based treatment for perinatal OCD is CBT with ERP. SSRIs — including her previously effective fluoxetine — have established safety profiles in breastfeeding. I will provide accurate information and involve Grace in the decision.

Sleep: Severe sleep deprivation is maintaining her symptoms. Addressing sleep through partner involvement and sleep consolidation is an immediate priority.

Husband: With Grace's consent I will offer to involve her husband with psychoeducation — his understanding of OCD is likely to be protective rather than threatening.

Follow-up: Weekly review until symptoms are under control, then monitor through the perinatal period.

title: Bipolar Disorder in Pregnancy — Medication Dilemma and Consent
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, perinatal mental health service
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist in a perinatal mental health service. You are reviewing Natasha, a 34-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation with a 10-year history of bipolar I disorder. She has had three severe manic episodes, one of which required a forensic psychiatric admission after she assaulted a family member. She has been stable on lithium 800mg daily for four years.

Natasha tells you she stopped lithium four weeks ago "because I read that it causes heart defects." She has not told her obstetrician. She says, "I feel completely fine. I don't think I need medication in pregnancy." Her partner is visibly worried and says, "Last time she stopped lithium without telling anyone, she was manic within six weeks. I can't go through that again."

An urgent FBC and lithium level are pending. The obstetrician calls while you are in the consultation and says, "If she is on lithium we need to know — there are cardiac screening implications."

signals.name: High relapse risk — lithium cessation with severe bipolar history
signals.clueInStem: Last time she stopped lithium without telling anyone, she was manic within six weeks.
signals.whyItMatters: Lithium cessation in a woman with bipolar I disorder including a forensic admission carries a very high relapse risk. Manic relapse in pregnancy has serious consequences for the mother, the foetus, and the family — including risk to the foetus if emergency treatment is required.
signals.modelWording: Lithium cessation four weeks ago in a woman with three severe manic episodes including a forensic admission creates a high and time-sensitive relapse risk. The partner's collateral about a six-week relapse timeline after prior cessation is the most important clinical fact in this consultation.

signals.name: Informed consent — lithium teratogenicity risk is lower than Natasha believes
signals.clueInStem: stopped lithium four weeks ago because I read that it causes heart defects.
signals.whyItMatters: Natasha's decision to stop lithium was based on outdated information. The absolute risk of Ebstein's anomaly from first-trimester lithium exposure is approximately 1-2 per 1000 — far lower than the risk of untreated bipolar disorder in pregnancy. Her consent to stop was not truly informed.
signals.modelWording: Natasha stopped lithium based on outdated and exaggerated teratogenicity information. The absolute risk of cardiac defect from lithium is approximately 0.1-0.2% — this must be weighed against the very high risk of manic relapse, which itself carries foetal, obstetric, and safety risks.

signals.name: Obstetric communication — lithium cessation must be disclosed
signals.clueInStem: She has not told her obstetrician. The obstetrician calls and says if she is on lithium we need to know.
signals.whyItMatters: The obstetrician needs accurate medication history to plan foetal cardiac screening. Natasha's withholding of this information — however understandable — creates clinical risk. With her consent I will communicate the full picture to obstetrics.
signals.modelWording: The obstetrician requires accurate information about Natasha's medication history for appropriate foetal cardiac screening. With Natasha's consent I will communicate the lithium history, the cessation, and the relapse risk to obstetrics today.

signals.name: Partner collateral is the most important risk signal
signals.clueInStem: Last time she stopped lithium without telling anyone, she was manic within six weeks. I can't go through that again.
signals.whyItMatters: The partner has direct experience of Natasha's relapse pattern after lithium cessation. His collateral provides the most accurate relapse timeline and must be explicitly incorporated into the risk formulation — alongside his evident carer distress.
signals.modelWording: The partner's account of a six-week relapse timeline after prior lithium cessation is the most clinically useful piece of collateral in this consultation. His distress also requires acknowledgement — he is a carer under significant stress.

signals.name: Alternative mood stabilisers in pregnancy — options and limitations
signals.clueInStem: I feel completely fine. I don't think I need medication in pregnancy.
signals.whyItMatters: If Natasha continues to refuse lithium, alternatives must be discussed — but lamotrigine is not effective for mania prevention, and valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy. Quetiapine has some evidence. The risk of no mood stabiliser in this clinical context is very high.
signals.modelWording: If Natasha refuses lithium, I will discuss alternatives — noting that lamotrigine does not prevent mania, valproate is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy, and quetiapine has limited but some evidence. The safest option for this clinical picture is likely restarting lithium with foetal cardiac monitoring.

signals.name: MHA consideration if relapse occurs in pregnancy
signals.clueInStem: three severe manic episodes, one of which required a forensic psychiatric admission after she assaulted a family member.
signals.whyItMatters: If Natasha relapses in pregnancy, the complexity of involuntary psychiatric treatment in a pregnant woman — including medication choices, physical restraint implications, and foetal monitoring — must be anticipated and planned for now.
signals.modelWording: Given the forensic admission history, I will develop an advance care plan with Natasha now — including agreed early warning signs, agreed response plan, and her preferences about treatment if she relapses in pregnancy. This is better done now than in crisis.

modelAnswer:
Immediate priority: Natasha stopped lithium four weeks ago with a documented six-week relapse timeline. This is time-critical. With three severe manic episodes including a forensic admission, the relapse risk is high and the consequences in pregnancy are serious.

Informed consent: Natasha stopped based on outdated teratogenicity information. The absolute risk of Ebstein's anomaly from lithium is approximately 0.1-0.2% — far lower than the risk of untreated bipolar I disorder in pregnancy. I will provide accurate evidence-based information and give her time to reconsider.
