Obstetric communication: With Natasha's consent I will communicate the full picture to obstetrics today — the lithium history, the cessation, and the relapse risk — so appropriate foetal cardiac screening can be planned.

Partner collateral: The partner's six-week relapse timeline is the most important clinical fact. His distress also requires acknowledgement.

Alternatives: If Natasha refuses lithium, I will discuss alternatives — noting that lamotrigine does not prevent mania, valproate is contraindicated, and quetiapine has limited evidence. Restarting lithium with foetal cardiac monitoring is the safest option for this clinical picture.

Advance care plan: I will develop a crisis plan with Natasha now — agreed early warning signs, agreed response, and her preferences if she relapses. This is better done now than in an acute manic episode at 28 weeks.

title: Elderly patient refusing dialysis
setting: Renal ward in a tertiary hospital
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist asked to urgently review Mr Howard, an 81-year-old man with vascular dementia, chronic kidney disease and recurrent delirium, admitted with fluid overload and hyperkalaemia. The renal team recommends urgent dialysis, but Mr Howard is refusing and repeatedly pulling at his cannula.

Mr Howard says, "The dialysis machine is a punishment device and you are trying to drain me dry." He cannot explain what potassium is or what may happen without dialysis. At times he recognises his daughter but later asks why his deceased wife has not visited.

His daughter holds enduring power of attorney and says, "Dad always said he never wanted machines keeping him alive." His son disagrees and says, "You have to do everything or I'll complain." The renal consultant says dialysis is needed tonight, but the ward team is unsure whether to proceed against Mr Howard's objection.

signals.name: Decision-specific capacity impaired by cognition and beliefs
signals.clueInStem: The dialysis machine is a punishment device and you are trying to drain me dry.
signals.whyItMatters: Delusional or confused beliefs may impair appreciation and weighing of dialysis risks; missing this may lead to wrongly treating refusal as capacitous.
signals.modelWording: Assess decision-specific capacity to refuse dialysis, focusing on understanding, retention, appreciation and weighing of the immediate medical risks.

signals.name: Immediate life-threatening physical risk
signals.clueInStem: dialysis is needed tonight
signals.whyItMatters: Urgent hyperkalaemia may require emergency treatment authority rather than prolonged family debate.
signals.modelWording: Clarify medical urgency with renal team because imminent risk from hyperkalaemia may justify emergency treatment if capacity is absent.

signals.name: Substitute decision-maker conflict
signals.clueInStem: Dad always said he never wanted machines keeping him alive.
signals.whyItMatters: Past wishes are relevant but must be interpreted in context of current reversibility, urgency and legal authority.
signals.modelWording: Clarify the daughter's legal authority and explore prior expressed wishes while distinguishing chronic end-of-life preferences from reversible emergency treatment.

signals.name: Possible delirium causing fluctuating capacity
signals.clueInStem: later asks why his deceased wife has not visited.
signals.whyItMatters: Delirium can fluctuate and may temporarily remove capacity, requiring reassessment after medical stabilisation.
signals.modelWording: Assess for delirium superimposed on dementia because fluctuating cognition may be driving refusal and behavioural disturbance.

signals.name: Least restrictive approach to treatment
signals.clueInStem: pulling at his cannula
signals.whyItMatters: Treatment against objection may require sedation or restraint; consultant must minimise harm and coercion.
signals.modelWording: Use the least restrictive method to deliver urgent care, including reassurance, family presence, analgesia and restraint only if necessary and proportionate.

signals.name: Documentation of defensible legal reasoning
signals.clueInStem: I'll complain.
signals.whyItMatters: Family dispute increases medico-legal risk and requires clear documentation of capacity, urgency and legal basis.
signals.modelWording: Document capacity findings, medical urgency, substitute decision-maker discussions and legal rationale for proceeding or not proceeding.

modelAnswer:
Mr Howard's refusal of dialysis cannot be treated as a simple autonomous refusal, because the stem shows several reasons why his decision-making is likely impaired and time-critical. The phrase "The dialysis machine is a punishment device and you are trying to drain me dry" is not merely an eccentric way of describing treatment; it suggests that his refusal is being shaped by confused or persecutory beliefs about the nature and purpose of dialysis. He also "cannot explain what potassium is or what may happen without dialysis," which means the assessment must focus on decision-specific capacity to refuse dialysis, especially understanding, retention, appreciation and weighing of the immediate risk. I would not ask the renal team to accept or override the refusal until I had made that capacity judgement in relation to this specific decision at this specific time.

The medical urgency matters because the renal consultant says dialysis is "needed tonight." In a patient admitted with fluid overload and hyperkalaemia, delay may carry a risk of arrhythmia, death or irreversible harm. I would therefore immediately clarify with the renal team the exact urgency, alternatives to dialysis, likely outcome if delayed, whether temporising measures are possible, and whether there is time to optimise Mr Howard's cognition before treatment. The capacity assessment cannot be conducted in isolation from the medical facts, because a person may need a different level of appreciation to refuse a treatment that is optional next week compared with one that may prevent death tonight.

I would also assess for delirium superimposed on dementia, because the stem states that at times he recognises his daughter but "later asks why his deceased wife has not visited." That fluctuation strongly suggests an acute confusional component, especially in a medically unwell older man. If delirium is contributing, I would ask the medical team to address reversible precipitants such as infection, uraemia, electrolyte disturbance, pain, constipation, hypoxia and medication effects, while recognising that the emergency may not allow prolonged delay. Capacity may fluctuate, but if dialysis is required tonight and he currently lacks capacity, the team needs a lawful emergency or substitute decision pathway rather than waiting passively.

The family conflict must be managed but not allowed to replace the capacity assessment. The daughter holds enduring power of attorney and says, "Dad always said he never wanted machines keeping him alive." That is clinically and ethically relevant, but I would explore whether that prior statement referred to burdensome long-term life support in an end-of-life context or to potentially reversible emergency dialysis in acute renal deterioration. The son says, "You have to do everything or I'll complain." That increases medico-legal pressure but does not alter the clinical or legal test. I would meet or speak with both children, clarify the daughter's legal authority and the scope of the EPOA, explain the medical urgency, and make clear that the decision will be based on Mr Howard's capacity, best interests or substituted judgement under the relevant law, not on whichever family member applies most pressure.

If Mr Howard lacks capacity and dialysis is immediately necessary to prevent serious harm, I would advise the team to proceed under the appropriate emergency treatment or substitute decision framework in the jurisdiction, while using the least restrictive and most dignified method possible. The clue that he is "repeatedly pulling at his cannula" means coercion, restraint or sedation may become part of the practical treatment question, not just the legal one. I would first attempt explanation in simple language, a calm environment, familiar staff or daughter presence if helpful, attention to pain and distress, and de-escalation. If restraint or sedation is required to deliver life-saving treatment, it should be proportionate, time-limited, monitored, documented and reviewed, not used simply because the ward is frustrated.

My role as consultant is to make the reasoning explicit and defensible under pressure. I would document the exact capacity findings, the medical urgency behind "dialysis is needed tonight," the delirium features, the discussion with the EPOA daughter and disagreeing son, the legal basis for proceeding or not proceeding, the least restrictive measures attempted, and the risks of both treatment and non-treatment. The son's threat "I'll complain" should prompt better documentation and communication, not defensive medicine. The ethically correct position is to respect capacitous refusal if present, but if capacity is absent and the risk is imminent, to act lawfully and proportionately to prevent avoidable death while still honouring Mr Howard's dignity and previously expressed values as far as possible.

title: Adolescent refusing admission after overdose
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, child and adolescent consultation-liaison service
stem:
You are supervising a Stage 2 registrar assessing Leila, a 16-year-old boarding school student brought to ED after ingesting an uncertain quantity of fluoxetine and paracetamol. She initially denied overdose but later told nursing staff, "I took enough to sleep and maybe not wake up."

Leila refuses blood tests, NAC treatment and mental health admission. She says, "I understand my liver could fail, but that is my choice." She becomes angry when staff contact her aunt and says, "No one has the right to lock me up." Her teacher has returned to the boarding school, and her aunt says Leila is "dramatic but clever" and should be discharged if she promises to be safe.

The ED consultant is concerned because the timing of ingestion is unclear. The registrar asks whether Leila is Gillick competent because she is articulate and can repeat the medical risks.

signals.name: Apparent understanding does not equal capacity
signals.clueInStem: I understand my liver could fail, but that is my choice.
signals.whyItMatters: A suicidal adolescent may repeat information but be unable to weigh consequences due to hopelessness, ambivalence or acute crisis.
signals.modelWording: Assess Gillick competence/capacity by testing appreciation and weighing, not merely factual repetition of risk.

signals.name: Unclear overdose timing requires urgent medical treatment
signals.clueInStem: the timing of ingestion is unclear.
signals.whyItMatters: Delayed paracetamol treatment may cause hepatic failure; medical stabilisation cannot wait for a perfect psychiatric assessment.
signals.modelWording: Prioritise urgent toxicology assessment and NAC where indicated because uncertain paracetamol timing creates potentially life-threatening risk.

signals.name: Suicidal intent and concealment
signals.clueInStem: I took enough to sleep and maybe not wake up.
signals.whyItMatters: Ambivalent suicidal intent and initial denial increase concern about ongoing risk and unreliable self-report.
signals.modelWording: Formulate risk based on ambivalent suicidal intent, concealment, overdose uncertainty and current refusal of treatment.

signals.name: MHA/minor treatment pathway
signals.clueInStem: No one has the right to lock me up.
signals.whyItMatters: Refusal of life-saving care may require use of mental health, guardianship or emergency medical powers depending on jurisdiction.
signals.modelWording: Consider applicable minor consent, emergency treatment and Mental Health Act pathways if acute mental disorder impairs safe refusal.

signals.name: Collateral minimisation
signals.clueInStem: dramatic but clever
signals.whyItMatters: Family minimisation may lead to unsafe discharge unless balanced with objective risk and school collateral.
signals.modelWording: Seek broader collateral from school, family and GP while not relying on minimising collateral to justify discharge.

signals.name: Discharge pressure without supervision
signals.clueInStem: should be discharged if she promises to be safe.
signals.whyItMatters: A safety promise is not a discharge plan, especially after overdose and treatment refusal.
signals.modelWording: Do not rely on a promise of safety; disposition requires medical clearance, risk formulation, supervision and follow-up.

modelAnswer:
Leila's presentation should be managed as a medically dangerous overdose with an unreliable refusal, not as a straightforward question of whether an articulate 16-year-old can repeat information. The registrar's question about Gillick competence is appropriate, but the conclusion cannot be based on the fact that she is articulate. Her statement, "I understand my liver could fail, but that is my choice," shows factual repetition of risk, but capacity in this context requires more than repeating the words "liver could fail." I would assess whether she can appreciate that this risk applies personally to her, weigh survival against the wish not to wake up, and make a stable decision not driven by acute suicidal crisis, shame, fear or hopelessness.

The medical issue is urgent because "the timing of ingestion is unclear." With an uncertain paracetamol overdose, delayed blood tests or delayed NAC can result in preventable hepatic failure. I would therefore advise the ED team that medical stabilisation and toxicology management must proceed in parallel with psychiatric assessment. I would not allow the search for a perfect capacity opinion to delay time-critical treatment. The immediate actions are safe observation, prevention of absconding, toxicology advice, paracetamol level and relevant bloods, NAC if indicated, and senior ED/paediatric involvement. If she refuses these interventions and lacks capacity, or if emergency treatment law applies because of imminent serious harm, the team should use the appropriate legal pathway to provide life-saving care.

Her risk cannot be minimised because she later told nursing staff, "I took enough to sleep and maybe not wake up." That phrase is clinically important because it shows at least ambivalent suicidal intent after an initial denial of overdose. Initial concealment followed by partial disclosure makes self-report less reliable, and her current refusal of treatment may be part of the same wish not to survive. I would assess intent, planning, access to further medication, triggers, shame, bullying or abuse, family conflict, mood symptoms, substance use, prior self-harm, protective factors and her ability to collaborate with safety. I would also clarify whether the boarding school context creates isolation, access to medication, academic pressure or family separation issues.

The legal tension is between respecting the developing autonomy of a 16-year-old and protecting her from a state-dependent refusal following a potentially lethal overdose. Her anger that "No one has the right to lock me up" should be acknowledged, not dismissed, because coercion can damage trust. However, if her ability to weigh information is impaired by suicidal intent or acute mental disorder, then her refusal of blood tests, NAC and admission may not be capacitous. Depending on the jurisdiction, the team may need to consider Gillick competence, parental or guardian involvement, emergency medical treatment powers, child protection or Mental Health Act assessment. I would supervise the registrar to frame this as lawful least-restrictive protection, not punishment.

Collateral needs to be broadened because the aunt's view that Leila is "dramatic but clever" and should be discharged if she promises to be safe is not an adequate safety assessment. A promise of safety is especially weak when the patient has taken an uncertain overdose, initially denied it and is refusing treatment. I would obtain collateral from the aunt, school, GP, parents or guardians where appropriate, and any prior mental health providers, while considering Leila's confidentiality and safety. The teacher has returned to the boarding school, which means the immediate support structure may be thin; I would clarify who can actually supervise her tonight and whether discharge would return her to a safe environment.

I would not discharge Leila simply because she can repeat medical risks or promises to be safe. Disposition requires medical clearance, completion of necessary overdose treatment, a defensible suicide-risk formulation, a safe adult supervision plan, means restriction, communication with guardians or statutory supports as required, and CAMHS follow-up or admission depending on ongoing risk. If she continues to refuse life-saving treatment and the assessment indicates impaired capacity, I would support proportionate treatment under the relevant emergency or mental health legal framework. My documentation would explicitly state why "I understand my liver could fail" did or did not amount to capacity, why the unclear ingestion timing changed urgency, how her statement "maybe not wake up" affected suicide-risk formulation, what collateral was obtained, and why the final disposition was the least restrictive safe option.

title: CTO renewal and least restrictive care
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist reviewing a community treatment order renewal for Sam, a 37-year-old man with schizophrenia and past relapses associated with stopping depot antipsychotic medication. He has been stable for 18 months on a CTO, lives independently and works part-time.

Sam says, "I only take the injection because you people threaten me with hospital." He reports tiredness, sexual dysfunction and weight gain. His case manager supports renewal because "he will definitely relapse if we remove it." Sam's sister says he has been reliable with appointments and has not used cannabis for over a year.

The registrar asks whether renewal is justified because Sam still lacks insight and says he would prefer tablets. The tribunal hearing is in one week, and the team has not documented any recent attempt at a voluntary treatment plan.

signals.name: Least restrictive principle central to CTO renewal
signals.clueInStem: the team has not documented any recent attempt at a voluntary treatment plan.
signals.whyItMatters: A CTO must not be renewed by inertia; failure to test voluntary care undermines legal defensibility.
signals.modelWording: Review whether ongoing CTO is the least restrictive option and document attempts to negotiate voluntary treatment.

signals.name: Coercion affecting alliance and consent
signals.clueInStem: I only take the injection because you people threaten me with hospital.
signals.whyItMatters: Perceived coercion may damage engagement and requires explicit consideration of autonomy and alternatives.
signals.modelWording: Explore perceived coercion and seek collaborative consent to treatment where possible despite CTO history.

signals.name: Relapse history relevant but not sufficient
signals.clueInStem: past relapses associated with stopping depot antipsychotic medication.
signals.whyItMatters: Historical relapse risk is relevant, but current functioning and supports must also be weighed.
signals.modelWording: Balance historical relapse risk against current stability, adherence, supports and willingness to consider alternatives.

signals.name: Medication side effects driving refusal
signals.clueInStem: tiredness, sexual dysfunction and weight gain.
signals.whyItMatters: Ignoring adverse effects increases resistance and weakens least restrictive treatment planning.
signals.modelWording: Address side effects and consider dose, medication or formulation changes to improve voluntary adherence.

signals.name: Collateral supports possible step-down
signals.clueInStem: reliable with appointments
signals.whyItMatters: Reliable engagement and abstinence may support a trial of less restrictive care.
signals.modelWording: Use collateral about sustained appointment attendance and cannabis abstinence to assess feasibility of voluntary treatment.

signals.name: Team inertia and risk aversion
signals.clueInStem: he will definitely relapse if we remove it.
signals.whyItMatters: Overconfident prediction may reflect team risk aversion rather than evidence-based legal reasoning.
signals.modelWording: Challenge risk-averse assumptions and require evidence-based justification for ongoing coercion.

modelAnswer:
Sam's CTO renewal should not proceed by habit or defensive practice, because the stem contains strong evidence that the least restrictive question has not been properly tested. He has been "stable for 18 months," lives independently and works part-time, and his sister reports that he has been "reliable with appointments" and abstinent from cannabis for over a year. Those facts do not prove that the CTO should end, but they make automatic renewal legally and ethically weak. The most important governance clue is that "the team has not documented any recent attempt at a voluntary treatment plan." A CTO is coercive treatment in the community, and the tribunal will expect evidence that less restrictive alternatives have been actively considered rather than assumed to fail.

Sam's own words, "I only take the injection because you people threaten me with hospital," are central. They show that the treatment may be technically effective but experienced as coercive and alienating. I would explore this directly with him rather than treating it as simply poor insight. I would ask what he understands about his illness, what he thinks happened during previous relapses, what he fears about depot medication, what he would accept voluntarily, and what early warning signs or relapse plan he would agree to. The aim is not to pretend there is no relapse risk, but to see whether a collaborative plan can manage that risk without ongoing compulsion.

The relapse history remains relevant because the stem states there were "past relapses associated with stopping depot antipsychotic medication." A consultant-level answer must not swing from coercion to naïve autonomy. I would review the severity of those relapses, whether there was violence, self-neglect, hospitalisation, substance use, time to relapse, response to treatment and whether he had capacity during relapse. Historical risk is part of the statutory and clinical formulation. However, historical risk alone is not sufficient if current circumstances have changed and if a monitored voluntary plan is feasible. The team must weigh past relapse against current stability, work, abstinence, appointment adherence, supports and Sam's willingness to consider tablets.

His side effects also need to be treated as legitimate clinical issues, not obstacles to compliance. He reports "tiredness, sexual dysfunction and weight gain," all of which can erode quality of life, autonomy, physical health and engagement. Ignoring these side effects would make the CTO less defensible because it would suggest the service is imposing treatment without actively reducing burden. I would consider dose review, metabolic monitoring, sexual side-effect management, alternative antipsychotic options, depot versus oral formulation, and a negotiated trial of oral medication if the risk can be managed. If a CTO is still recommended, I would explain why the benefits outweigh these burdens and what is being done to reduce them.

The case manager's statement that "he will definitely relapse if we remove it" needs careful handling. It may reflect genuine concern, but the certainty and the phrase "definitely" also suggest risk aversion and fear of blame. I would lead an MDT review before the tribunal, asking the team to identify evidence rather than predictions: what exactly happened previously, what has changed, what early warning signs can be monitored, what supports exist, and what contingency plan would trigger rapid review. The ethical tension is between preventing relapse and avoiding unnecessary coercion. A consultant has to hold both, not simply choose the least risky option for the service.

My tribunal position would depend on this updated assessment. If the statutory criteria remain met because relapse risk is severe, insight is poor, voluntary adherence is not realistic and consequences of relapse are serious, I would recommend renewal but only with a clear plan to address side effects and move toward voluntary care. If the evidence supports a less restrictive plan, I would recommend step-down or non-renewal with intensive voluntary follow-up, relapse plan, sister involvement if Sam agrees, and rapid re-entry pathway. In either case, I would not tell the registrar that poor insight alone is enough. My documentation would record Sam's views, the coercion concern, side effects, relapse history, sister's collateral, voluntary alternatives considered, least restrictive reasoning and the precise rationale for whatever recommendation is made to the tribunal.

title: Psychotic patient refusing antipsychotic treatment
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist responsible for an acute inpatient unit.

Omar is a 28-year-old man admitted involuntarily after threatening neighbours with a metal pole. He has persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. He refuses antipsychotic medication, saying, "The tablets are trackers that will let them control my thoughts." He accepts food and physical observations but refuses all psychiatric medication.

His father says Omar became unwell after stopping risperidone depot three months ago. The registrar asks whether Omar's refusal means he has capacity because he can clearly say "no." Nursing staff report escalating intimidation of other patients, including standing over an elderly patient and saying, "You are one of them."

The unit is full, and staff are asking whether medication can be given by force tonight.

signals.name: Refusal driven by psychotic belief
signals.clueInStem: The tablets are trackers that will let them control my thoughts.
signals.whyItMatters: A clear refusal does not equal capacity if delusions impair appreciation and reasoning.
signals.modelWording: Assess whether persecutory delusions impair Omar's capacity to refuse antipsychotic treatment.

signals.name: Risk to others on ward
signals.clueInStem: You are one of them.
signals.whyItMatters: Threatening behaviour toward vulnerable inpatients increases urgency for containment and treatment.
signals.modelWording: Assess immediate risk to others, especially vulnerable patients, and adjust observation and containment accordingly.

signals.name: Mental Health Act treatment authority
signals.clueInStem: admitted involuntarily
signals.whyItMatters: Involuntary status may permit treatment under statutory criteria but does not remove need for proportionality and documentation.
signals.modelWording: Clarify statutory authority for involuntary treatment and ensure treatment is necessary, proportionate and least restrictive.

signals.name: Collateral evidence of relapse after depot cessation
signals.clueInStem: stopping risperidone depot three months ago.
signals.whyItMatters: Collateral links non-adherence to relapse and informs treatment choice and relapse planning.
signals.modelWording: Use collateral regarding depot cessation and relapse to support formulation and treatment planning.

signals.name: Force medication threshold
signals.clueInStem: medication can be given by force tonight.
signals.whyItMatters: Forced medication is high-risk and should not be driven by staff anxiety alone.
signals.modelWording: Use forced medication only if legally authorised, clinically necessary, proportionate and after less restrictive options fail.

signals.name: System pressure should not drive coercion
signals.clueInStem: The unit is full
signals.whyItMatters: Bed pressure must not become the rationale for coercive treatment.
signals.modelWording: Acknowledge unit pressure but base treatment decisions on clinical risk, legal authority and necessity.

modelAnswer:
Omar's refusal of antipsychotic medication should be taken seriously, but it should not be mistaken for capacitous refusal simply because he can clearly say "no." The registrar's question is a common but dangerous simplification. The key stem clue is Omar's explanation that "The tablets are trackers that will let them control my thoughts." That is not a reasoned refusal based on side effects, past values or a balanced understanding of treatment. It strongly suggests that persecutory delusions are impairing his appreciation of what the medication is and his ability to weigh risks and benefits. I would therefore assess capacity specifically for refusing antipsychotic treatment, focusing on whether psychosis prevents him from understanding the nature and purpose of medication, the consequences of refusal, and the relationship between treatment and his current admission.

The urgency is increased because risk to others is already active on the ward. Omar was admitted involuntarily after "threatening neighbours with a metal pole," and nursing staff now report that he is standing over an elderly patient and saying, "You are one of them." That phrase suggests that his persecutory beliefs are being applied to vulnerable inpatients, which increases risk of defensive or pre-emptive aggression. I would not wait for a physical assault before acting. My immediate actions would be to review him urgently, increase observation, separate him from vulnerable patients where possible, reduce stimulation, ensure staff safety, and create a clear ward risk plan. The elderly patient and other inpatients are vulnerable third parties, so the decision is not only about Omar's autonomy.

Because he is "admitted involuntarily," I would clarify the statutory authority for treatment under the local Mental Health Act. Involuntary status may permit treatment for mental illness, but it does not remove the need for clinical necessity, proportionality, least restrictive practice and correct procedure. I would check whether additional second opinions, treatment authorisations, capacity documentation, medication forms or senior approvals are required locally. I would also ensure the team understands that forced medication is not justified merely because the unit is under pressure or staff are anxious. The question is whether medication is legally authorised, clinically necessary to treat mental illness and manage risk, and whether less restrictive options have been attempted.

I would use the father's collateral that Omar became unwell after "stopping risperidone depot three months ago" to build the treatment formulation, not as a substitute for Omar's current assessment. It suggests a relapse pattern after depot cessation and may inform medication choice, but I would still ask about prior response, side effects, reasons for stopping, physical health, substance use, and whether depot treatment remains appropriate. A consultant-level plan should not simply restart the old depot by force; it should consider therapeutic engagement, oral options, side-effect concerns, and a longer-term relapse-prevention strategy.

The team is asking whether "medication can be given by force tonight," and this is the ethical hinge. I would first attempt oral medication with a clear, non-argumentative explanation, offering choices where possible and avoiding confrontation with the delusion. If Omar refuses and continues to pose serious risk to others or is deteriorating, involuntary medication may be justified if the legal criteria are met. If physical restraint or IM medication is required, it must be the least restrictive effective intervention, properly staffed, physically monitored and documented. It should never be framed as punishment for refusal or as a way to make a full unit easier to manage.

The phrase "The unit is full" is relevant to operational stress but must not drive coercion. I would acknowledge staff strain while explicitly basing the decision on Omar's psychosis, impaired capacity, treatment need and risk to vulnerable patients. I would lead an MDT discussion so nursing staff, registrar and security understand the agreed plan, observation level, de-escalation approach, medication pathway and review timetable. My documentation would record the delusional basis of refusal, capacity reasoning, risk to others, legal authority, less restrictive attempts, collateral from father, rationale for any forced treatment, monitoring arrangements and review plan. That is what makes the decision clinically and legally defensible.

title: Dementia patient refusing residential care
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, old age psychiatry service
stem:
You are the old age psychiatry consultant asked to assess Mrs Bell, a 77-year-old woman with Alzheimer's disease admitted after being found wandering at night in heavy rain. OT assessment shows she leaves the stove on, misses medications and cannot manage finances.

Mrs Bell insists on returning home and says, "I have lived there for fifty years and I am not going to a prison." She cannot recall being found by police and repeatedly asks when her husband is coming, although he died six years ago. Her daughter wants residential care, but her son says, "She has a right to make bad decisions."

The geriatrician believes discharge home alone is unsafe. Social work says guardianship may be required, but the family wants a decision today because the residential care bed may be lost.

signals.name: Accommodation capacity impaired by memory and appreciation
signals.clueInStem: She cannot recall being found by police
signals.whyItMatters: Inability to retain and appreciate recent risk events undermines capacity for accommodation decisions.
signals.modelWording: Assess decision-specific capacity regarding accommodation, focusing on retention and appreciation of home safety risks.

signals.name: High vulnerability if discharged home
signals.clueInStem: wandering at night in heavy rain.
signals.whyItMatters: Wandering, medication mismanagement and stove risk create serious foreseeable harm.
signals.modelWording: Recognise high vulnerability from wandering, self-neglect, medication mismanagement and environmental hazards.

signals.name: Guardianship pathway
signals.clueInStem: guardianship may be required
signals.whyItMatters: Restrictive placement requires proper legal process if capacity is absent and family disagreement persists.
signals.modelWording: Consider guardianship/tribunal pathway for accommodation decisions if Mrs Bell lacks capacity and no valid substitute decision-maker can decide.

signals.name: Autonomy versus safety conflict
signals.clueInStem: She has a right to make bad decisions.
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant must distinguish capacitous unwise decisions from incapacitous decisions with serious risk.
signals.modelWording: Differentiate an unwise capacitous decision from incapacity due to dementia impairing appreciation of risk.

signals.name: Time pressure from residential bed
signals.clueInStem: the residential care bed may be lost.
signals.whyItMatters: System pressure should not shortcut capacity assessment or legal process.
signals.modelWording: Acknowledge placement pressure but ensure capacity assessment and legal steps are completed defensibly.

signals.name: Family disagreement affecting disposition
signals.clueInStem: Her daughter wants residential care, but her son says
signals.whyItMatters: Family conflict may delay safe discharge planning and requires structured communication.
signals.modelWording: Hold a family meeting to explain capacity, risk and least restrictive options while avoiding alignment with one relative.

modelAnswer:
Mrs Bell's wish to return home must be heard respectfully, but the consultant task is to decide whether this is an unwise capacitous decision or an incapacitous decision driven by dementia-related inability to appreciate risk. Her statement, "I have lived there for fifty years and I am not going to a prison," expresses a powerful attachment to home and fear of institutionalisation. That does not by itself prove incapacity. Older adults are allowed to value home over safety if they can understand and weigh the risks. However, the stem then gives concrete evidence that she may not be able to do that: she was found "wandering at night in heavy rain," leaves the stove on, misses medications, cannot manage finances and "cannot recall being found by police." Those details point to impaired retention and appreciation of recent serious harm.

I would assess capacity decision-specifically for accommodation and care, not globally. I would ask Mrs Bell what happened before admission, what concerns others have about home, what could happen if she wanders at night or leaves the stove on, how she would manage medications, food, finances and emergencies, and what alternatives exist such as increased home supports, respite or residential care. The fact that she repeatedly asks when her husband is coming although he died six years ago suggests significant memory impairment and possible disorientation, which may interfere with her ability to retain and use information. If she cannot remember the police incident or appreciate that it could recur, she is unlikely to have capacity for the accommodation decision, even though her emotional preference for home is understandable.

The ethical tension is captured by the son's statement, "She has a right to make bad decisions." He is correct as a general principle, and I would acknowledge that. The question is whether Mrs Bell is making a bad decision with capacity or whether Alzheimer's disease is preventing her from understanding and weighing foreseeable harm. I would explain this distinction to the family. I would also avoid aligning automatically with the daughter who wants residential care, because family disagreement can distort the assessment. The consultant role is to provide an independent opinion based on functional evidence, not to vote with one sibling.

The risk if discharged home alone is not theoretical. The OT assessment shows stove risk, medication non-adherence and inability to manage finances; the police incident shows wandering in dangerous conditions. The geriatrician believes discharge home alone is unsafe, and I would integrate that multidisciplinary evidence into the capacity and disposition formulation. I would still consider least restrictive alternatives before concluding that residential care is required. Could intensive home care, meals, medication supervision, stove isolation, door sensors, family support, community nursing or respite reduce risk to an acceptable level? If not, and if capacity is absent, then guardianship or tribunal involvement may be required to authorise accommodation decisions.

The system pressure is explicit: "the residential care bed may be lost." I would not allow that pressure to shortcut the assessment or legal process. Losing a bed is frustrating and may have practical consequences, but it is not a lawful reason to declare someone incapable or move them without authority. I would tell the team and family that if the bed can be held while urgent capacity and guardianship steps occur, that should be explored, but the process must remain defensible. If there is immediate risk and she cannot return home while legal processes are pending, the team may need an interim lawful placement or hospital stay depending on local legislation.

My actions would include reviewing Mrs Bell directly, obtaining OT, geriatric, nursing and family collateral, assessing cognition and function, holding a family meeting, clarifying whether any valid substitute decision-maker exists, and involving social work/guardianship services. If she lacks capacity and no less restrictive home option is realistically safe, I would support a guardianship or tribunal pathway for accommodation and care. If she retains capacity, I would respect her decision but intensify risk mitigation and document that she understands the consequences. In either scenario, the model answer must not reduce the case to "dementia equals residential care." The defensible consultant formulation is that her values are real, the risks are concrete, capacity is decision-specific, family pressure cuts both ways, and the legal pathway must not be bypassed because a bed may be lost.

title: Impaired colleague and mandatory reporting
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, clinical director of mental health
stem:
You are the clinical director of a public mental health service.

A senior registrar tells you confidentially that Dr Lane, a consultant psychiatrist, appeared intoxicated during an after-hours shift and gave confusing verbal orders for rapid tranquillisation. Nursing staff say Dr Lane smelled of alcohol and later wrote incomplete notes. One nurse says, "I was scared to question him because he is the consultant."

Dr Lane calls you the next morning and says, "I had one drink at dinner and people are exaggerating because they don't like me." He asks you not to escalate because he is going through a divorce. The hospital executive wants the matter handled quietly to avoid reputational damage.

You must decide how to respond while ensuring patient safety, fairness to Dr Lane and compliance with professional obligations.

signals.name: Potential impairment affecting patient safety
signals.clueInStem: appeared intoxicated during an after-hours shift
signals.whyItMatters: Possible impairment while practising creates immediate risk to patients and may require removal from clinical duties.
signals.modelWording: Prioritise patient safety by assessing whether Dr Lane is fit for duty and preventing further unsupervised clinical practice until clarified.

signals.name: Mandatory reporting threshold
signals.clueInStem: gave confusing verbal orders for rapid tranquillisation.
signals.whyItMatters: Practising while impaired and issuing unsafe orders may meet mandatory notification thresholds depending on jurisdiction.
signals.modelWording: Consider mandatory notification obligations if impairment placed the public at substantial risk of harm.

signals.name: Power imbalance silencing staff
signals.clueInStem: I was scared to question him because he is the consultant.
signals.whyItMatters: Hierarchy can suppress safety concerns and requires explicit support for speaking up.
signals.modelWording: Support staff who raised concerns and address hierarchy-related barriers to escalation.

signals.name: Executive pressure to minimise issue
signals.clueInStem: handled quietly to avoid reputational damage.
signals.whyItMatters: Reputation management must not override statutory obligations or patient safety.
signals.modelWording: Resist pressure to minimise the incident and follow formal governance and professional reporting processes.

signals.name: Fair process for colleague
signals.clueInStem: people are exaggerating because they don't like me.
signals.whyItMatters: Natural justice requires factual inquiry, support and non-punitive handling while protecting patients.
signals.modelWording: Ensure procedural fairness while separating support for Dr Lane from patient safety and reporting duties.

signals.name: Documentation and incident record
signals.clueInStem: incomplete notes.
signals.whyItMatters: Incomplete notes increase clinical and medico-legal risk, especially around sedation decisions.
signals.modelWording: Secure relevant documentation, medication records and incident reports to support review and patient follow-up.

modelAnswer:
This presentation has to be managed first as a patient-safety and professional-governance issue, not as an interpersonal complaint about a colleague. The phrase "appeared intoxicated during an after-hours shift" is an immediate safety trigger, particularly because Dr Lane allegedly gave "confusing verbal orders for rapid tranquillisation." Rapid tranquillisation carries risk of respiratory depression, hypotension, oversedation and death if poorly assessed or documented. My first action as clinical director would be to ensure Dr Lane is not providing further unsupervised clinical care until fitness for duty is clarified, and to review any patients affected by his orders, including observations, medication doses, adverse events and documentation.

The nurse's statement, "I was scared to question him because he is the consultant," is not a peripheral workplace issue. It shows hierarchy suppressing safety escalation. I would speak with the nurse and registrar in a supportive, factual way, reassure them that raising patient-safety concerns is appropriate, and make sure there is no retaliation. I would also review whether the service culture makes it difficult to challenge senior doctors after hours. Consultant leadership here means protecting staff who speak up while maintaining fairness to Dr Lane.

Dr Lane's explanation, "I had one drink at dinner and people are exaggerating because they don't like me," should be heard, but it cannot end the matter. He is entitled to procedural fairness, confidentiality within limits, and support, especially as he says he is going through a divorce. I would meet with him, explain the concerns factually, advise him not to work clinically until assessment, and offer occupational health, employee assistance, GP support or impairment pathway. Compassion and accountability are not opposites. Supporting him does not mean concealing risk.

The hospital executive's wish for the matter to be "handled quietly to avoid reputational damage" creates a clear ethical and governance tension. Reputation cannot override patient safety, incident review or mandatory reporting obligations. I would escalate through medical administration and clinical governance, complete incident reporting, secure medication charts and notes, and consider whether the threshold for mandatory notification is met. The threshold depends on jurisdiction and facts, but practising while impaired and issuing unsafe treatment orders may amount to substantial risk to the public. I would seek advice if needed, but I would not allow executive discomfort to suppress reporting.

I would also address documentation. The stem says Dr Lane "later wrote incomplete notes." Incomplete documentation after sedation orders increases medico-legal risk and may compromise ongoing care. I would secure the record, ensure factual corrections are made through proper addenda rather than retrospective alteration, and review whether patients or families require disclosure if harm occurred.

My documentation would be factual: who reported what, what patient reviews were undertaken, what restrictions were placed on clinical duties, what support was offered to Dr Lane, what governance steps occurred, and the rationale for any mandatory notification decision. The consultant standard is to maintain patient safety, natural justice, staff safety and legal obligations simultaneously, rather than choosing loyalty to a colleague or loyalty to the organisation's reputation.

title: Serious medication error and open disclosure
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, inpatient unit clinical lead
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist leading an acute inpatient ward.

A 54-year-old woman, Helen, was inadvertently given clozapine 300 mg at night instead of quetiapine 300 mg due to a medication chart transcription error. She became hypotensive, fell overnight and sustained a fractured wrist. The night nurse says, "I thought pharmacy had checked it." The registrar says he copied the medication list from a previous discharge summary without confirming it.

Helen's daughter arrives angry and says, "No one told us this was your mistake." The hospital risk manager asks you to prepare for open disclosure but warns that "we should avoid admitting liability." Staff are distressed and blaming each other.

You are asked to manage the immediate clinical consequences and lead the incident response.

signals.name: Immediate clinical harm from medication error
signals.clueInStem: became hypotensive, fell overnight and sustained a fractured wrist.
signals.whyItMatters: Patient harm requires urgent medical review, monitoring and prevention of further adverse events.
signals.modelWording: Prioritise Helen's clinical stabilisation, falls management and monitoring for clozapine-related adverse effects.

signals.name: Open disclosure required
signals.clueInStem: No one told us this was your mistake.
signals.whyItMatters: Failure to disclose worsens harm, complaint risk and ethical breach.
signals.modelWording: Conduct timely open disclosure with apology, factual explanation, known consequences and next steps.

signals.name: Systems error not individual blame only
signals.clueInStem: pharmacy had checked it.
signals.whyItMatters: Blame culture prevents learning; root cause analysis must examine prescribing, transcription and checking systems.
signals.modelWording: Frame the incident as requiring systems review rather than premature individual blame.

signals.name: Registrar practice and supervision issue
signals.clueInStem: copied the medication list from a previous discharge summary without confirming it.
signals.whyItMatters: Unsafe prescribing practice requires supervision, education and possible remediation.
signals.modelWording: Review registrar prescribing practice and supervision while ensuring learning and accountability.

signals.name: Risk manager advice must not prevent honesty
signals.clueInStem: avoid admitting liability.
signals.whyItMatters: Open disclosure can be honest without speculative blame; avoiding truth undermines trust.
signals.modelWording: Provide factual disclosure and apology for harm without speculating beyond known facts or assigning premature liability.

signals.name: Staff distress and team functioning
signals.clueInStem: Staff are distressed and blaming each other.
signals.whyItMatters: Team distress can impair ongoing safety and requires containment and debriefing.
signals.modelWording: Support staff debriefing and psychological safety while maintaining accountability and patient-centred focus.

modelAnswer:
Helen's case is a serious medication error with actual harm, so the first consultant action is clinical stabilisation, not administrative language management. The stem states she was inadvertently given clozapine 300 mg instead of quetiapine 300 mg and then "became hypotensive, fell overnight and sustained a fractured wrist." I would ensure she receives immediate medical review, ongoing observations, falls assessment, fracture management, monitoring for clozapine-related sedation, hypotension, anticholinergic effects and cardiac concerns, and that the medication chart is corrected so no further dose is administered. Patient safety and harm mitigation come before the incident meeting.

The daughter's statement, "No one told us this was your mistake," is a major communication failure. This requires timely open disclosure. I would meet Helen and her daughter with an appropriate senior clinician and, if possible, the ward manager or risk representative. I would explain the known facts clearly, apologise for the error and the harm, outline what is being done clinically, and describe the review process. I would not speculate beyond known facts or assign premature individual blame, but I would not hide behind vague language. The risk manager's warning to "avoid admitting liability" does not mean avoiding honesty. A factual apology and transparent explanation are ethical and clinically necessary.

The incident must be framed as a systems failure until properly reviewed. The night nurse says, "I thought pharmacy had checked it," and the registrar says he "copied the medication list from a previous discharge summary without confirming it." Those clues point to failures in medication reconciliation, prescribing verification, pharmacy checking, nursing administration, handover and perhaps electronic chart design. I would initiate an incident report and root-cause or systems review that examines how clozapine could be transcribed and administered to a non-clozapine patient, what checks failed, and whether clozapine governance safeguards were bypassed.

The registrar's practice requires supervision and remediation, but I would avoid scapegoating. Copying a medication list without confirming it is unsafe and must be addressed through prescribing supervision, education and review of competence. At the same time, the nurse and pharmacy processes need review because medication safety is deliberately built on multiple checks. Staff are "distressed and blaming each other," so I would organise a debrief that supports staff emotionally while keeping the focus on patient harm, accountability and learning.

I would document the clinical event, open disclosure conversation, family questions, immediate treatment, incident notification, medication-chart correction, and planned review. I would also ensure Helen receives follow-up for the fracture and psychological impact, and that any complaint pathway is explained. The consultant-level response is not to protect the service by minimising the error, nor to punish a single junior doctor to create the appearance of action. It is to care for the patient, tell the truth, preserve evidence, identify system failures and implement changes that reduce recurrence.

title: Executive pressure to discharge unsafe patient
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist on an acute inpatient unit during severe bed pressure.

Tariq is a 32-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder admitted after threatening his ex-partner while believing she was "infected by demons." He remains guarded, intermittently hostile and has refused family meetings. Yesterday he told a nurse, "When I leave, I know where she lives."

The hospital executive director phones you and says, "We need this bed today; ED is breaching targets and he is no longer floridly psychotic." The bed manager suggests discharge to temporary accommodation with crisis follow-up. The registrar is uncomfortable but says, "Maybe we can document that he denies intent."

Tariq's ex-partner has an active family violence order and has not been contacted because staff are unsure about confidentiality.

signals.name: Explicit threat to identifiable person
signals.clueInStem: When I leave, I know where she lives.
signals.whyItMatters: A specific threat to an identifiable victim creates serious risk and potential duty to protect/warn considerations.
signals.modelWording: Treat the statement as a specific dynamic risk to an identifiable person requiring urgent risk formulation and protective action.

signals.name: Executive pressure compromising clinical independence
signals.clueInStem: We need this bed today
signals.whyItMatters: Bed pressure must not override clinical safety or legal duties.
signals.modelWording: Maintain clinical independence and refuse discharge if risk cannot be safely managed despite executive pressure.

signals.name: False reassurance from denial of intent
signals.clueInStem: document that he denies intent.
signals.whyItMatters: Denial does not negate contextual and behavioural risk indicators.
signals.modelWording: Do not rely on denial of intent when recent threats, guardedness and family violence context indicate ongoing risk.

signals.name: Family violence and victim safety
signals.clueInStem: active family violence order
signals.whyItMatters: Victim safety planning and possible information-sharing may be required despite confidentiality concerns.
signals.modelWording: Consider family violence risk, victim safety planning and lawful information-sharing where serious risk exists.

signals.name: Inadequate disposition plan
signals.clueInStem: temporary accommodation with crisis follow-up.
signals.whyItMatters: Crisis follow-up may be inadequate for specific violence risk and active psychosis.
signals.modelWording: Reject disposition plans that do not address specific violence risk, victim protection and treatment adherence.

signals.name: Defensible documentation under pressure
signals.clueInStem: ED is breaching targets
signals.whyItMatters: Performance targets may later be scrutinised if adverse outcome occurs.
signals.modelWording: Document clinical reasoning, executive pressure, risk factors, protective actions and rationale for admission/discharge decision.

modelAnswer:
Tariq should not be discharged simply because the hospital executive needs the bed. The phrase "When I leave, I know where she lives" is a specific threat toward an identifiable person in the context of psychosis and family violence. That single sentence changes the risk formulation. It is not neutral that he remains guarded and intermittently hostile, has refused family meetings, and was admitted after threatening his ex-partner while believing she was "infected by demons." I would treat this as ongoing dynamic risk to an identifiable victim, not as a resolved psychotic episode.

The executive director's statement, "We need this bed today," and the reference to ED targets create a system-pressure trap. A consultant can acknowledge access block and work constructively to create capacity, but clinical independence must be maintained. I would tell the executive that discharge is not safe at this point unless specific violence risk, victim safety and treatment adherence can be managed. I would offer to review other possible discharges or step-down options, but I would not sign an unsafe discharge to meet a performance target. If needed, I would escalate through clinical governance and document the pressure and my reasoning.

The registrar's suggestion to "document that he denies intent" is exactly the wrong risk logic. Denial of intent does not neutralise a direct threat, especially in a guarded patient with persecutory beliefs and an active family violence context. I would supervise the registrar that risk assessment is not a box-tick of stated intent; it is a formulation of behaviour, threats, access, mental state, history, substances, victim vulnerability and protective controls. The phrase "document that he denies intent" should be replaced by a documented explanation of why denial is or is not reliable in light of the rest of the evidence.

The active family violence order is not a side issue. It indicates prior legal recognition of risk and means that victim safety planning must occur. Staff are unsure about confidentiality, but confidentiality does not prevent lawful information sharing where there is serious risk to an identifiable person. I would consider police/family violence liaison, check the terms of the order, assess whether the ex-partner needs warning or safety support through appropriate channels, and document the legal and ethical basis for any disclosure. The tension is between preserving Tariq's confidentiality and protecting a foreseeable victim. In this case, the threat and context make victim safety an active obligation.

A plan of "temporary accommodation with crisis follow-up" is inadequate unless it specifically addresses the risk pathway. Where will he stay, how far from the ex-partner, who supervises medication, what are the legal restrictions, what happens if he leaves, what is the police role, what is the family violence safety plan, and what treatment engagement exists? If those questions cannot be answered, discharge is not defensible. I would reassess his psychosis, insight, capacity, substance use, access to the victim, access to weapons and willingness to accept treatment. If risk remains high, continued admission or involuntary treatment is justified if statutory criteria are met.

My documentation would record the exact threat, the active family violence order, the executive pressure, the registrar's uncertainty, victim-safety actions, confidentiality reasoning, and why the chosen disposition is the least restrictive safe option. If an adverse event occurred, the record should show that the consultant recognised the risk and acted on it, rather than allowing bed pressure to become the hidden determinant of discharge.

title: Registrar working beyond competence
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, training supervisor and unit consultant
setting: Regional inpatient and emergency psychiatry service
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist supervising a Stage 1 registrar in a regional service.

Over two weeks, several nurses report that the registrar has been making independent decisions overnight without calling for help, including discharging a suicidal patient from ED and prescribing rapid tranquillisation without physical observations. The registrar says, "I thought consultants expected us to cope independently."

Last night a patient required ICU transfer after oversedation. The registrar did not call you until after the transfer and later documented, "Consultant aware," although you had not been contacted. The NUM says staff have lost confidence and now bypass the registrar.

The director of training asks you to manage the patient safety issue while supporting the registrar's training.

signals.name: Patient harm from unsupervised practice
signals.clueInStem: required ICU transfer after oversedation.
signals.whyItMatters: Actual harm means immediate review of supervision, scope of practice and affected patient care.
signals.modelWording: Prioritise patient safety by reviewing the adverse event and restricting independent high-risk decisions until competence is assured.

signals.name: False documentation
signals.clueInStem: Consultant aware
signals.whyItMatters: Inaccurate documentation is a serious professionalism and medico-legal issue.
signals.modelWording: Address inaccurate documentation directly as a professionalism and governance concern requiring correction and escalation.

signals.name: Supervision failure and unclear escalation culture
signals.clueInStem: I thought consultants expected us to cope independently.
signals.whyItMatters: Unsafe independence may reflect both trainee judgement and system supervision failures.
signals.modelWording: Clarify supervision expectations, escalation thresholds and after-hours support responsibilities.

signals.name: Medication safety breach
signals.clueInStem: rapid tranquillisation without physical observations.
signals.whyItMatters: Sedation without monitoring risks respiratory compromise, hypotension and death.
signals.modelWording: Review rapid tranquillisation practice and ensure mandatory physical monitoring is embedded.

signals.name: Team loss of confidence
signals.clueInStem: staff have lost confidence
signals.whyItMatters: Bypassing the registrar fragments care and worsens safety unless leadership restores clear roles.
signals.modelWording: Restore team safety by clarifying roles, escalation pathways and supervised decision-making arrangements.

signals.name: Training support and remediation
signals.clueInStem: supporting the registrar's training.
signals.whyItMatters: Patient safety must be balanced with fair remediation rather than punitive abandonment.
signals.modelWording: Develop a remediation and supervision plan that supports learning while protecting patients.

modelAnswer:
This scenario requires immediate patient-safety action and a supervision response, not a vague reminder to the registrar to call more often. The stem includes actual harm: a patient "required ICU transfer after oversedation." That is a sentinel warning that the registrar is making high-risk decisions beyond competence or without adequate escalation. My first action would be to ensure the ICU patient and any other patients affected by recent overnight decisions are reviewed, including the suicidal ED discharge and any rapid tranquillisation episodes. I would restrict the registrar from making independent high-risk after-hours decisions until competence and supervision arrangements are clarified.

The documentation issue is particularly serious. The registrar wrote "Consultant aware" although I had not been contacted. That is not a harmless shorthand. It is inaccurate documentation that could mislead future clinicians and medico-legal review, and it raises professionalism concerns. I would address it directly with the registrar, require the record to be corrected through a proper addendum rather than hidden alteration, and escalate through clinical governance and the training pathway. The response must be fair and factual, but inaccurate documentation cannot be minimised as stress or inexperience.

The registrar's explanation, "I thought consultants expected us to cope independently," suggests a system and supervision failure as well as an individual performance problem. I would ask how escalation expectations were communicated, whether after-hours workload is excessive, whether the registrar knew rapid tranquillisation policies, and whether they feared being criticised for calling. A Stage 1 registrar should not be left to infer when consultant input is required for suicidal discharge, seclusion, rapid tranquillisation or deteriorating medical status. I would create explicit escalation thresholds and make myself or the duty consultant visibly available.

The rapid tranquillisation clue, "without physical observations," is a concrete safety breach. Sedation without monitoring can cause respiratory depression, hypotension, arrhythmia or death. I would audit recent rapid tranquillisation events, reinforce policy, ensure nursing staff are empowered to refuse unsafe medication administration or escalate, and arrange direct teaching and observed practice for the registrar. The aim is not to humiliate the registrar, but to protect patients and restore safe practice.

The NUM's statement that "staff have lost confidence" matters because the MDT is now bypassing the registrar. That may be understandable, but it fragments care and can create further risk. I would meet the nursing team, acknowledge their concerns, clarify who makes which decisions, and establish a safe escalation pathway that does not rely on informal bypassing. Staff need to know that calling the consultant directly is acceptable when high-risk decisions exceed the registrar's competence.

At the same time, the director of training is right that the registrar requires support, not abandonment. I would develop a remediation plan with increased supervision, case review, prescribing/rapid tranquillisation training, observed assessments, documentation review, and clear competence milestones. Depending on the severity and insight, temporary removal from after-hours duties may be necessary. My documentation would cover patient reviews, incident reporting, correction of the false note, supervision changes, registrar discussion, MDT communication and training plan. The consultant-level standard is to hold patient safety, procedural fairness, education and governance together.

title: Coronial inquiry and defensible documentation
candidateRole: Consultant psychiatrist, community mental health team clinical lead
setting: Community mental health service after patient death
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist clinical lead of a community mental health team.

Daniel, a 41-year-old man with recurrent depression and alcohol dependence, died by suicide two days after discharge from crisis team follow-up. The file notes state "denies suicidal ideation, low risk," but there is no documented formulation. His partner had phoned the team the day before death saying, "He has given away his tools and keeps saying we'll be better off without him."

A junior clinician documented the call as "partner anxious" and no senior review occurred. The family has requested records and says, "You ignored our warning." The service manager tells you a coronial investigation is likely and asks you to review the case.

Staff are distressed and worried about blame. You must lead the immediate response, documentation review and service learning process.

signals.name: Missed collateral warning signs
signals.clueInStem: He has given away his tools and keeps saying we'll be better off without him.
signals.whyItMatters: Preparatory behaviour and burdensomeness statements are high-risk warning signs; missing collateral may be a major systems failure.
signals.modelWording: Identify the partner's collateral as significant dynamic suicide risk requiring senior review and safety action.

signals.name: Inadequate risk formulation documentation
signals.clueInStem: denies suicidal ideation, low risk
signals.whyItMatters: A denial-based risk label is not defensible without formulation, context and rationale.
signals.modelWording: Critique reliance on denial of suicidal ideation and absence of documented risk formulation.

signals.name: Need for immediate postvention and family response
signals.clueInStem: You ignored our warning.
signals.whyItMatters: Family communication must be compassionate, transparent and coordinated without speculative defensiveness.
signals.modelWording: Provide compassionate family contact, acknowledge distress, explain review process and avoid premature conclusions.

signals.name: Senior review escalation failure
signals.clueInStem: no senior review occurred.
signals.whyItMatters: Failure to escalate collateral risk indicates possible service process gap.
signals.modelWording: Review escalation processes for high-risk collateral calls and after-hours clinical decision-making.

signals.name: Staff distress and blame anxiety
signals.clueInStem: Staff are distressed and worried about blame.
signals.whyItMatters: A just culture response supports staff while preserving accountability and learning.
signals.modelWording: Use a just culture approach: support staff, preserve records and focus on systems learning.

signals.name: Records request and coronial process
signals.clueInStem: a coronial investigation is likely
signals.whyItMatters: Records must be preserved, factual and managed through appropriate governance/legal channels.
signals.modelWording: Preserve records and coordinate information release through appropriate legal and governance processes.

modelAnswer:
Daniel's death needs to be approached as a serious incident with potential coronial scrutiny, but the clinical heart of the review is already visible in the stem. The partner's call that "He has given away his tools and keeps saying we'll be better off without him" was not simply an anxious family call. Giving away meaningful possessions and expressing perceived burdensomeness are major dynamic suicide warning signs. In a man with recurrent depression and alcohol dependence, two days after discharge from crisis follow-up, that collateral should have triggered senior review and active safety planning.

The file note "denies suicidal ideation, low risk" is not defensible as a risk formulation. It records one negative symptom response and then applies a label. It does not reconcile the denial with the partner's warning, alcohol dependence, recent crisis involvement, possible preparatory behaviour, or the very high-risk transition period after discharge. I would explicitly identify this in the review: the failure was not that risk prediction is impossible, but that available dynamic risk information was not adequately weighted or escalated.

The junior clinician's documentation of the call as "partner anxious" and the fact that "no senior review occurred" suggest a system problem in triage and escalation. I would construct a factual timeline: when the call came, who took it, what was documented, what policies exist for collateral suicide warnings, whether the clinician had supervision available, whether the partner was called back, and what attempts were made to contact Daniel. I would review whether the service culture unintentionally discounts family collateral when the patient denies suicidal ideation.

The family's statement, "You ignored our warning," requires a compassionate and transparent response. I would not become defensive or speculate about causation. I would arrange senior contact through an open disclosure and postvention pathway, acknowledge the family's distress, explain that the service is reviewing the care, provide information about support and complaints processes, and preserve all records. If a coronial investigation is likely, communication should be factual, empathic and coordinated with governance/legal advice, but not evasive.

Staff are "distressed and worried about blame," so I would lead this as a just-culture review. That means supporting staff while still asking hard questions about decision-making, documentation and escalation. Just culture does not mean no accountability; it means distinguishing human error, system weakness, training needs and reckless practice. The review should lead to concrete improvements, such as mandatory senior review for collateral involving preparatory behaviour, clearer documentation standards, family-call escalation protocols, and safer discharge-from-crisis procedures.

My documentation and governance response would include preservation of the record, incident notification, timeline, family contact, staff support, clinical review findings and action plan. The consultant-level lesson is that suicide-risk assessment must synthesise behaviour, collateral and context, not rely on "denies suicidal ideation." Daniel's partner gave the team a clinically significant warning; the service response must acknowledge that and improve the system that allowed it to be minimised.

title: Contextual suicide risk despite denial
stem:
You are the consultant psychiatrist asked to advise an ED registrar about Mr Cole, a 52-year-old separated man brought in by police after sitting in his car outside his former workplace for several hours.

Mr Cole denies suicidal ideation and says, "I'm not stupid enough to say anything that gets me locked up." He lost his job two weeks ago after an investigation into misconduct. His ex-wife reports he has been drinking heavily, giving away tools and saying, "The kids will be better off without me." Police found a hose and tape in the boot of his car, which he says were "for gardening."

The ED is overcrowded, and Mr Cole wants to leave. The registrar says he is calm, denies intent and does not meet criteria for admission. His ex-wife is frightened but says she cannot stay involved because "he blames me for everything."

signals.name: Denial unreliable in high-risk context
signals.clueInStem: I'm not stupid enough to say anything that gets me locked up.
signals.whyItMatters: This suggests strategic denial and invalidates reliance on self-report alone.
signals.modelWording: Do not rely on denial of suicidal ideation where the patient implies concealment and collateral indicates high dynamic risk.

signals.name: Preparatory behaviour and means
signals.clueInStem: Police found a hose and tape in the boot of his car
signals.whyItMatters: Potential means consistent with suicide method require serious risk formulation.
signals.modelWording: Treat hose and tape in the car, combined with recent losses and collateral, as possible preparatory behaviour.

signals.name: Major psychosocial loss
signals.clueInStem: lost his job two weeks ago
signals.whyItMatters: Recent shame, unemployment and separation increase acute suicide risk.
signals.modelWording: Formulate recent job loss, shame and relationship breakdown as acute precipitants.

signals.name: Alcohol increasing impulsivity
signals.clueInStem: drinking heavily
signals.whyItMatters: Alcohol increases impulsivity, disinhibition and near-term suicide risk.
signals.modelWording: Assess alcohol intoxication/dependence because it increases impulsivity and undermines safety planning.

signals.name: Collateral indicates burdensomeness
signals.clueInStem: The kids will be better off without me.
signals.whyItMatters: Perceived burdensomeness is a concerning suicide risk marker.
signals.modelWording: Give significant weight to ex-wife's collateral regarding burdensomeness and giving away possessions.

signals.name: ED pressure and unsafe discharge risk
signals.clueInStem: The ED is overcrowded
signals.whyItMatters: Crowding must not lower threshold for unsafe discharge.
signals.modelWording: Acknowledge ED pressure but base disposition on risk formulation, not denial or access block.

modelAnswer:
Mr Cole should be treated as a high-risk presentation despite his calmness and denial of suicidal ideation, because the stem contains a coherent pattern of concealment, preparation and acute shame. His statement, "I'm not stupid enough to say anything that gets me locked up," is not reassuring; it tells me that he understands the assessment process and may be deliberately withholding suicidal intent to avoid interruption. In that context, I would not accept the registrar's view that calm presentation and denial mean he does not meet criteria for admission. I would take over the risk formulation and make clear that the decision cannot be based on self-report alone.

The most concerning clue is that police found "a hose and tape in the boot of his car" after he had been sitting outside his former workplace for several hours. That is not a neutral gardening detail in this context. It is potential access to means, possibly preparatory behaviour, and it sits alongside his recent job loss "two weeks ago after an investigation into misconduct," his separation, heavy alcohol use, giving away tools and the collateral statement, "The kids will be better off without me." These details together form a high-risk behavioural sequence: acute shame, occupational loss, relationship rupture, intoxication, perceived burdensomeness, giving away possessions, possible method preparation and strategic concealment. I would explicitly document that denial of suicidal ideation is unreliable because it conflicts with collateral and behaviour.

My immediate action would be to prevent premature departure while the assessment is completed. This does not mean automatically detaining him forever, but it does mean the ED cannot allow him to leave simply because he is calm. I would ask ED and police to secure the hose and tape, clarify exactly where he was found, whether the car was running or positioned in a secluded place, whether there were other means in the car, and whether he had sent messages, made financial arrangements or said goodbye to anyone. I would assess intoxication and withdrawal risk because his ex-wife reports he has been "drinking heavily," and alcohol increases impulsivity, disinhibition and near-term suicide risk. I would also assess depression, shame, hopelessness, sleep, psychosis, prior attempts, access to medications or firearms, and his capacity to collaborate with a safety plan.

I would obtain collateral urgently from police, the ex-wife, GP and, if relevant and lawful, workplace or other supports, because the ex-wife's statement that she cannot stay involved because "he blames me for everything" means she is both a source of critical collateral and potentially unsafe as a discharge support. I would not ask her to supervise him if she is frightened or being blamed. The ethical tension is between respecting an adult man's wish to leave and the duty to act on strong evidence of concealed suicidal risk. In my view, autonomy is not properly respected by accepting a strategically concealed account at face value; it is respected by doing a careful, proportionate assessment and using the least restrictive option that actually manages the risk.
