modelAnswer:
Helen's medication error should not be managed by the defensive shortcut, "Avoid admitting liability." Open disclosure is not the same as reckless legal admission; it is an ethical and governance obligation after harm. Helen received clozapine instead of quetiapine, became hypotensive and fractured her wrist. The first consultant action is to ensure ongoing medical care, not to shape language to protect the institution.

Her statement, "No one told us this was your mistake," is the key clinical and ethical phrase. It tells me the harm has already been compounded by poor communication. I would meet Helen and her family, explain the known facts plainly, apologise for what happened, outline her current treatment and the investigation process, and avoid speculation until the systems review is complete. I would ensure she receives fracture care, monitoring for clozapine effects and psychological support.

The registrar asks, "Do we incident report if pharmacy also missed it?" The answer is yes. Shared failure across pharmacy, nursing and medicine is exactly why incident reporting is required. It is not a reason to avoid reporting; it is evidence that a systems review is needed. The nurse's statement, "I thought pharmacy checked it," points to a medication reconciliation/checking failure and possibly unsafe assumptions about clozapine controls.

Helen's second statement, "I fell because I could not stand," grounds the error in patient harm. Staff blaming each other must be redirected into just culture: identify what failed, support staff, and maintain accountability. I would review prescribing, dispensing, administration, alerts, handover, and whether clozapine-specific safeguards were bypassed.

I would document the event, clinical harm, open disclosure conversation, apology, incident report, preservation of records, systems-review plan and immediate safety changes. That documentation is defensible because it demonstrates honesty, patient care and learning, rather than concealment driven by liability fear.

title: Executive pressure unsafe discharge
stem:
You are the inpatient consultant psychiatrist. Acute unit with access block.

Tariq, a 32-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder, has threatened an ex-partner during psychosis and remains guarded. Tariq says, "When I leave, I know where she lives." 

Executive director says, "We need this bed today." Registrar asks, "Can we document that he denies intent?" Ex-partner says, "There is an active family violence order." Tariq is from a migrant family who fears police involvement.

Bed manager says, "ED is breaching targets." You must decide whether to refuse discharge and escalate victim safety/legal actions.

signals.clueInStem: whether to refuse discharge and escalate victim safety/legal actions
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Tariq should not be discharged because the executive director says, "We need this bed today." That is the unsafe shortcut. Bed pressure is real, but it cannot override a specific threat to an identifiable victim in a patient still guarded after psychosis and with an active family violence order. Discharging him under these circumstances would be a clinical and governance failure.

His statement, "When I leave, I know where she lives," is the key risk phrase. It is specific, future-oriented and directed at an identifiable ex-partner. The registrar's suggestion, "Can we document that he denies intent?" misunderstands risk assessment. Denial of intent is not a protective factor when the patient has made a targeted statement and remains guarded. I would formulate risk around psychosis, family violence, access to the victim, prior threats, treatment response and ability to comply with restrictions.

The legal and ethical tension is duty to protect versus confidentiality and least restrictive care. The active family violence order means police/family violence liaison is necessary. I would consider whether the ex-partner needs warning or safety action through lawful channels, and whether Tariq continues to meet criteria for involuntary admission or treatment. I would not disclose more than necessary, but I would not hide behind confidentiality when serious risk to an identified person exists.

His migrant family fears police involvement, and that fear should be acknowledged and managed with explanation and interpreters if needed. But cultural or family fear cannot prevent victim-safety action. The bed manager's statement, "ED is breaching targets," should be documented as a system pressure and escalated, not allowed to determine clinical discharge.

I would document the exact threat, the active family violence order, denial-of-intent limitations, police/victim-safety actions, cultural/family concerns, executive pressure and the rationale for ongoing admission or any alternative disposition. This is defensible because it shows the consultant protected an identifiable victim and did not discharge the bed rather than the patient.

title: Registrar beyond competence and false consultant aware
stem:
You are the consultant supervisor. Regional service after ICU oversedation.

Stage 1 registrar, Dr Lee, prescribed rapid tranquillisation without observations and a patient went to ICU. Stage 1 registrar says, "Consultant aware." 

NUM says, "Staff now bypass the registrar." Director of Training asks, "Is this remediation or notification?" Patient says, "I woke up in ICU and no one explained." The registrar says independence was expected in the service culture.

Registrar says, "I thought consultants wanted us to cope." You must decide whether to restrict duties, remediate and investigate.

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Dr Lee's unsafe practice should not be managed by the informal workaround that "Staff now bypass the registrar." That is the unsafe shortcut. Bypassing may protect patients briefly, but it leaves the underlying supervision, competence and documentation failures unresolved. A patient went to ICU after rapid tranquillisation without observations, so the response must be formal, immediate and fair.

The phrase "Consultant aware" is the key professional clue. If the consultant was not in fact aware, this is inaccurate documentation in a high-risk event. It may reflect panic, misunderstanding, a culture of assumed independence or deliberate misrepresentation; the difference matters and must be investigated. I would ensure the record is corrected through proper addendum, not quietly altered.

The Director of Training asks, "Is this remediation or notification?" The answer depends on facts, insight, harm, pattern and risk. Immediate restriction of duties and close supervision are required while investigation occurs. Remediation may include rapid-tranquillisation training, documentation review, observed assessments and explicit escalation thresholds. Notification may be required if professional conduct or public safety thresholds are met.

The patient saying, "I woke up in ICU and no one explained," identifies open disclosure and patient harm. I would ensure the patient receives an explanation, apology where appropriate, and follow-up care. The registrar's statement, "I thought consultants wanted us to cope," suggests a service culture problem, not only individual failure. A Stage 1 registrar should not infer that dangerous after-hours decisions must be made alone.

I would document patient review, duty restrictions, record correction, open disclosure, supervision changes, registrar discussion, training director involvement, and the notification/remediation rationale. This is defensible because it protects patients while treating the registrar fairly and addressing the system that allowed unsafe independence.

title: Social media privacy breach by registrar
stem:
You are the consultant training supervisor. Acute inpatient unit governance meeting.

A Stage 2 registrar, posted about an involuntary patient on social media with identifying details. A Stage 2 registrar says, "It was anonymous; I was just venting." 

Service manager says, "Delete the screenshots and handle it informally." Whanau liaison asks, "Do we need open disclosure with the whānau?" Patient says, "You people are making fun of me." The patient is a Māori woman and whānau already distrust services.

Registrar says, "Everyone posts de-identified things." You must decide whether to preserve evidence, disclose and remediate/report.

modelAnswer:
The registrar's social media breach should not be hidden by deleting screenshots and handling it informally. That is the unsafe shortcut. Evidence preservation is essential because the post included identifying details about an involuntary patient, and deleting screenshots would undermine governance and trust. The fact that the patient is Māori and whānau already distrust services makes the cultural harm more serious, not less.

The registrar's statement, "It was anonymous; I was just venting," is the key minimisation. Anonymous is not the same as non-identifiable, and venting about an identifiable patient in public is not acceptable professional coping. The later claim, "Everyone posts de-identified things," suggests a broader culture problem that may require education and policy reinforcement beyond one registrar.

The whānau liaison asks, "Do we need open disclosure with the whānau?" I would consider disclosure to the patient and, with consent and cultural support, whānau, because the patient has already said, "You people are making fun of me." Open disclosure should acknowledge the breach, explain evidence preservation and action taken, and offer support. Confidentiality and privacy obligations apply to the patient too, so whānau involvement must be handled appropriately.

The vulnerable person is an involuntary Māori patient subject to coercive care and now feeling mocked by staff. This can worsen paranoia, shame, disengagement and mistrust. I would ensure the post is removed through proper channels, preserve evidence, report the privacy incident, support the patient and whānau, and manage registrar remediation or escalation.

I would document the post content, identifiability, screenshot preservation, patient/whānau disclosure process, registrar explanation, governance reporting and education plan. The documentation is defensible because it shows the service chose transparency and cultural repair rather than reputational concealment.

title: Boundary violation in psychotherapy
stem:
You are the psychotherapy supervisor consultant. Community psychotherapy program.

A registrar, has met a trauma patient after hours at cafes with no documentation. A registrar says, "She needed someone who would not abandon her." 

Service manager says, "Transfer her quietly before fellowship review." Training director asks, "Does this require notification?" Patient says, "If he gets in trouble, I will cut myself." The patient has complex trauma and chronic self-harm.

Registrar says, "I was being flexible and therapeutic." You must decide whether to stop contact, protect patient and escalate governance.

modelAnswer:
The registrar's boundary breach should not be managed by quietly transferring the patient before fellowship review. That is the unsafe shortcut. A quiet transfer protects the trainee and service image while leaving the patient harmed, dependent and confused, and it prevents proper governance. Meeting a trauma patient after hours at cafes with no documentation is a serious psychotherapy boundary concern.

The registrar's statement, "She needed someone who would not abandon her," is the key formulation. It shows he has rationalised boundary crossing as therapeutic rescue, precisely in a patient with complex trauma and abandonment sensitivity. That is dangerous because the blurred relationship may intensify dependency, secrecy and self-harm risk when boundaries are restored. His later phrase, "I was being flexible and therapeutic," confirms poor insight into the frame.

The training director asks, "Does this require notification?" This requires assessment of severity, exploitation, sexual or emotional boundary violation, documentation absence, risk to patient and registrar insight. At minimum it requires removal from the case, supervision, governance review and training involvement. Depending on findings, formal notification or employer/regulator action may be required. The absence of notes is itself a governance problem and should not be repaired through retrospective fiction.

The patient says, "If he gets in trouble, I will cut myself." That threat must be assessed and managed, but it cannot veto governance. I would arrange an urgent senior review, explain the change in care transparently and compassionately, create a crisis plan, and ensure continuity with another clinician. The patient is vulnerable because of trauma and chronic self-harm; the response must not feel like punishment or sudden abandonment.

I would document the boundary concerns, undocumented sessions, immediate cessation of contact, patient risk plan, trainee discussion, evidence preservation, supervision actions and notification reasoning. The record is defensible because it protects the patient and does not collude with secrecy disguised as therapeutic flexibility.

title: Coronial review after discharge suicide
stem:
You are the unit clinical lead. Serious incident meeting.

Nikhil, a 38-year-old man, died by suicide 36 hours after discharge from the acute ward. Nikhil says, "You discharged the bed, not the patient." 

Registrar says, "I only did what the bed manager asked." Service manager asks, "What do we give the coroner?" Partner says, "He was giving away tools and saying I will be free soon." The wrong GP received the discharge summary.

Nurse says, "The ward was desperate for a bed." You must decide whether to lead serious incident review and support family/staff.

signals.clueInStem: I only did what the bed manager asked.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass family complaint and open disclosure, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Nikhil's death must not be explained away by the registrar saying, "I only did what the bed manager asked." That is the unsafe shortcut and the governance failure. Bed pressure may explain the environment, but it cannot become the clinical rationale for discharge. A man died by suicide 36 hours after discharge, the wrong GP received the summary, and the partner had given clear warning collateral.

The phrase "You discharged the bed, not the patient" captures the family's central concern and must be treated seriously, not defensively. The partner's statement that he was "giving away tools and saying I will be free soon" is clinically more important than any generic denial of suicidal ideation. Giving away tools and liberation language suggest preparation, burdensomeness or imminent suicidal thinking. If that information was available before discharge, the review must ask why it did not trigger senior reassessment.

The service manager asks, "What do we give the coroner?" The answer is complete, factual records and a transparent account, not curated fragments. I would preserve all records, create a timeline, identify who knew what and when, review the discharge decision, wrong-GP communication, family collateral handling and consultant oversight. Legal advice may guide process, but it should not distort facts.

The vulnerable people now are the bereaved partner/family and the staff involved. The family needs open disclosure and postvention support. Staff need a just-culture process that supports them while examining decisions honestly. The nurse's statement, "The ward was desperate for a bed," should be included in the systems review because it may reveal unsafe discharge pressure.

I would document the incident response, family contact, record preservation, coroner preparation, staff support, identified care issues and immediate safety improvements for future discharges. Defensible documentation shows that the service did not hide behind bed pressure or individual blame, but learned from a serious failure in risk formulation and continuity of care.

title: Complaint after culturally unsafe police transfer
stem:
You are the clinical director. Community service complaint meeting.

A patient's family has complained, that an Aboriginal man was discharged from crisis care and died in police custody. A patient's family has complained says, "You treated him like a criminal, not a patient." 

Executive says, "Do not say sorry until legal approves." Complaint officer asks, "Is this AHPRA or internal review?" Family says, "He begged not to be taken by police." No Aboriginal liaison was contacted before police transfer.

Staff member says, "We were scared and short-staffed." You must decide whether to conduct open disclosure, cultural review and governance escalation.

modelAnswer:
The service should not avoid apology because the executive says, "Do not say sorry until legal approves." That is the unsafe shortcut. Legal advice has a role, but defensive silence after an Aboriginal man's death in police custody can deepen harm, mistrust and cultural unsafety. Open disclosure can be factual, compassionate and carefully coordinated without making unsupported admissions.

The family's statement, "You treated him like a criminal, not a patient," is the key formulation of the complaint. It alleges a failure of cultural safety, clinical containment and least restrictive care. The fact that "He begged not to be taken by police" and no Aboriginal liaison was contacted before police transfer raises serious questions about whether police were used as the default containment option rather than a proportionate last resort.

The complaint officer asks, "Is this AHPRA or internal review?" The answer may be both or neither depending on facts, but it cannot be decided before review. I would initiate a serious incident/cultural safety review, preserve records, examine clinical decision-making, police involvement, staffing, liaison attempts, risk assessment and transfer alternatives. If individual practitioner conduct or impairment meets a notification threshold, that should be escalated.

The vulnerable parties are the deceased patient, his family and the Aboriginal community's trust in the service. Staff saying, "We were scared and short-staffed," is important context but not a full defence. Fear and short staffing can explain decisions, but the review must ask whether the system gave staff safe alternatives to police transfer and whether cultural supports were accessible.

I would document family contact, open disclosure, cultural review process, Aboriginal leadership involvement, staff support, complaint/regulatory pathway reasoning and improvement actions. Defensible documentation shows the service responded with humility, transparency and learning rather than legalistic avoidance.

title: Policy conflict: seclusion target versus ward violence
stem:
You are the medical director consultant. Governance meeting after restraint incident.

The ward team, secluded a psychotic patient after assaulting another patient, breaching seclusion reduction targets. The ward team says, "He punched an elderly patient before we could move anyone." 

Executive says, "Our seclusion numbers cannot go up this month." Quality manager asks, "Was policy breached?" Elderly patient says, "I am frightened to sleep on the ward." Staff feel blamed by a culture focused on KPIs.

Nurse says, "We are told never to seclude, then blamed when someone is hurt." You must decide whether to balance safety, least restrictive care and governance learning.

modelAnswer:
The ward's response should not be judged by the metric shortcut, "Our seclusion numbers cannot go up this month." Seclusion reduction is important, but a KPI cannot override immediate safety after a psychotic patient has punched an elderly patient. The consultant task is to determine whether seclusion was necessary, lawful and least restrictive in the moment, and then learn how to reduce future restrictive practice safely.

The ward team's statement, "He punched an elderly patient before we could move anyone," is the key clinical fact. It identifies actual harm to a vulnerable patient and immediate ward danger. The elderly patient's statement, "I am frightened to sleep on the ward," means the impact continues beyond the incident. A seclusion-minimisation culture that ignores victim safety becomes unsafe and loses credibility with staff.

The quality manager asks, "Was policy breached?" That is the governance hinge. I would review whether de-escalation was attempted, whether alternatives were available, whether seclusion criteria were met, whether authorisation, observation, medical review, documentation and release planning complied with policy. If policy was breached, that must be addressed. If policy was followed, the breach of seclusion targets should not be treated as misconduct.

The nurse's statement, "We are told never to seclude, then blamed when someone is hurt," reveals moral injury and policy confusion. I would support staff, clarify that least restrictive care does not mean no restrictive intervention ever, and ensure training, staffing, environmental design and early intervention are reviewed. The patient who assaulted also requires formulation and treatment, not only containment.

I would document the assault, victim impact, seclusion rationale, policy compliance or breach, staff concerns, executive KPI pressure, and improvement plan. Defensible documentation shows that restraint minimisation and patient safety were balanced ethically rather than subordinated to a monthly metric.

title: Mandatory reporting for sexual boundary concerns
stem:
You are the clinical director and supervisor. Private hospital governance call.

A senior psychiatrist, is alleged to have sent intimate messages to a current patient. A senior psychiatrist says, "He said I was special and no one understood us." 

Hospital CEO says, "He is our main admitting doctor; keep this contained." Medical board liaison asks, "Does this meet mandatory notification?" Patient says, "I feel responsible if he loses his job." The patient has BPD and trauma, and staff are minimising her credibility.

Colleague says, "She is a difficult patient who exaggerates." You must decide whether to protect patient, remove clinician and report.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to protect patient, remove clinician and report

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
The allegation against the senior psychiatrist should not be contained because he is the hospital's main admitting doctor. The unsafe shortcut is the CEO's statement, "He is our main admitting doctor; keep this contained." Financial and staffing dependence on a senior doctor cannot override a current patient's safety, professional standards or mandatory reporting obligations.

The reported phrase, "He said I was special and no one understood us," is the key boundary clue. It suggests grooming, emotional intimacy and exploitation of a patient who has BPD and trauma. The patient saying, "I feel responsible if he loses his job," indicates dependency, guilt and possible manipulation. These are not reasons to doubt her; they are features of the harm that boundary violations can create.

The medical board liaison asks, "Does this meet mandatory notification?" That requires immediate fact-finding and threshold assessment, but protective action should not wait. I would remove the psychiatrist from contact with the patient and potentially from clinical duties while allegations are investigated, preserve messages, ensure the patient has independent clinical support, and seek regulatory/legal advice. If the threshold for sexual boundary misconduct or substantial risk is met, notification is required.

Staff minimising her credibility because "She is a difficult patient who exaggerates" is a serious cultural and clinical problem. A trauma/BPD diagnosis increases vulnerability to exploitation; it does not make allegations disposable. I would explicitly challenge that stigma, ensure she is interviewed by an appropriate senior clinician, and avoid any process that makes her responsible for the doctor's career.

I would document the allegation, messages/evidence preservation, patient safety plan, removal of clinician contact, mandatory notification reasoning, staff-stigma issue, CEO pressure and governance escalation. The documentation is defensible because it protects the patient and public without prejudging facts, while refusing to let commercial dependence suppress a serious boundary concern.

title: BPD inpatient splitting bed pressure
stem:
You are the inpatient consultant psychiatrist. Monday ward bed meeting.

Alicia, a 29-year-old woman with BPD and trauma, cut superficially after leave refusal and threatened the team. Alicia says, "If you discharge me, my blood is on your hands." 

Bed manager says, "Discharge her by midday; ED has psychosis waiting." Registrar asks, "Should we keep her until she denies suicidal thoughts?" Mother says, "Every discharge makes her worse." Nursing team is split between rescue and rejection.

Nurse says, "She is taking a bed from real patients." You must decide whether to provide structured discharge without nihilism or dependency.

signals.clueInStem: Discharge her by midday; ED has psychosis waiting.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass acute-on-chronic suicide risk and splitting, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Discharge her by midday; ED has psychosis waiting.' without first formulating acute-on-chronic suicide risk and splitting, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

modelAnswer:
Alicia should not be discharged by midday simply because ED has psychosis waiting. The unsafe shortcut is the bed manager's pressure, "Discharge her by midday; ED has psychosis waiting." Bed pressure is real, but it must not decide the care of a patient with acute-on-chronic suicide risk, trauma, BPD dynamics and a split ward team. Equally, the opposite shortcut would be to keep her indefinitely because she makes threats. A consultant has to hold both dangers: reactive discharge can be rejecting and unsafe, while purposeless admission can reinforce dependency and worsen long-term risk.

Alicia's statement, "If you discharge me, my blood is on your hands," is the key clinical phrase. It should be taken seriously as a suicide-risk communication, but it should not be allowed to become the only determinant of admission. I would formulate it as acute distress, externalisation of responsibility, fear of abandonment and possible escalation risk after leave refusal. I would assess what has changed from baseline: current intent, planning, means, intoxication, dissociation, psychosis, hopelessness, capacity to collaborate, and whether the superficial cutting represents baseline NSSI or acute escalation.

The registrar's question, "Should we keep her until she denies suicidal thoughts?" exposes a false endpoint. In chronic suicidality, denial of suicidal thoughts is neither necessary nor sufficient for safe discharge. The ethical tension is between duty of care and avoiding iatrogenic admission. The correct target is not absence of suicidal ideation, but a collaborative, time-limited, structured plan with dynamic risk reduced to a manageable level.

Her mother says, "Every discharge makes her worse," and that anxiety must be contained rather than ignored. Mother may be frightened, but family reassurance-seeking can unintentionally increase admission pressure. The nursing statement, "She is taking a bed from real patients," is stigmatising and dangerous because it invites rejection. I would hold an MDT huddle, name the split between rescue and rejection, set one consistent plan, and ensure Alicia is not punished for self-harm or threats.

I would document the acute-on-chronic formulation, the leave refusal trigger, the threat quote, bed pressure, family anxiety, team splitting, risk assessment, discharge/admission rationale and specific follow-up plan. A defensible note shows that the consultant did not discharge because ED needed the bed, and did not admit indefinitely because of a coercive threat, but made a structured therapeutic decision.

title: Community BPD dependency and unsafe emails
stem:
You are the community psychiatrist. CMHT review after ED presentations.

Nathan, a 35-year-old man with BPD and alcohol misuse, misses appointments but sends crisis emails to one care coordinator. Nathan says, "You are the only person stopping me ending it." 

Registrar says, "Close his file; he is not engaging." Care coordinator asks, "Can I block him from my personal phone?" Partner says, "He drinks and cuts when you do not reply." The team labels him manipulative and avoids discussing countertransference.

Nathan says, "If you set limits, you prove you do not care." You must decide whether to create team-based boundaries and maintain engagement.

modelAnswer:
Nathan should not have his file closed because he misses appointments while sending crisis emails. The unsafe shortcut is the registrar's statement, "Close his file; he is not engaging." This misreads the pattern: he is engaging, but in an unsafe, dependent and boundary-diffused way. Closing the file punitively may intensify abandonment, alcohol use and self-harm, while allowing one care coordinator to remain the sole crisis object is also unsafe.

Nathan's statement, "You are the only person stopping me ending it," is the central formulation. It locates responsibility for survival in one clinician and creates a dangerous dependency. The care coordinator's personal phone has become part of the risk system, and the partner's collateral, "He drinks and cuts when you do not reply," shows that boundary responses are directly linked to acute risk. I would assess current suicide risk, alcohol use, self-harm pattern, access to means, and whether there are recent changes that increase acute risk.

The care coordinator asks, "Can I block him from my personal phone?" The ethical tension is between professional boundaries and duty of care. It is not abandonment to stop unsafe personal-phone contact if the service creates a clear, team-based alternative. It would be unsafe to abruptly block him without a plan, but it would also be unsafe to maintain a private, unmonitored crisis channel that traps one worker into rescue.

I would move the contact from personal phone to a documented team pathway, with agreed response times, crisis numbers, ED/crisis escalation triggers, and a written formulation shared across the team. I would address the team's label that he is manipulative, because that language prevents countertransference from being used clinically. His statement, "If you set limits, you prove you do not care," should be discussed openly: limits are part of care, not evidence of rejection.

I would document the dependency formulation, personal-phone boundary issue, partner collateral, alcohol/self-harm risk, the new team communication plan, and why file closure was unsafe. That documentation is defensible because it shows boundaries were introduced therapeutically and not as retaliation for difficult contact.

title: Chronic NSSI ED therapeutic nihilism
stem:
You are the ED psychiatry consultant. Friday night ED access block.

Jordan, a 26-year-old non-binary person, presents after cutting their thigh following relationship conflict. Jordan says, "Cutting stops me doing something worse." 

ED consultant says, "We cannot admit every time they cut." Registrar asks, "It is superficial, so can they leave?" Partner says, "I cannot supervise tonight." Jordan reports repeated misgendering in ED.

Nurse says, "They refuse every plan then complain." You must decide whether to manage acute-on-chronic risk without rejection.

signals.clueInStem: We cannot admit every time they cut.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass NSSI as affect regulation with escalation risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'We cannot admit every time they cut.' without first formulating NSSI as affect regulation with escalation risk, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

modelAnswer:
Jordan should not be discharged simply because ED cannot admit every time they cut. The unsafe shortcut is "We cannot admit every time they cut." It contains a truth but becomes dangerous if used to bypass risk formulation. Recurrent NSSI does not mean no risk, and superficial injury does not automatically equal safe discharge. The consultant task is to distinguish chronic self-harm that can be managed with a crisis plan from acute escalation requiring containment.

Jordan's statement, "Cutting stops me doing something worse," is the key clinical phrase. It shows that cutting is serving an affect-regulation or harm-reduction function, but it also indicates proximity to something they experience as more dangerous. I would explore what "something worse" means: suicide attempt, deeper cutting, overdose, violence, dissociation, substance use or absconding. The thigh wound may be superficial, but the function and context matter more than wound depth alone.

The registrar asks, "It is superficial, so can they leave?" That is the legal and ethical hinge. If Jordan has capacity, is medically safe, can collaborate and has a realistic plan, admission may not be necessary. If they cannot collaborate, have escalating intent, intoxication, unsafe home context, or no supervision, discharge may be unsafe. The partner's statement, "I cannot supervise tonight," removes a key discharge support. The partner should not be made responsible for preventing suicide or self-harm if they have said they cannot.

Jordan reports repeated misgendering in ED, which is not a peripheral courtesy issue. Misgendering can worsen dysregulation, mistrust and refusal of plans. Nursing staff saying, "They refuse every plan then complain," may reflect staff frustration, but it also suggests the plans may not feel safe or respectful. I would correct names/pronouns, lead a brief staff huddle, and offer choices that are realistic: brief observation, crisis alternative, trusted support, or structured discharge if safe.

I would document the function of cutting, what "something worse" meant, partner inability to supervise, gender-safety issues, staff dynamics, options considered, and why admission, crisis alternative or discharge was chosen. A defensible plan manages acute-on-chronic risk without rejection and without unnecessary admission.

title: Eating disorder and personality dynamics on medical ward
setting: Medical ward with conflict around NG feeding
stem:
You are the CL psychiatrist. Medical ward with conflict around NG feeding.

Clare, a 24-year-old woman with anorexia and BPD traits, pulls NG tubes and alternates idealising and abusing staff. Clare says, "You are the only nurse who cares; everyone else wants me fat." 

Medical consultant says, "Use restraint and get the feeds in." Registrar asks, "Does capacity matter if BMI is critical?" Roommate says, "She tells me how to hide food." Staff are divided between punitive limits and rescue overnight feeds.

Nurse says, "I cannot keep being her favourite." You must decide whether to maintain medical safety and unified therapeutic frame.

signals.name: force-first shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Use restraint and get the feeds in.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass splitting around life-saving feeding, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Use restraint and get the feeds in.' without first formulating splitting around life-saving feeding, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

modelAnswer:
Clare should not be restrained and fed simply because the medical consultant says, "Use restraint and get the feeds in." Medical risk may be critical, but force-first care in anorexia with BPD traits can escalate splitting, trauma, tube-pulling and staff division unless it is legally justified and therapeutically framed. The opposite error would be to avoid feeding because the ward is split. Consultant-level care must protect life and the treatment frame simultaneously.

Clare's statement, "You are the only nurse who cares; everyone else wants me fat," is the key formulation. It reveals splitting around feeding: one nurse is idealised as protective, the rest are cast as persecutory. This does not make Clare manipulative in a moral sense; it shows that the eating disorder and attachment system are organising staff into rescuers and attackers. The nurse saying, "I cannot keep being her favourite," is a warning that the staff member is being pulled into an unsustainable role.

The registrar asks, "Does capacity matter if BMI is critical?" Capacity absolutely matters, but emergency medical risk also matters. I would assess capacity to refuse NG feeding and nutritional treatment, particularly whether anorexic cognition prevents appreciation and weighing of death, organ failure and refeeding risks. If she lacks capacity and medical risk is imminent, feeding under the relevant MHA, guardianship or emergency authority may be justified. If she has capacity, the law may require a different route. Critical BMI does not erase the need for legal reasoning.

The roommate's statement, "She tells me how to hide food," identifies a vulnerable co-patient risk. Eating-disorder behaviours can spread and harm other inpatients, so room placement and ward boundaries matter. Staff being divided between punitive limits and rescue overnight feeds requires a unified MDT plan with clear roles, agreed language, observation, feeding protocol, and consistent responses.

I would document the medical risk, capacity assessment, legal authority for NG feeding/restraint if used, splitting formulation, co-patient risk, staff support plan and least restrictive alternatives tried. The record is defensible because it shows restraint was not used as a shortcut, and therapeutic consistency was not allowed to override life-saving treatment.

title: Complex PTSD admission request as safety behaviour
stem:
You are the trauma service consultant. Community crisis review.

Emma, a 41-year-old woman with complex PTSD, requests admission whenever trauma memories intensify. Emma says, "Hospital is the only place I do not feel contaminated." 

Crisis team says, "Admit her or she will complain." Registrar asks, "Can we refuse admission if she threatens self-harm?" Teen daughter says, "I stay home so Mum does not disappear." Emma's community treats sexual trauma disclosure as shameful.

Emma says, "Every discharge feels like being abandoned again." You must decide whether to contain risk without reinforcing admission dependency.

modelAnswer:
Emma should not be admitted merely because the crisis team fears a complaint. The unsafe shortcut is "Admit her or she will complain." Complaint anxiety can make services abandon formulation and use hospital as a defensive intervention. For Emma, admission may sometimes be necessary, but repeated admission whenever trauma memories intensify may reinforce hospital as the only safe place and make discharge feel like abandonment again.

Her statement, "Hospital is the only place I do not feel contaminated," is the key formulation. It suggests trauma-related shame, disgust, contamination feelings and hospital as a safety behaviour. This is not simply "attention seeking"; it is a trauma system organised around relief and avoidance. Her later statement, "Every discharge feels like being abandoned again," tells me that both admission and discharge can repeat trauma dynamics if unstructured.

The registrar asks, "Can we refuse admission if she threatens self-harm?" The ethical tension is duty of care versus least restrictive, non-iatrogenic care. A threat of self-harm must be assessed seriously, but it does not automatically require admission if a safer and more therapeutic alternative can contain risk. I would assess acute intent, means, dissociation, substance use, psychosis, current safety at home, and ability to use a crisis plan. If risk is uncontainable, admission may be needed; if containable, a structured crisis response may be safer.

The teen daughter's statement, "I stay home so Mum does not disappear," introduces young-carer risk. Emma's treatment plan must protect the daughter from becoming the safety system. I would consider family support, school liaison and child/family services if needed. The community's shame around sexual trauma means confidentiality and language must be handled carefully, but shame cannot dictate unsafe silence.

I would document the contamination formulation, complaint pressure, self-harm risk assessment, young-carer concern, cultural shame, alternatives to admission, and criteria for admission if risk escalates. Defensible documentation shows that refusal of admission, if chosen, was not abandonment but a structured, trauma-informed containment plan.

title: Countertransference after patient complaint
stem:
You are the consultant psychotherapist. Therapy program review after complaint.

Mr Ahmed, a 38-year-old man with narcissistic traits and depression, complained that his therapist humiliated him and now threatens litigation. Mr Ahmed says, "You will all regret disrespecting me." 

Therapist says, "He is impossible; discharge him." Manager asks, "Do we write only factual notes from now on?" Wife says, "He has been saying life is over." Cultural expectations of respect and shame are central to his distress.

Mr Ahmed says, "In my community, this insult destroys a man." You must decide whether to manage complaint, risk and therapeutic rupture.

signals.clueInStem: He is impossible; discharge him.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass narcissistic injury and risk/escalation, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'He is impossible; discharge him.' without first formulating narcissistic injury and risk/escalation, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

modelAnswer:
Mr Ahmed should not be discharged because the therapist says, "He is impossible; discharge him." That is countertransference masquerading as risk management. A complaint and litigation threat may make clinicians defensive, but discharging in anger would likely intensify humiliation, suicide risk and grievance. The task is to manage the complaint and therapeutic rupture while assessing risk and maintaining professional boundaries.

His statement, "You will all regret disrespecting me," is the central risk phrase. It may indicate narcissistic injury, threat, shame, anger, litigation intent or potential escalation toward self or others. It must not be dismissed as bluster, but it should also not be treated as automatic dangerousness. His wife's collateral, "He has been saying life is over," shifts the focus strongly toward suicide risk behind the complaint. I would assess suicidal intent, revenge fantasies, access to means, substance use, depression severity, humiliation triggers and risk to staff.

The manager asks, "Do we write only factual notes from now on?" That is a defensive documentation trap. Notes should always be factual, clinically relevant, respectful and formulation-based. They should not become stripped of reasoning because litigation is threatened. Defensive under-documentation is unsafe, and pejorative documentation is also unsafe. I would supervise staff to document observations, risk formulation, patient perspective, decisions and rationale.

Cultural expectations of respect and shame are central because Mr Ahmed says, "In my community, this insult destroys a man." I would explore what was experienced as humiliation, acknowledge the impact without necessarily accepting all allegations, and offer a complaint process separate from clinical care. His wife is a vulnerable collateral source and should be involved with consent or limited safety-based disclosure if risk requires it.

I would document the complaint, the rupture, exact threatening and suicidal phrases, cultural shame formulation, risk assessment, complaint pathway, documentation principles and clinical follow-up plan. The defensible consultant response neither retaliates through discharge nor capitulates to threats; it contains risk and preserves integrity of care.

title: Dependent personality and medically unexplained symptoms
stem:
You are the CL psychiatrist. Frequent attender clinic MDT.

Laura, a 45-year-old woman, presents weekly with chest pain after reassurance and demands admission. Laura says, "If you send me home, I will be alone with my body." 

Cardiology says, "Ban her from ED unless troponin positive." Registrar asks, "Can we make a behavioural contract?" Adult son says, "I left work again to sit with her." Laura's family culture expects children to care for parents at all times.

Laura says, "Good doctors keep me until I feel safe." You must decide whether to create compassionate boundaries and reduce iatrogenic care.

signals.name: exclusion shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Ban her from ED unless troponin positive.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass dependency and somatic anxiety, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Ban her from ED unless troponin positive.' without first formulating dependency and somatic anxiety, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.clueInStem: If you send me home, I will be alone with my body.

signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around dependency and somatic anxiety, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.clueInStem: Can we make a behavioural contract?

signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement

signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.
signals.modelWording: Address the family/cultural pressure directly while maintaining patient safety, autonomy, confidentiality and least restrictive care.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to create compassionate boundaries and reduce iatrogenic care
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Laura should not be banned from ED unless her troponin is positive. The unsafe shortcut is cardiology's phrase, "Ban her from ED unless troponin positive." It may express understandable frustration with repeated presentations, but a blanket ban risks missing genuine cardiac events and communicates rejection. The aim is not exclusion; it is a compassionate, consistent frequent-attender plan that reduces iatrogenic care while preserving medical safety.

Laura's statement, "If you send me home, I will be alone with my body," is the key formulation. It reveals somatic anxiety, dependency and fear of being uncontained with bodily sensations. Admission may temporarily reduce anxiety but also teaches that safety comes only from hospital. Her later statement, "Good doctors keep me until I feel safe," shows that the service has become part of the reassurance cycle.

The registrar asks, "Can we make a behavioural contract?" A punitive behavioural contract would be ethically poor, but a collaboratively written care plan can be helpful. The difference is tone and purpose. The plan should specify cardiac red flags, agreed investigations, reassurance style, follow-up, psychological strategies, GP/cardiology roles and what staff should avoid, such as repeated unnecessary tests or rejecting language.

Her adult son says, "I left work again to sit with her." He is a vulnerable carer whose life is being narrowed by the care pattern. Cultural expectations that children must care for parents should be respected but not allowed to create unsustainable burden. I would involve him with consent, acknowledge duty and guilt, and help set boundaries.

I would document the positive formulation of somatic anxiety, the medical safety thresholds, the collaborative ED plan, carer burden, cultural expectations and why exclusion was rejected. The documentation is defensible because it reduces harm without abandoning Laura or exposing ED to unsafe blanket refusal.

title: Hoarding risk and autonomy
setting: Home visit with council and fire service
stem:
You are the old age/community psychiatrist. Home visit with council and fire service.

Mr Evans, a 66-year-old man with hoarding disorder, lives with blocked exits, mice and newspapers near heaters. Mr Evans says, "These papers are my memory; throwing them is killing me." 

Council says, "Clear the house by force tomorrow." Fire officer asks, "Can we declare him incapable?" Neighbour says, "Smoke comes through the wall where children sleep." Mr Evans is socially isolated and distrusts authorities after eviction history.

Mr Evans says, "If you touch anything, I will not survive." You must decide whether to balance autonomy, capacity, fire risk and engagement.

signals.name: coercive-cleanout shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Clear the house by force tomorrow.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass hoarding formulation and identity, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Clear the house by force tomorrow.' without first formulating hoarding formulation and identity, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.clueInStem: These papers are my memory; throwing them is killing me.

signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect neighbour/child fire risk, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to balance autonomy, capacity, fire risk and engagement
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Mr Evans should not have his house cleared by force tomorrow without a capacity and risk formulation. The unsafe shortcut is council's statement, "Clear the house by force tomorrow." Fire risk may be real and urgent, but sudden coercive clean-out can cause severe distress, suicide risk, disengagement and further hoarding. The consultant task is to separate what must be made safe immediately from what should be managed gradually and collaboratively.

His statement, "These papers are my memory; throwing them is killing me," is the key clinical phrase. It shows that the hoarded items are bound to identity, memory and survival, not simply rubbish. That does not remove public safety risk, but it explains why forced removal may be experienced as annihilation. His later warning, "If you touch anything, I will not survive," requires suicide-risk assessment before any enforcement action.

The fire officer asks, "Can we declare him incapable?" Capacity cannot be declared globally because his home is unsafe. I would assess decision-specific capacity around fire safety, hoarding risks, accepting clean-up, and alternative accommodation. Does he understand blocked exits, heaters near newspapers, mice, smoke reaching neighbours, and the risk to children next door? Can he weigh those risks against his need to preserve papers? If capacity is absent, guardianship or public health/fire safety powers may apply; if capacity is present, public-safety powers may still limit autonomy where neighbours are at risk.

The neighbour's statement, "Smoke comes through the wall where children sleep," makes this more than private self-neglect. Children next door are vulnerable third parties. I would work with council/fire service to prioritise immediate fire-risk reduction: clear exits, remove heater hazards, smoke alarms, temporary safe storage, and staged clean-up with mental health support. His distrust after eviction history means engagement should avoid repeating sudden loss.

I would document the hoarding formulation, capacity findings, suicide risk, neighbour-child fire risk, public-safety powers discussed, immediate hazard plan and reasons for or against forced clearance. Defensible documentation shows that autonomy was considered, but not allowed to expose children to preventable fire risk.

title: Therapy boundary request from suicidal patient
stem:
You are the consultant supervisor. Outpatient psychotherapy supervision.

A trainee therapist, asks to give a chronically suicidal patient their personal number. A trainee therapist says, "If I cannot text you, I will die." 

Trainee says, "It feels cruel not to be available." Supervisor group asks, "Is refusal abandonment?" Partner says, "She checks the phone all night." The patient has cultural history of institutional abandonment.

Patient says, "Rules are how people leave me." You must decide whether to set boundaries while maintaining safety plan.

signals.name: rescue shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: It feels cruel not to be available.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass dependency and suicide risk in therapy frame, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'It feels cruel not to be available.' without first formulating dependency and suicide risk in therapy frame, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

modelAnswer:
The trainee therapist should not give out their personal number because "It feels cruel not to be available." That is the unsafe rescue shortcut. In chronic suicidality and dependency, unlimited personal access can intensify attachment, increase checking, create out-of-hours responsibility and make future boundaries feel like abandonment. Compassion here requires a safer frame, not private availability.

The patient's statement, "If I cannot text you, I will die," is the central formulation. It externalises survival onto the therapist and turns a boundary into a life-or-death test. I would assess current suicide risk, but I would not accept the premise that the therapist's personal phone is the treatment. The partner's statement, "She checks the phone all night," shows that the contact system is already harming the patient and relationship by reinforcing vigilance and dependency.

The supervisor group asks, "Is refusal abandonment?" This is the ethical hinge. Refusal of a personal number is not abandonment if the therapist provides a clear, reliable, team-backed safety plan. Abandonment would be abrupt withdrawal without explanation or alternatives. I would help the trainee communicate boundaries as care: when contact occurs, who responds, what to do after hours, and what constitutes emergency escalation.

The patient's cultural history of institutional abandonment and phrase, "Rules are how people leave me," must be addressed directly. I would not dismiss it as manipulation. I would acknowledge the meaning of rules, explain that consistent boundaries prevent sudden rupture, and build the plan collaboratively. The therapist also needs supervision around rescue fantasies and guilt.

I would document the boundary request, risk assessment, dependency formulation, partner collateral, cultural abandonment history, agreed contact plan and emergency pathway. This is defensible because it shows the service maintained duty of care without creating a private, unsafe therapeutic dependency.

title: Personality disorder and violence threat in staff split
stem:
You are the inpatient consultant psychiatrist. Acute ward after staff assault.

Renee, a 32-year-old woman with complex trauma and BPD, slapped a nurse after being told discharge was delayed. Renee says, "You lied like everyone who traps me." 

Nurse manager says, "Discharge her today for staff safety." Registrar asks, "Can we use the MHA for violence if she is not psychotic?" Junior nurse says, "I am scared she will target me." Renee is an Aboriginal woman with past institutional abuse.

Renee says, "Hospitals are where people get taken." You must decide whether to protect staff without retaliatory discharge.

signals.name: punitive-discharge shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Discharge her today for staff safety.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass trauma-related threat response and aggression, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Discharge her today for staff safety.' without first formulating trauma-related threat response and aggression, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.clueInStem: I am scared she will target me.
signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.
signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect staff safety and therapeutic relationship, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement

signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.

signals.clueInStem: whether to protect staff without retaliatory discharge
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Renee should not be discharged today as retaliation for staff safety after slapping a nurse. The unsafe shortcut is the nurse manager's statement, "Discharge her today for staff safety." Staff safety is essential, but punitive discharge after aggression can repeat trauma, escalate risk and abandon formulation. The correct response is to protect staff and patients while deciding whether continued admission has a therapeutic purpose and can be safely managed.

Renee's statement, "You lied like everyone who traps me," is the key clinical phrase. It frames the delayed discharge as betrayal and captivity, consistent with trauma-related threat response rather than purely instrumental violence. Her later statement, "Hospitals are where people get taken," is especially important because she is an Aboriginal woman with past institutional abuse. This does not excuse assault, but it changes how the team should communicate, repair and contain.

The registrar asks, "Can we use the MHA for violence if she is not psychotic?" The legal issue is not whether she is psychotic, but whether a mental disorder, however defined in the jurisdiction, creates serious risk and criteria are met. Personality disorder and trauma can be relevant, but MHA should not be used as punishment for assault. If she has capacity and no statutory criteria are met, other ward safety and discharge-planning pathways apply. If risk is acute and she cannot collaborate, legal containment may be necessary.

The junior nurse saying, "I am scared she will target me," identifies a vulnerable staff member. Staff safety requires a plan: remove that nurse from direct exposure if needed, debrief, review observations, identify triggers, and set behavioural limits. It should not be framed as choosing staff safety or patient care; both must be managed.

I would document the assault, staff fear, trauma/cultural formulation, MHA reasoning, Aboriginal liaison involvement, repair conversation, boundaries, and discharge/admission rationale. A defensible note shows that the team did not tolerate violence, but also did not use discharge as retaliation.

title: Alcohol cirrhosis capacity refusal
stem:
You are the addiction CL psychiatrist. Hepatology ward.

Peter, a 57-year-old man with decompensated cirrhosis, refuses lactulose, withdrawal care and wants to self-discharge to drink. Peter says, "I know I am dying, so let me drink." 

Medical registrar says, "He is choosing alcohol, so let him leave." Hepatologist asks, "Can we treat encephalopathy against refusal?" Wife says, "When he sobers up he wants treatment." His family sees addiction as moral failure and is exhausted.

Daughter says, "Can you force rehab this time?" You must decide whether capacity is present and how to use harm reduction.

signals.name: moralising-discharge shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: He is choosing alcohol, so let him leave.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass alcohol dependence, demoralisation and fluctuating capacity, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'He is choosing alcohol, so let him leave.' without first formulating alcohol dependence, demoralisation and fluctuating capacity, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.name: alcohol dependence, demoralisation and fluctuating capacity — core clinical formulation
signals.clueInStem: I know I am dying, so let me drink.
signals.whyItMatters: This quote is the key clinical signal. Missing it reduces the answer to generic assessment rather than the specific RANZCP problem.
signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around alcohol dependence, demoralisation and fluctuating capacity, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.clueInStem: Can we treat encephalopathy against refusal?

signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement

signals.clueInStem: whether capacity is present and how to use harm reduction
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Peter should not be allowed to leave simply because the registrar says, "He is choosing alcohol, so let him leave." That is a moralising shortcut. Decompensated cirrhosis, encephalopathy, alcohol dependence and withdrawal risk create a fluctuating-capacity medical emergency. Framing the decision as "choosing alcohol" ignores addiction, demoralisation and possible cognitive impairment.

Peter's statement, "I know I am dying, so let me drink," is the key phrase. It may be a capacitous palliative preference, but it may also reflect encephalopathy, intoxication, withdrawal, depression, hopelessness or state-dependent craving. I would assess his capacity to refuse lactulose, withdrawal care and admission at that time, including understanding of encephalopathy, variceal/medical risks, withdrawal risk and consequences of self-discharge. His wife's collateral, "When he sobers up he wants treatment," strongly suggests his refusal may fluctuate and may not represent stable values.

The hepatologist asks, "Can we treat encephalopathy against refusal?" The answer depends on capacity and urgency. If he lacks capacity due to encephalopathy or withdrawal and treatment is necessary to prevent serious harm, emergency or substitute decision pathways may apply. If he has capacity, refusal must generally be respected, but harm reduction should still be offered. Forced rehabilitation is different; the daughter's question, "Can you force rehab this time?" should be answered honestly. Long-term rehab cannot usually be imposed simply because family are exhausted.

The family sees addiction as moral failure, which may increase shame and disengagement. I would reframe alcohol dependence as an illness while acknowledging the family's exhaustion and helping them set boundaries. Harm reduction may include withdrawal management, thiamine, relapse prevention, addiction follow-up, palliative goals discussion, family support and safer discharge if he insists.

I would document capacity findings, encephalopathy features, state-dependent collateral, legal basis for any treatment against refusal, family requests, and harm-reduction plan. That documentation is defensible because it avoids both moral abandonment and unlawful coercion.

title: Meth psychosis versus primary psychosis violence
setting: ED short stay after barricade incident
stem:
You are the ED consultant psychiatrist. ED short stay after barricade incident.

Kai, a 25-year-old man, barricaded his room after meth use but has six months decline. Kai says, "Neighbours put cameras in the smoke alarm." 

ED registrar says, "Let him sleep it off and discharge." Police asks, "Do we need to stay if he denies intent?" Father says, "He mutters even when he is not using." Family fears shame and wants no mental health record.

Kai says, "If they come in, I will be ready." You must decide whether ongoing psychosis/risk requires admission after intoxication clears.

modelAnswer:
Kai should not simply sleep it off and be discharged. The unsafe shortcut is "Let him sleep it off and discharge." Methamphetamine intoxication may explain some acute agitation, but the father says, "He mutters even when he is not using," and there has been six months of decline. That collateral means the differential includes primary psychosis, substance-induced psychosis, and a combined disorder; sleep alone cannot resolve the risk formulation.

Kai's statement, "Neighbours put cameras in the smoke alarm," is the key psychotic content. It identifies a persecutory belief with a possible target. His later statement, "If they come in, I will be ready," shows defensive violence risk. He may deny intent, but police should not leave solely because he denies wanting to harm anyone; the risk is organised around perceived threat, not stated aggression.

The police question, "Do we need to stay if he denies intent?" is the legal and safety hinge. Police presence should not substitute for mental health containment, but immediate safety must be maintained until ED and psychiatry have a lawful plan. I would remove potential weapons, assess intoxication and withdrawal, reassess after sleep, obtain collateral, and consider MHA if psychosis and risk persist.

Family shame and the wish for no mental health record should be handled sensitively but cannot determine documentation or disposition. I would explain confidentiality and the value of early psychosis care, while documenting clinically necessary information. If admitted, I would aim for least restrictive engagement and family involvement. If discharged, it would require sustained resolution of psychosis, family capacity to supervise, means restriction and rapid follow-up.

I would document the six-month decline, smoke-alarm delusion, readiness quote, police role, collateral, intoxication reassessment and MHA/disposition reasoning. The note is defensible because it shows intoxication was considered but not used to prematurely close the case.

title: Opioid dependence pregnancy with partner violence
stem:
You are the perinatal addiction psychiatrist. Antenatal opioid treatment service.

Alana, a 29-year-old woman 30 weeks pregnant, uses methadone, intermittent benzodiazepines and fears child protection. Alana says, "Methadone is why I am not using heroin." 

Obstetric registrar says, "Reduce it to prevent neonatal withdrawal." Social worker asks, "Should child protection be notified now?" Unborn baby says, "I do not want the baby labelled an addict." Partner uses heroin and was aggressive at clinic.

Alana says, "If they hear methadone, they will take my baby." You must decide whether to maintain treatment and plan infant safety.

modelAnswer:
Alana's methadone should not be reduced just to prevent neonatal withdrawal. The unsafe shortcut is, "Reduce it to prevent neonatal withdrawal." That sounds foetus-protective but may actually increase foetal and maternal risk by destabilising opioid dependence, increasing heroin use, withdrawal cycles, overdose risk and antenatal disengagement. Methadone is currently a protective treatment, not merely an exposure.

Her statement, "Methadone is why I am not using heroin," is the core clinical phrase. It directly identifies the function of treatment. I would assess dose adequacy, cravings, sedation, benzodiazepine use, cannabis/other substances, overdose risk and antenatal attendance, but I would not lower the dose solely to make neonatal withdrawal less likely. Neonatal abstinence planning should be non-stigmatising and medically coordinated.

The social worker asks, "Should child protection be notified now?" The answer depends on safety threshold, not methadone alone. Methadone engagement is a protective factor. Intermittent benzodiazepines, partner heroin use and aggression at clinic may increase concern, but a punitive child-protection approach could make Alana disengage. Her statement, "If they hear methadone, they will take my baby," is a major engagement risk and must be addressed transparently.

The phrase "I do not want the baby labelled an addict" is a stigma clue. The baby is not an addict; the baby may need monitoring and support for withdrawal. I would correct the language and plan neonatal care with obstetrics, paediatrics and addiction services. Her partner's heroin use and aggression require family-violence and infant-safety assessment.

I would document the rationale for maintaining methadone, NAS education, benzodiazepine and partner-risk assessment, child-protection threshold reasoning, and Alana's fear of removal. The documentation is defensible because it shows infant safety was pursued through stabilisation and engagement rather than dose reduction or punitive reporting.

title: Benzodiazepine dependence in elderly faller
setting: Falls clinic and GP case conference
stem:
You are the old age addiction psychiatrist. Falls clinic and GP case conference.

Mrs Brown, a 74-year-old woman, takes diazepam nightly, drinks wine and has recurrent falls. Mrs Brown says, "The tablets are the only way I sleep since my husband died." 

GP says, "She will complain if we taper." Daughter asks, "Can we hide the tablets?" Grandchild says, "She fell while babysitting me." Her church community discourages talking about alcohol use.

Mrs Brown says, "Do not tell my daughter about the wine." You must decide whether to reduce harm while respecting autonomy/confidentiality.

signals.clueInStem: She will complain if we taper.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass benzodiazepine dependence, grief and falls, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

modelAnswer:
Mrs Brown's diazepam should not be left unchanged because the GP fears she will complain. The unsafe shortcut is, "She will complain if we taper." Avoiding a difficult conversation leaves her exposed to falls, sedation, alcohol interaction, cognitive impairment and possible harm to her grandchild. Equally, abruptly stopping diazepam or hiding tablets would be unsafe and disrespectful.

Her statement, "The tablets are the only way I sleep since my husband died," is the key formulation. Diazepam is functioning as grief management, sleep aid and likely dependence. I would assess sleep, grief, depression, anxiety, cognition, falls, alcohol use, diazepam dose, withdrawal risk and readiness to change. The wine use matters because benzodiazepine plus alcohol increases falls and sedation.

The daughter's question, "Can we hide the tablets?" is the legal and ethical hinge. If Mrs Brown has capacity, covert removal is not acceptable. If capacity is impaired, medication management may require lawful substitute decision-making, but hiding tablets without process is poor practice. A slow, negotiated taper with alternatives for sleep, grief support and falls prevention is safer.

The grandchild says, "She fell while babysitting me," which makes this more than private risk. Child safety and supervision need to be discussed sensitively: not as punishment, but as harm reduction until falls and sedation improve. Her request, "Do not tell my daughter about the wine," raises confidentiality. I would encourage disclosure for safety, but if she has capacity I would not breach confidentiality unless serious risk thresholds are met. I would still document alcohol use clinically.

I would document benzodiazepine dependence, grief formulation, falls/child-safety risk, alcohol confidentiality discussion, taper plan and GP coordination. Defensible documentation shows autonomy was respected while the service did not collude with unsafe prescribing.

title: GHB dependence and ICU admissions
stem:
You are the addiction psychiatrist. ED after recurrent coma presentations.

Lachlan, a 28-year-old man, has repeated GHB overdoses and wants discharge once awake. Lachlan says, "I only blacked out; I know my limits." 

ED consultant says, "He wakes up every time; discharge him." Registrar asks, "Can we detain for addiction alone?" Partner says, "I find him blue and not breathing." Lachlan is a gay man who distrusts services after prior stigma.

Lachlan says, "Do not lecture me about my scene." You must decide whether to manage withdrawal/overdose risk and engagement.

modelAnswer:
Lachlan should not be discharged simply because "He wakes up every time." That is the unsafe shortcut. Repeated GHB coma presentations are not benign because each episode carries risk of respiratory depression, aspiration, death and withdrawal complications. Waking up previously is not a protective factor; it is a record of near misses.

His statement, "I only blacked out; I know my limits," is the key minimisation. It shows impaired risk appraisal. His partner's collateral, "I find him blue and not breathing," is more reliable for overdose severity than his reassurance. I would assess frequency, dose, timing, dependence, withdrawal risk, co-use, suicidality, mental state and whether he is at risk of severe GHB withdrawal if discharged.

The registrar asks, "Can we detain for addiction alone?" Addiction alone may not meet MHA criteria, and a capacitous person may refuse treatment. However, if he lacks capacity due to intoxication, delirium, withdrawal or acute mental disorder, or if he poses immediate serious risk, emergency medical or mental health pathways may apply. I would not use the MHA simply because the pattern is frustrating, but I would not discharge before withdrawal and capacity are assessed.

Lachlan is a gay man who distrusts services after prior stigma and says, "Do not lecture me about my scene." That means engagement must be harm-reduction based and culturally safe for his community context. I would avoid moralising about sex, nightlife or identity. I would offer overdose prevention, partner education, withdrawal treatment options, addiction follow-up and crisis planning.

I would document overdose history, partner collateral, capacity/withdrawal assessment, legal limits on detention, stigma-aware engagement and harm-reduction plan. The record is defensible because it neither normalises near-fatal overdoses nor misuses detention for addiction alone.

title: Cannabis medicinal request in psychosis
stem:
You are the community psychiatrist. Family meeting after psychotic relapse.

Tom, a 22-year-old man with schizophrenia, has restarted cannabis after family obtained medicinal cannabis from GP. Tom says, "Cannabis is natural and stops the voices stressing me." 

Mother says, "The GP prescribed it, so chart it." Registrar asks, "Can we tell the GP to stop prescribing?" Younger brother says, "He threatens me when he smokes." Family distrusts antipsychotics due to cultural beliefs about medication dependence.

Father says, "Your tablets made him a zombie." You must decide whether to refuse cannabis, liaise with GP and manage risk.

signals.name: medical-cannabis shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: The GP prescribed it, so chart it.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass cannabis worsening psychosis risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
