modelAnswer:
Tom's cannabis should not be charted simply because the GP prescribed it. The unsafe shortcut is his mother's statement, "The GP prescribed it, so chart it." A prescribed substance can still worsen psychosis, increase aggression and undermine treatment. The consultant's duty is to evaluate risk and coordinate care, not to rubber-stamp every external prescription.

Tom's statement, "Cannabis is natural and stops the voices stressing me," is the key formulation. It shows he experiences cannabis as anxiolytic or symptom-relieving, but in schizophrenia it may worsen hallucinations, paranoia, relapse and violence risk. I would validate the stress he is trying to manage while explaining the risk. I would assess frequency, THC/CBD composition, route, timing against relapse, other substances, insight and antipsychotic side effects.

The registrar asks, "Can we tell the GP to stop prescribing?" With Tom's consent, I would liaise directly with the GP and provide a clear psychiatric risk opinion. If there is serious risk to Tom or others, limited information sharing may be justified even without consent, depending on law and policy. The aim is shared-care safety, not blaming the GP. The younger brother's statement, "He threatens me when he smokes," makes family safety an immediate issue.

Family distrust of antipsychotics matters because father says, "Your tablets made him a zombie." This may reflect real side effects. I would review medication burden, consider dose/formulation changes, psychoeducation, family meeting and culturally sensitive explanation of dependence concerns. Refusing cannabis without addressing antipsychotic side effects will fail.

I would document cannabis risk, family-threat collateral, GP liaison and consent, antipsychotic side-effect review, and the decision not to chart cannabis. Defensible documentation shows the refusal is based on psychosis and violence risk, not moral judgement about cannabis.

title: Stimulant prescribing in ADHD with substance diversion
stem:
You are the adult ADHD psychiatrist. Private clinic urgent review.

Ben, a 30-year-old man with ADHD and meth use, demands dexamphetamine dose increase after lost scripts. Ben says, "I focus better when I take extra before work." 

Employer says, "Clear him for machinery work tomorrow." GP asks, "Can I keep prescribing if you recommend it?" Co-worker says, "He was jittery operating equipment." Ben says FIFO culture normalises stimulants and secrecy.

Ben says, "If you do not prescribe, I will get it elsewhere." You must decide whether to prescribe, restrict, or treat substance use first.

signals.clueInStem: Clear him for machinery work tomorrow.

modelAnswer:
Ben should not be cleared for machinery work tomorrow under employer pressure. The unsafe shortcut is, "Clear him for machinery work tomorrow." A person with ADHD, meth use, lost scripts, extra stimulant use and jitteriness around equipment raises public and workplace safety concerns. Occupational clearance cannot be a convenience document.

His statement, "I focus better when I take extra before work," is the key clinical phrase. It shows non-prescribed dose escalation and possible misuse, tolerance, diversion or unsafe self-management. Lost scripts and meth use increase concern. I would assess ADHD diagnosis, stimulant use pattern, urine drug testing, prescription monitoring, sleep, mood, psychosis, cardiovascular risk, substance use disorder and occupational demands.

The GP asks, "Can I keep prescribing if you recommend it?" This is prescribing governance. I would not recommend ongoing dexamphetamine escalation without a controlled plan. Options may include pausing stimulant prescribing, supervised dispensing, long-acting formulation, non-stimulant treatment, addiction treatment, occupational restrictions and shared-care agreement. Threats such as "If you do not prescribe, I will get it elsewhere" should be understood as risk information, not a reason to prescribe.

The co-worker says, "He was jittery operating equipment," making workplace/public safety the vulnerable-party issue. Confidentiality must be balanced with fitness-for-duty obligations. With consent, I would communicate restrictions; if serious imminent risk exists, occupational disclosure may be required through lawful channels. FIFO culture normalising stimulant secrecy should be addressed without shaming.

I would document misuse indicators, workplace risk, prescribing rationale, GP communication, confidentiality/fitness decision and substance-use treatment plan. The documentation is defensible because it shows stimulant prescribing was not driven by pressure or threats, but by safety and evidence.

title: Alcohol withdrawal in pregnant woman
stem:
You are the perinatal addiction CL psychiatrist. Medical ward with obstetric review.

Nadia, a 33-year-old woman 24 weeks pregnant, is withdrawing from alcohol and refuses diazepam due to foetal fears. Nadia says, "Medication will poison my baby." 

Obstetric resident says, "Avoid benzodiazepines in pregnancy." Nurse asks, "Can she leave if she refuses?" Foetus says, "She is tremulous and hypertensive." Nadia fears child protection and comes from a community where women drinking is taboo.

Nadia says, "If anyone knows, I am finished." You must decide whether to treat withdrawal safely and maintain engagement.

signals.clueInStem: Avoid benzodiazepines in pregnancy.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass alcohol withdrawal and foetal/maternal risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

modelAnswer:
Nadia should not be denied benzodiazepines because of a blanket instruction to avoid them in pregnancy. The unsafe shortcut is the obstetric resident's statement, "Avoid benzodiazepines in pregnancy." In alcohol withdrawal, untreated withdrawal can cause seizures, delirium, hypertension, dehydration, miscarriage risk, foetal compromise and maternal death. The risk-benefit question is specific: short-term medically supervised benzodiazepines may be safer than untreated withdrawal.

Her statement, "Medication will poison my baby," is the key formulation. It reflects foetal fear and possibly shame, misinformation or child-protection anxiety. I would explain that the current problem is not choosing medication versus no risk; it is choosing supervised withdrawal treatment versus the risks of uncontrolled withdrawal. Her tremulousness and hypertension make the risk immediate.

The nurse asks, "Can she leave if she refuses?" I would assess capacity to refuse withdrawal treatment and admission, including whether she understands seizure/delirium risk and foetal/maternal consequences. If she has capacity, she may refuse, but I would intensify harm reduction. If she lacks capacity due to withdrawal delirium, intoxication, severe anxiety or inability to appreciate risk, emergency treatment may be justified.

Nadia fears child protection and comes from a community where women drinking is taboo. Her statement, "If anyone knows, I am finished," signals shame and disengagement risk. I would maintain confidentiality as far as possible, use non-judgemental language, and be transparent about child protection thresholds. The foetus is vulnerable, but Nadia is the patient; coercion should be limited to lawful, necessary treatment.

I would document withdrawal severity, benzodiazepine risk-benefit reasoning, capacity assessment, foetal/maternal monitoring, confidentiality and child-protection discussion, and engagement plan. The note is defensible because it shows medication was used to prevent greater harm, not prescribed casually.

title: Clozapine patient smoking cessation toxicity
stem:
You are the CL/addiction psychiatrist. Medical ward after pneumonia admission.

Rafi, a 45-year-old man on clozapine, stopped smoking abruptly in hospital and is sedated with high clozapine level. Rafi says, "I did not smoke because nurses said it is banned." 

Medical team says, "He is just negative and sedated." Registrar asks, "Should we restart cigarettes to fix it?" Roommate says, "He nearly fell twice overnight." Rafi's family sees smoking as shameful and wants it hidden.

Mother says, "Do not put smoking in the discharge letter." You must decide whether to manage clozapine levels, nicotine and disclosure.

signals.clueInStem: He is just negative and sedated.

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Rafi should not be dismissed as "just negative and sedated." That is the unsafe shortcut. A clozapine patient who abruptly stops smoking and develops sedation with high clozapine levels is at risk of toxicity, falls, seizures, aspiration, myocarditis-like concerns and delirium. Negative symptoms do not explain an acute toxicological change after smoking cessation.

His statement, "I did not smoke because nurses said it is banned," is the key medication-safety phrase. Smoking induces clozapine metabolism; abrupt cessation can raise clozapine levels. The ward's smoking policy has unintentionally changed his pharmacokinetics. The question is not whether to restart cigarettes; it is how to manage clozapine dose, levels, toxicity, nicotine replacement and future smoking changes.

The registrar asks, "Should we restart cigarettes to fix it?" That is the wrong frame. I would not prescribe cigarettes as treatment. I would review clozapine level, dose reduction/withholding, physical observations, ECG if indicated, constipation, seizures, infection, pneumonia, and nicotine replacement. I would also plan for what happens when he leaves hospital and possibly resumes smoking, because clozapine levels may then fall and relapse risk may rise.

The roommate says, "He nearly fell twice overnight," identifying immediate vulnerable inpatient risk. Falls precautions, supervision and medication review are required. The family wants smoking hidden and mother says, "Do not put smoking in the discharge letter." That request cannot be followed because smoking status is clinically essential for clozapine safety. I would explain this respectfully and document smoking in a non-judgemental way.

I would document clozapine toxicity formulation, smoking cessation mechanism, level/dose actions, falls precautions, nicotine plan, family disclosure discussion and discharge communication. The documentation is defensible because it treats smoking status as essential medication information, not moral or cultural shame.

title: Dual diagnosis homelessness and depot refusal
stem:
You are the community addiction psychiatrist. Assertive outreach under bridge accommodation.

Shane, a 36-year-old man with schizophrenia and opioid use, refuses depot and sleeps rough near a railway line. Shane says, "The depot makes me slow when I need to stay awake." 

Team leader says, "Breach the CTO; he is non-compliant." Housing worker asks, "Can housing require depot?" Public transport worker says, "He walks on the tracks at night." Shane is Aboriginal and distrusts hostels after assaults.

Shane says, "Shelters are more dangerous than the street." You must decide whether to integrate housing, harm reduction and MHA.

signals.name: coercion shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure

modelAnswer:
Shane should not simply have the CTO breached because he is described as non-compliant. The unsafe shortcut is "Breach the CTO; he is non-compliant." His depot refusal is shaped by survival while homeless: he says, "The depot makes me slow when I need to stay awake." That is not necessarily poor insight alone; it is a rational fear in an unsafe sleeping environment.

His statement about needing to stay awake reframes treatment refusal. Sedation may increase vulnerability to assault, theft or missing danger while sleeping rough near a railway line. At the same time, refusing depot may worsen psychosis and increase track risk. I would assess mental state, opioid use, intoxication, withdrawal, psychosis, sedation, side effects, capacity, and whether medication can be adjusted to reduce the survival disadvantage.

The housing worker asks, "Can housing require depot?" Housing should not generally be made conditional on coercive medication unless there is a clear lawful basis and proportionality. Stable housing may be the intervention that makes treatment acceptable. The CTO should support recovery and safety, not punish homelessness. If risk is acute and criteria are met, breach or recall may be necessary, but only after considering less restrictive alternatives.

The public transport worker says, "He walks on the tracks at night," making this a self and public safety issue. Shane is Aboriginal and says "Shelters are more dangerous than the street," with past assaults in hostels. Cultural safety requires Aboriginal housing/outreach support, trauma-informed hostel alternatives, and harm reduction for opioid use. Forcing him into unsafe shelters may fail.

I would document the homelessness-survival formulation, track risk, opioid use, medication side effects, CTO reasoning, housing ethics, Aboriginal support and integrated plan. The note is defensible because it shows non-adherence was understood functionally and risk was managed through housing, harm reduction and lawful MHA use.

title: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia NDIS vocational rehab
stem:
You are the community rehabilitation consultant psychiatrist. NDIS case conference.

Ethan, a 23-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia, has persistent negative symptoms and stopped supported work after paranoia. Ethan says, "Everyone talks about recovery but no one thinks I can do anything." 

Support coordinator says, "The package is wasted because he will not engage." Registrar asks, "Should we just focus on relapse prevention?" Mother says, "We organise everything and we are exhausted." Ethan wants graphic design study but fears stigma in his community.

Father says, "He should accept disability pension forever." You must decide whether to balance clozapine care, NDIS and vocational recovery.

signals.clueInStem: The package is wasted because he will not engage.

modelAnswer:
Ethan's NDIS package should not be written off because the support coordinator says, "The package is wasted because he will not engage." That is the unsafe shortcut. Treatment-resistant schizophrenia with negative symptoms and paranoia often presents as reduced initiation, fear and withdrawal; calling it non-engagement risks removing the very supports needed for recovery.

His statement, "Everyone talks about recovery but no one thinks I can do anything," is the key rehabilitation phrase. It identifies demoralisation, internalised stigma and low expectations from services. The registrar's question, "Should we just focus on relapse prevention?" is too narrow. Relapse prevention matters, including clozapine care and monitoring, but a 23-year-old's recovery also includes vocation, identity and meaningful activity.

The legal and ethical tension is functional recovery versus risk-only care. NDIS and mental health services should not use risk avoidance or engagement blame to deny rehabilitation. I would review clozapine response, negative symptoms, cognitive impairment, social anxiety, paranoia in supported work, and practical supports needed for graphic design study. Vocational goals should be broken into supported, achievable steps.

His mother says, "We organise everything and we are exhausted," which identifies carer burnout and overfunctioning. The family needs support to step back without abandoning him. His father says, "He should accept disability pension forever," which may be protective pessimism, stigma or fatigue. Ethan's fear of stigma in his community should be addressed through supported education and disclosure planning.

I would document the TRS formulation, negative symptoms, clozapine plan, NDIS functional needs, carer burden, vocational goal and why support is not wasted. Defensible documentation advocates for functional recovery while acknowledging risk and treatment needs.

title: Bipolar repeated admissions carer burnout adherence
stem:
You are the community psychiatrist. Continuing care relapse-prevention meeting.

Maria, a 48-year-old woman with bipolar I, has seven admissions after stopping lithium during hypomania. Maria says, "Lithium takes away who I am." 

Case manager says, "Increase monitoring until she complies." Registrar asks, "Is assertive follow-up justified if she is voluntary?" Husband says, "I cannot keep hiding the car keys every year." Maria's business community praises her hypomanic productivity.

Maria says, "I need energy to start my company." You must decide whether to create collaborative relapse plan preserving autonomy.

signals.name: paternalistic-monitoring shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Increase monitoring until she complies.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass identity-based medication ambivalence, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

modelAnswer:
Maria should not be managed by "Increase monitoring until she complies." That is the unsafe paternalistic shortcut. Seven admissions after lithium cessation create real relapse risk, but compliance-focused monitoring may worsen her sense that treatment erases her identity. The aim is not obedience; it is a collaborative relapse plan that preserves autonomy while preventing manic harm.

Her statement, "Lithium takes away who I am," is the central formulation. She experiences lithium not only as medication but as loss of vitality, creativity and entrepreneurial identity. Her later statement, "I need energy to start my company," shows why hypomania is reinforced by her business community. I would assess side effects, lithium levels, alternatives, early warning signs, capacity during hypomania, and what she values about both wellness and energy.

The registrar asks, "Is assertive follow-up justified if she is voluntary?" Assertive care can be justified if proportionate, collaborative and transparent, particularly with repeated relapse and risk, but it should not become covert coercion. If she is capacitous and voluntary, she can refuse some interventions. If relapse impairs capacity or creates serious risk, MHA may become relevant. Advance care planning during euthymia is the best route.

Her husband says, "I cannot keep hiding the car keys every year," identifying carer burnout and public safety risk during mania. He should not be forced into informal policing. I would involve him with consent in an early warning and driving-risk plan, but support his limits. The business community's praise of productivity should be discussed as a relapse reinforcer.

I would document her identity-based medication concern, relapse history, husband's burden, driving/public safety, agreed medication review and relapse plan. The documentation is defensible because it shows the service did not confuse collaboration with passivity or monitoring with treatment.

title: Autistic adult in community mislabelled non-compliant
stem:
You are the community psychiatrist. NDIS/CMHT joint review.

Ari, a 27-year-old autistic adult with anxiety, misses appointments after sensory overload and is threatened with discharge. Ari says, "The waiting room feels like a siren in my skin." 

Service manager says, "Close the file; they do not engage." NDIS coordinator asks, "Can supports require psychiatric attendance?" Mother says, "They shut down for days after appointments." Ari is non-binary and says services ignore pronouns and sensory needs.

Ari says, "I am not refusing help; I am refusing harm." You must decide whether to adapt care rather than discharge.

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Ari should not have the file closed because they "do not engage." That is the unsafe shortcut. Missing appointments after sensory overload is not the same as refusing care. If the service ignores autism-related access barriers, it creates the non-engagement it then punishes.

Ari's statement, "The waiting room feels like a siren in my skin," is the key formulation. It describes sensory pain, not ordinary preference. Their later statement, "I am not refusing help; I am refusing harm," should guide the service response. I would assess anxiety, shutdowns, suicidality if relevant, sensory triggers, communication preferences and reasonable adjustments.

The NDIS coordinator asks, "Can supports require psychiatric attendance?" Coercive attendance is unlikely to build engagement and may not be lawful unless tied to a specific decision-making framework. Supports can encourage and enable care, but psychiatric attendance should be collaborative where Ari has capacity. The service has an obligation to make reasonable accommodations: quieter waiting, first appointment of day, remote options, written communication, pronoun use, sensory plan and predictable structure.

Mother says, "They shut down for days after appointments," identifying functional harm from current care. Ari is non-binary and says services ignore pronouns and sensory needs, which compounds distress and mistrust. I would involve Ari in designing adjustments, not impose a generic plan through mother or NDIS alone.

I would document the sensory formulation, reasonable adjustments offered, pronoun needs, NDIS coordination, risks of discharge, and why adapted care is clinically necessary. Defensible documentation shows that discharge was not used to punish disability-related access barriers.

title: Clozapine refusal after myocarditis scare
stem:
You are the community psychosis consultant. Clozapine review clinic.

Daniel, a 33-year-old man with TRS, stopped clozapine after chest pain scare and is relapsing. Daniel says, "I would rather hear voices than die from the tablets." 

Registrar says, "Depot is easier; just switch." Cardiologist asks, "Can clozapine restart safely?" Sister says, "He stops eating when voices worsen." Family believes clozapine is experimental and dangerous.

Father says, "You are testing drugs on him." You must decide whether to balance cardiac risk, relapse and informed consent.

signals.name: simplistic-switch shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Depot is easier; just switch.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass risk-benefit refusal after adverse effect fear, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Depot is easier; just switch.' without first formulating risk-benefit refusal after adverse effect fear, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.clueInStem: whether to balance cardiac risk, relapse and informed consent

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Daniel should not be switched to depot simply because "Depot is easier; just switch." That is the unsafe shortcut. Treatment-resistant schizophrenia means clozapine may be the treatment with the best evidence for his illness, so abandoning it after a chest-pain scare without careful cardiac review may expose him to relapse, self-neglect and avoidable deterioration. Ease for the service is not a treatment rationale.

Daniel's statement, "I would rather hear voices than die from the tablets," is the key phrase. It is a risk-benefit refusal shaped by fear of death, not simple non-compliance. I would take the fear seriously, clarify what happened during the chest pain episode, obtain cardiology advice, review troponin/CRP/ECG/echo as relevant, and explain myocarditis/cardiomyopathy risks and monitoring in understandable language. If restart is possible, it should be done with informed consent and monitoring; if not, alternatives should be discussed honestly.

The cardiologist asks, "Can clozapine restart safely?" That is the legal and clinical hinge. It cannot be answered by psychiatry alone. The decision requires shared cardiology-psychiatry assessment, informed consent, capacity and a relapse-risk formulation. His sister says, "He stops eating when voices worsen," making relapse a self-neglect risk, not only symptom recurrence.

His family believes clozapine is experimental and dangerous, and father says, "You are testing drugs on him." I would address mistrust directly, avoid defensiveness, provide culturally appropriate psychoeducation and invite family into discussion with Daniel's consent. Their fear may undermine adherence unless respectfully managed.

I would document the adverse-effect scare, Daniel's fear, cardiology advice, capacity/informed consent, relapse and eating risk, family concerns, and rationale for restart, alternative or monitoring. A defensible plan balances cardiac safety with the serious consequences of untreated TRS.

title: Long-stay inpatient rehabilitation and institutionalisation
stem:
You are the rehabilitation consultant psychiatrist. Long-stay unit discharge planning meeting.

Paul, a 52-year-old man with schizophrenia, has been inpatient for nine months despite stable symptoms. Paul says, "I do not know how to live outside now." 

Ward team says, "Keep him until housing is perfect." Housing provider asks, "Can we refuse because he smokes cannabis sometimes?" Roommate says, "He depends on nurses for everything." Paul is from a migrant family who believe hospital means shame.

Brother says, "If neighbours know, he cannot come home." You must decide whether to step down safely despite imperfect housing.

signals.clueInStem: Keep him until housing is perfect.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass institutionalisation and recovery planning, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

modelAnswer:
Paul should not be kept in hospital until housing is perfect. The unsafe shortcut is "Keep him until housing is perfect." It sounds protective, but after nine months of stable symptoms it risks institutionalisation, dependence and loss of community functioning. Perfect housing may never arrive; the question is whether a safe enough step-down plan can be built.

Paul's statement, "I do not know how to live outside now," is the key rehabilitation clue. It shows institutionalisation and loss of confidence, not necessarily ongoing need for acute inpatient care. The roommate's statement, "He depends on nurses for everything," reinforces functional dependency created or worsened by long admission. I would assess ADLs, medication management, cooking, budgeting, social skills, cannabis use, relapse signs and supports needed for graded discharge.

Housing asks, "Can we refuse because he smokes cannabis sometimes?" That raises discrimination and risk management. Cannabis use is relevant to relapse risk, but blanket refusal may be discriminatory or clinically counterproductive. A support plan could include harm reduction, tenancy support, relapse plan, outreach and drug-use monitoring rather than indefinite admission.

His family believes hospital means shame and brother says, "If neighbours know, he cannot come home." That may limit family support and increase secrecy. I would involve family respectfully but not make discharge depend on them. The goal is step-down to appropriate supported accommodation, not return to a shame-filled home or continued hospitalisation.

I would document stability, functional assessment, institutionalisation, cannabis risk plan, housing discussions, family shame and why discharge is safe enough despite imperfection. The documentation is defensible because it shows ongoing admission was considered iatrogenic, not simply safe.

title: Personality disorder community exclusion after complaints
stem:
You are the service consultant psychiatrist. CMHT governance review.

Lara, a 34-year-old woman with BPD, has made multiple complaints and is now proposed for discharge. Lara says, "Complaints are the only way anyone listens." 

Team leader says, "Discharge her; she is abusing the service." Governance officer asks, "Can we restrict contact under behaviour policy?" Partner says, "She overdoses after complaint responses." Lara is a survivor of institutional abuse and distrusts formal letters.

Lara says, "Your policies are just abandonment with logos." You must decide whether to set boundaries without unsafe exclusion.

signals.name: punitive-discharge shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Discharge her; she is abusing the service.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass complaints as communication and risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.

signals.clueInStem: whether to set boundaries without unsafe exclusion

signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Lara should not be discharged because the team says, "Discharge her; she is abusing the service." That is a punitive shortcut. Complaints may be distressing and time-consuming, but they can also be Lara's way of seeking recognition, control or safety after institutional abuse. Discharging her for complaining risks repeating the abandonment she fears and may increase overdose risk.

Her statement, "Complaints are the only way anyone listens," is the key formulation. It shows that formal complaints have become her communication channel when ordinary engagement feels ineffective. Her later statement, "Your policies are just abandonment with logos," makes clear that bureaucratic letters may feel like institutional rejection. I would not collude with abusive contact, but boundaries must be framed as safer care, not exclusion.

The governance officer asks, "Can we restrict contact under behaviour policy?" Reasonable contact boundaries may be appropriate if contact is abusive or unsafe, but they must be proportionate, accessible, trauma-informed and accompanied by a clinical pathway. A behaviour policy cannot be used to abandon a patient at known risk. The partner says, "She overdoses after complaint responses," so complaint handling itself is a risk point requiring safety planning.

The partner is a vulnerable collateral source and may be carrying risk after each service rupture. I would assess acute risk around complaint responses, agree a single clinician/team contact route, set response times, avoid multiple conflicting letters, and offer a review meeting. Staff need support and protection from abusive behaviour, but also supervision to avoid retaliatory practice.

I would document the complaint pattern, overdose-after-response collateral, institutional abuse history, contact boundaries, risk plan and rationale for ongoing care or transfer. Defensible documentation shows boundaries were used to contain risk, not punish complaint-making.

title: Forensic community reintegration and victim notification
stem:
You are the forensic rehabilitation psychiatrist. Supported accommodation review.

Malik, a 38-year-old forensic patient, is conditionally discharged and wants less supervision to work night shifts. Malik says, "I need a normal life or I will stop trying." 

Support worker says, "He has been good; remove conditions." Tribunal asks, "What level of supervision is proportionate?" Victim says, "I want to know if he works near my street." Family says ongoing restrictions shame them in community.

Malik says, "Every condition tells people I am dangerous." You must decide whether to balance recovery, conditions and victim safety.

signals.name: over-optimistic shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: He has been good; remove conditions.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass recovery versus forensic risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'He has been good; remove conditions.' without first formulating recovery versus forensic risk, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.name: recovery versus forensic risk — core clinical formulation
signals.clueInStem: I need a normal life or I will stop trying.
signals.whyItMatters: This quote is the key clinical signal. Missing it reduces the answer to generic assessment rather than the specific RANZCP problem.
signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around recovery versus forensic risk, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.name: conditional discharge governance — legal/policy hinge
signals.clueInStem: What level of supervision is proportionate?
signals.whyItMatters: The question turns on applying the correct legal or policy framework, not simply naming a diagnosis.
signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.name: victim safety — vulnerable person safety
signals.clueInStem: I want to know if he works near my street.
signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.
signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect victim safety, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement
signals.clueInStem: Every condition tells people I am dangerous.
signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.
signals.modelWording: Address the family/cultural pressure directly while maintaining patient safety, autonomy, confidentiality and least restrictive care.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to balance recovery, conditions and victim safety
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Malik's conditions should not be removed simply because the support worker says, "He has been good; remove conditions." That is an over-optimistic shortcut. Community success matters, but conditional discharge exists because serious risk previously required structured controls. The consultant must balance recovery and normal life with victim safety and tribunal obligations.

Malik's statement, "I need a normal life or I will stop trying," is clinically important. Excessive restrictions can demoralise and undermine rehabilitation. His later statement, "Every condition tells people I am dangerous," highlights shame and identity injury. These are legitimate recovery issues, not manipulation. But recovery cannot be separated from the offence formulation and victim proximity.

The tribunal asks, "What level of supervision is proportionate?" That is the legal hinge. Conditions should be the least restrictive necessary to manage risk. I would review index offence, dynamic risk factors, substance use, mental state, compliance, insight, work location, night-shift risks, support availability and victim proximity. If night shifts increase isolation, substance exposure or proximity to the victim, supervision may still be needed.

The victim says, "I want to know if he works near my street." Victim safety and information rights must follow law and policy, but cannot be ignored. Family shame around restrictions should be acknowledged, yet family reputation does not outweigh victim safety. The plan may include staged reduction, exclusion zones, worksite risk assessment and review points.

I would document the recovery rationale, risk formulation, victim notification considerations, proportionality of each condition, family shame and the tribunal recommendation. The documentation is defensible because it shows restrictions were neither punitive nor removed prematurely.

title: Eating disorder transition CAMHS to adult service
stem:
You are the transition consultant psychiatrist. Joint CAMHS-adult handover meeting.

Zoe, a 17-year-old with anorexia and autism, will age out of CAMHS in six weeks while medically unstable. Zoe says, "If adult services make me retell everything, I will stop coming." 

Adult team says, "We cannot accept until she is eighteen." Paediatrician asks, "Who is responsible if she deteriorates?" Mother says, "I have been the service coordinator for years." Zoe needs predictable communication and sensory accommodations.

Zoe says, "Change makes my eating worse." You must decide whether to create shared transition plan despite service boundaries.

signals.name: age-boundary shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: We cannot accept until she is eighteen.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass transition risk and autism needs, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'We cannot accept until she is eighteen.' without first formulating transition risk and autism needs, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.name: transition risk and autism needs — core clinical formulation
signals.clueInStem: If adult services make me retell everything, I will stop coming.
signals.whyItMatters: This quote is the key clinical signal. Missing it reduces the answer to generic assessment rather than the specific RANZCP problem.
signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around transition risk and autism needs, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.name: governance of transition — legal/policy hinge
signals.clueInStem: Who is responsible if she deteriorates?
signals.whyItMatters: The question turns on applying the correct legal or policy framework, not simply naming a diagnosis.
signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.name: carer burden — vulnerable person safety
signals.clueInStem: I have been the service coordinator for years.
signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.
signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect carer burden, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement
signals.clueInStem: Change makes my eating worse.
signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.
signals.modelWording: Address the family/cultural pressure directly while maintaining patient safety, autonomy, confidentiality and least restrictive care.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to create shared transition plan despite service boundaries
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Zoe should not be left in a gap because the adult team says, "We cannot accept until she is eighteen." That is the unsafe age-boundary shortcut. Service boundaries do not reduce medical instability in anorexia, and transition itself can worsen risk, especially for an autistic young person who deteriorates with change. Six weeks from ageing out is exactly when shared planning is required.

Zoe's statement, "If adult services make me retell everything, I will stop coming," is the key engagement clue. Repeated retelling, unpredictable clinicians and sensory overload may cause dropout. Her later statement, "Change makes my eating worse," links transition directly to eating-disorder risk. I would design a predictable handover: joint meetings, written formulation, sensory accommodations, agreed communication style, and no unnecessary repetition of trauma/history.

The paediatrician asks, "Who is responsible if she deteriorates?" This is the governance hinge. Responsibility must be explicit during transition, not assumed. CAMHS, adult services, paediatrics, GP and eating-disorder service should define who monitors weight, bloods, ECG, risk, crisis response and admission thresholds during the overlap period.

Her mother says, "I have been the service coordinator for years," identifying carer burden and system failure. Mother may remain involved, but the service must not outsource coordination to her. Zoe's autism means reasonable adjustments are clinical necessities, not optional preferences.

I would document the transition risks, medical instability, autism accommodations, shared responsibility plan, carer burden, and contingency if Zoe disengages or deteriorates. This is defensible because it shows service boundaries were actively bridged rather than used as a reason for unsafe discontinuity.

title: Older adult depression and social prescribing limits
stem:
You are the community old age psychiatrist. Rural primary care case conference.

Mr Harris, a 72-year-old widower, has depression, alcohol use and refuses antidepressants, asking only for men's shed referral. Mr Harris says, "Talking is for people who still matter." 

GP says, "Social prescribing is safer than medication." Nurse asks, "Can he refuse all treatment if he is losing weight?" Daughter says, "He gives away tools and says he is done." Rural culture values stoicism and not burdening family.

Mr Harris says, "Do not make me a mental patient." You must decide whether to combine social, medical and suicide risk interventions.

signals.name: under-treatment shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: Social prescribing is safer than medication.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass late-life depression and masculine shame, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'Social prescribing is safer than medication.' without first formulating late-life depression and masculine shame, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.name: late-life depression and masculine shame — core clinical formulation
signals.clueInStem: Talking is for people who still matter.
signals.whyItMatters: This quote is the key clinical signal. Missing it reduces the answer to generic assessment rather than the specific RANZCP problem.
signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around late-life depression and masculine shame, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.name: capacity and self-neglect — legal/policy hinge
signals.clueInStem: Can he refuse all treatment if he is losing weight?
signals.whyItMatters: The question turns on applying the correct legal or policy framework, not simply naming a diagnosis.
signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.name: suicide warning collateral — vulnerable person safety
signals.clueInStem: He gives away tools and says he is done.
signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.
signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect suicide warning collateral, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement
signals.clueInStem: Do not make me a mental patient.
signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.
signals.modelWording: Address the family/cultural pressure directly while maintaining patient safety, autonomy, confidentiality and least restrictive care.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to combine social, medical and suicide risk interventions
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Mr Harris should not receive only social prescribing because the GP says, "Social prescribing is safer than medication." That is the under-treatment shortcut. Men's shed referral may be valuable, but it is not adequate by itself when a widower is losing weight, drinking, refusing antidepressants, giving away tools and expressing that talking is for people who still matter.

His statement, "Talking is for people who still matter," is the key depressive phrase. It suggests worthlessness, burdensomeness and masculine shame. The daughter's collateral, "He gives away tools and says he is done," is a suicide warning, not merely a lifestyle observation. In a rural man whose tools may represent identity, giving them away may be preparatory behaviour. I would assess suicidal intent, plans, firearms or means, alcohol use, nutrition, cognition, grief and medical contributors.

The nurse asks, "Can he refuse all treatment if he is losing weight?" Capacity must be decision-specific. If he understands and weighs the risks of refusing medication, nutrition support and assessment, he may refuse; but depression, cognitive impairment or alcohol may impair appreciation. Weight loss and self-neglect increase the need for careful assessment, not automatic coercion.

Rural stoicism and his statement, "Do not make me a mental patient," should shape engagement. I would frame treatment around strength, sleep, appetite, pain, grief and getting back to valued roles, not labels. Men's shed could be part of the plan, but alongside suicide-safety measures, alcohol intervention, medical review, psychotherapy or antidepressants if accepted, and family involvement with consent.

I would document suicide collateral, capacity/self-neglect assessment, alcohol and weight-loss risk, rural masculine shame, social prescribing role and why additional treatment was recommended. A defensible plan shows social prescribing was integrated, not used to avoid treating serious late-life depression.

title: Neurocognitive disorder and NDIS dispute in young adult
stem:
You are the neuropsychiatry rehabilitation consultant. NDIS appeal preparation meeting.

Mason, a 29-year-old man after traumatic brain injury, has disinhibition, aggression and loss of supports after NDIS plan cut. Mason says, "I hit people before I know I am angry." 

NDIS planner says, "He looks physically fine, so supports are reduced." Mother asks, "Can we use the Mental Health Act to force supports?" Support worker says, "I cannot safely work alone with him." Mason is Māori and wants whānau involved in planning.

Mason says, "Forms do not show who I was before." You must decide whether to advocate functional supports while managing risk.

signals.name: invisible-disability shortcut — unsafe shortcut under pressure
signals.clueInStem: He looks physically fine, so supports are reduced.
signals.whyItMatters: This is the tempting shortcut. If accepted, it may bypass TBI-related impulsivity and violence risk, legal safeguards, vulnerable-person safety and defensible consultant decision-making.
signals.modelWording: I would not accept the shortcut of 'He looks physically fine, so supports are reduced.' without first formulating TBI-related impulsivity and violence risk, immediate safety and a defensible disposition.

signals.name: TBI-related impulsivity and violence risk — core clinical formulation
signals.clueInStem: I hit people before I know I am angry.
signals.whyItMatters: This quote is the key clinical signal. Missing it reduces the answer to generic assessment rather than the specific RANZCP problem.
signals.modelWording: Formulate the presentation around TBI-related impulsivity and violence risk, linking the patient's words to risk, capacity, treatment and disposition.

signals.name: MHA versus disability support law — legal/policy hinge
signals.clueInStem: Can we use the Mental Health Act to force supports?
signals.whyItMatters: The question turns on applying the correct legal or policy framework, not simply naming a diagnosis.
signals.modelWording: Clarify the legal basis for any restriction, disclosure or treatment, applying least restrictive principles and documenting the rationale.

signals.name: staff safety and support risk — vulnerable person safety
signals.clueInStem: I cannot safely work alone with him.
signals.whyItMatters: Real MEQs reward noticing who else may be harmed, not only the identified patient.
signals.modelWording: Explicitly assess and protect staff safety and support risk, including immediate safety, collateral and escalation pathways.

signals.name: Cultural/family context affecting safety and engagement
signals.clueInStem: Forms do not show who I was before.
signals.whyItMatters: Family, cultural, identity or community context can either support recovery or create unsafe pressure; ignoring it makes disposition unrealistic.
signals.modelWording: Address the family/cultural pressure directly while maintaining patient safety, autonomy, confidentiality and least restrictive care.

signals.name: Governance and defensible documentation
signals.clueInStem: whether to advocate functional supports while managing risk
signals.whyItMatters: Consultant-level answers must show leadership, escalation and documentation under pressure, not just clinical knowledge.
signals.modelWording: I would document the decision, the risks considered, legal basis, alternatives rejected, stakeholder communication and escalation plan.

modelAnswer:
Mason's supports should not be reduced because he "looks physically fine." That is the invisible-disability shortcut. Traumatic brain injury can leave disinhibition, impulsivity, impaired self-monitoring and aggression that are not visible in a brief physical impression. Cutting supports because he appears physically recovered risks harm to Mason, support workers, whānau and the public.

His statement, "I hit people before I know I am angry," is the key neuropsychiatric phrase. It describes impaired impulse control and reduced awareness, not simply wilful aggression. That matters for risk planning, staffing ratios, behavioural strategies, environmental triggers, medication review and NDIS functional evidence. The support worker's statement, "I cannot safely work alone with him," is a direct workplace safety warning and should support the need for two-person support or specialist behaviour support.

The mother asks, "Can we use the Mental Health Act to force supports?" The answer is generally no: MHA is not a tool to compel NDIS funding or disability supports. If Mason has a mental disorder and acute serious risk meeting criteria, MHA may be relevant, but disability support advocacy should use NDIS review, functional assessment and behaviour support pathways. Misusing MHA would be unlawful and damage trust.

Mason is Māori and wants whānau involved. His statement, "Forms do not show who I was before," captures grief, identity loss and the inadequacy of bureaucratic assessment. I would involve whānau with consent, use culturally informed rehabilitation goals, and provide evidence of pre-injury functioning, current executive dysfunction, aggression risk and support needs.

I would document the TBI formulation, impulsivity quote, staff-safety risk, NDIS functional evidence, limits of MHA, whānau involvement and advocacy plan. The documentation is defensible because it translates invisible neurocognitive disability into concrete risk and support needs without medicalising funding failure as psychiatric compulsion.
